The SDSS observations, Keck spectroscopy, and HST imaging (White et al. 2005, AJ, 129, 2102; White et al. 2003, AJ, 126, 1), reveal a SMBH of 2 10^9 M _ . The host galaxy has been detected in thermal dust, non-thermal radio continuum, CO line, and [ CII ] 158 m emission (Figure 1). High resolution imaging of the CO emission reveals a massive reservoir of molecular gas, 2 10^ 10 M _ , distributed on a scale of 6 kpc in the host galaxy (Walter et al. 2004).

The broad band SED of J1148+5251, shows a clear FIR excess, consistent with 50K dust emission and with the radio-FIR correlation for star forming galaxies (Wang et al. 2008, ApJ, 687, 848). The high CO excitation in J1148+5251 (Bertoldi et al. A A, 406, L55) is comparable to that seen in starburst nuclei implying a predominantly dense ( 10^5 cm ^ 3 ), warm ISM. Recent high resolution imaging of the [ CII ] emission in the host galaxy of J1148+5251 reveals an extreme starburst region with a diameter of 1.5 kpc (Walter et al. 2009, Nature, 475, 699), forming stars at the 'Eddington limited' rate of 1000 M _ year ^ 1 kpc ^ 2 (Thompson et al. ApJ, 630, 167).

sources.summary: The review describes J1148+5251 as a z=6.42 quasar whose host has dust, radio, CO, and [CII] detections, dense gas, and an extreme compact starburst.

quotes.quote: E.g. in the case of the z=6.42 quasar J1148+5251, FIR emission was first detected by Bertoldi et al. \ (2003a). The SDSS observations, Keck spectroscopy, and HST imaging (White et al. \ 2003, 2005), reveal a SMBH of 2 10 ^9 M _ and a very compact structure. The host galaxy has now been detected in thermal dust, non-thermal radio continuum, CO line, and [ CII ] 158 m emission (Figure 19). High resolution imaging of the CO emission reveals a massive reservoir of molecular gas, 2 10 ^ 10 M _ , distributed on a scale of 6 kpc in the host galaxy (Walter et al. 2003, 2004).

The broad band SED of J1148+5251, shows a clear FIR excess, consistent with 50K dust emission and with the radio-FIR correlation for star forming galaxies (Wang et al. \ 2008). The high CO excitation in J1148+5251 (Bertoldi et al. \ 2003b, Riechers et al., subm.) is comparable to that seen in starburst nuclei implying dense (10 ^5 cm ^ 3 ), warm gas. The high--resolution imaging of the [ CII ] emission in the host galaxy of J1148+5251 reveals an extreme starburst region with a diameter of 1.5kpc (Maiolino et al. \ 2005, Walter et al. \ 2009), forming stars at the `Eddington limited' rate of 1000 M _ year ^ 1 kpc ^ 2 (Thompson et al. \ 2005).

sources.summary: The paper presents Herschel/PACS detections of SDSS J1148+5251, finding FIR emission, a possible nearby confusing source, and a high infrared luminosity in its dusty high-redshift host.

quotes.quote: The quasar SDSS J1148+5251 at z=6.42 hereafter J1148+5251 fan03 was observed in PACS scan-map mode using two cross scans in the green (100 m, FWHM 6 7) and in the red (160 m, FWHM 11 ) band pog10 , yielding an effective on-source time of 1150 sec in 3810 sec of total AOR length. J1148+5251 The QSO J1148+5251 at z=6.42 is one of the highest redshift quasars known to date. It has been detected previously at several sub-mm bee06,rob04 and mm ber03,rie09 wavelengths.

A careful inspection of the data for J1148+5251 shows a companion 10 to the northwest of the QSO in the FIR (this is best seen in the 160 m filter). We note though that depending on the shape of its SED this additional source will affect measurements of J1148+5251 done with large ( 10 ) beams. Aperture photometry of J1148+5251 yields a flux density at 100 and 160 m of 4.1 1.0 mJy and 6.3 2.0 mJy, respectively (see Table table1 for a summary of all flux measurements, including literature data).

Using the new Herschel photometry to determine total infrared luminosities for these two objects (integrating the SEDs between 1 and 200 m, rest frame) yields L_ FIR = 8.1 10^ 13 L_ for J1148+5251 and a total L_ FIR = 3.7 10^ 14 L_ for 1202 0725.

sources.summary: The paper images J1148+5251 with HST, finds no extended component, rejects the small ON-band excess as an artifact, and sets host line-emission and continuum limits.

quotes.quote: The present study aims to detect extended emission around two z>6 quasars (for which suitable narrow-band filters exist), SDSS J103027.10+052455.0 z=6.309 ; hereafter, J1030+0524 fan01 and SDSS J114816.64+525150.3 z=6.419 ; hereafter, J1148+5251 fan03 .

Results for J1148+5251 The light profile of J1148+5251 also does not show any extended component. In this case a small excess is seen at 1'' in the ON observation. This is most likely due to an observational artifact (see below). Following the same approach as adopted for J1030+0524, we can exclude a host galaxy brighter than 21 mag in the ON image (the light profile being perfectly consistent with a point-source), and 21 mag for the continuum.
These limits yield a flux from the host of < 5.4 10^ 16 erg s ^ 1 cm ^ 2 , i.e., L ( ,host) < 6.6 10^ 10 or <2.5 10^ 44 erg s ^ 1 . The limit on the host continuum is M_ 1450 >-25.8 mag.

sources.summary: The paper models SDSS J1148+5251 as a high-redshift quasar host whose dusty far-infrared emission is powered by both the central source and intense star formation.

quotes.quote: Here we investigate the origin of the far-infrared continuum of SDSS J1148+5251, using it as a prototype for the more general class of high-luminosity high-redshift quasars. We run the radiative transfer code TRADING to follow the transfer of radiation from the central source and from stellar sources through the dusty environment of the host galaxy. We adopt simple models for the central source, including all the radiation that can travel beyond the dusty torus. The radiation from stellar sources is modeled using the code P E GASE. The model is based on the output of the semi-analytical merger tree code, GAMETE/QSOdust , which enables to predict the evolution of the host galaxy and of its nuclear black hole, following the star formation history and chemical evolution -- including dust -- in all the progenitor galaxies of SDSS J1148+5251.

We find that the radiation emitted by the central source, which dominates the observed spectral energy distribution from UV/optical to near and mid infrared wavelengths, can also provide an important source of heating for the dust distributed in the host galaxy, powering at least 30 and up to 70 of the observed far infrared emission at rest-frame wavelengths [ 20 - 1000 ] m. The remaining fraction is contributed by stellar sources and can only be achieved if the host galaxy is able to sustain a star formation rate of 900 M_ /yr at z=6.4 .

sources.summary: The review highlights J1148 as a z=6.42 quasar host with constrained UV star formation, modeled far-infrared emission, and [CII] evidence for a quasar-driven outflow.

quotes.quote: (2012) carried out deep HST/WFC3 near-infrared observations of quasar SDSS J1148+5251 ( z=6.42 , hereafter J1148), attempting to detect the UV stellar continuum from young stars in the quasar host galaxy. After careful PSF subtraction, it yields a non-detection, constraining a UV-based star formation rate to be less than 250 M_ yr ^ 1 .

(2015) carried out the first detailed radiative transfer model to fit the broad-band multi-wavelength SED of the well observed quasar J1148. They found that the central AGN heating could contribute 30-70 of the observed FIR emission, but starburst contribution is still consistent with a star formation rate of 1000 M_ yr ^ 1 .

The high [ CII ] line luminosity ( >10^ 10 L_ ) in J1148 ( z=6.4 ) has allowed Maiolino et al. (2012) and Cicone et al. (2015) to obtain the first evidence of quasar-driven outflow in this system. The inferred outflow rate ( 1400 300 M_ yr^ 1 ) is of the same order of the star formation rate, thus implying a short gas depletion time ( <10 Myr ). This result suggests that the star formation in the host galaxy of J1148 would be quenched on very short timescales.

sources.summary: The paper reports a 78 ks X-ray observation of z=6.4 quasar SDSS J1148+5251, measuring its absorbed power-law spectrum, luminosity, optical-X-ray slope, black hole mass, and molecular gas.

quotes.quote: We present the 78-ks observations of the z=6.4 quasar SDSS J1148+5251. The source is clearly detected in the energy range 0.3-7 keV with 42 counts (with a significance 9 ). The X-ray spectrum is best-fitted by a power-law with photon index =1.9 absorbed by a gas column density of N_ H =2.0^ +2.0 _ 1.5 10^ 23 cm^ 2 . We measure an intrinsic luminosity at 2-10 keV and 10-40 keV equal to 1.5 10^ 45 erg s^ 1 , comparable with luminous local and intermediate-redshift quasar properties.

Moreover, the X-ray to optical power-law slope value ( _ OX =-1.76 0.14 ) of J1148 is consistent with the one found in quasars with similar rest-frame 2500 luminosity ( L_ 2500 10^ 32 erg s^ 1 ^ 1 ). Then we use data to test a physically motivated model that computes the intrinsic X-ray flux emitted by a quasar starting from the properties of the powering black hole and assuming that X-ray emission is attenuated by intervening, metal-rich ( Z Z_ ) molecular clouds distributed on kpc scales in the host galaxy. Our analysis favors a black hole mass M_ BH 3 10^9 M_ and a molecular hydrogen mass M_ H_2 2 10^ 10 M_ , in good agreement with estimates obtained from previous studies.

sources.summary: The paper describes J1148+5251 as a z=6.419 QSO host detected in dust, radio continuum, CO, and CII, with extended molecular gas and an enriched interstellar medium within 870 Myr of the big bang.

quotes.quote: The best studied of the z 6 QSOs is the most distant QSO known, J1148+5251, at z = 6.419 . This galaxy been detected in thermal dust, non-thermal radio continuum, and CO line emission (Walter et al. 2003; Bertoldi al. 2003; Carilli et al. 2004), with an implied dust mass of 7 10^8 M _ , and a molecular gas mass of 2 10^ 10 M _ . The molecular gas is extended over 1" , or 5.5 kpc (Figure 2).

Most recently, IRAM 30m observations of J1148+5251 have yielded the first detection of the fine structure line of [ CII ] at cosmologically significant redshifts (Maiolino et al. 2005). This line is thought to dominate ISM cooling in photon-dominated regions, ie. the interface regions between giant molecular clouds and HII regions. These observations of J1148+5251 demonstrate that large reservoirs of dust and metal enriched atomic and molecular gas can exist in the most distant galaxies, within 870 Myr of the big bang. The molecular gas and [ CII ] emission suggest a substantially enriched ISM on kpc-scales.

sources.summary: The paper models SDSS J1148+5251 as a z=6.4 hyperluminous quasar whose dust mass requires high supernova dust yields, uncertain star formation history, and an early massive black hole.

quotes.quote: In this paper we present analytical models for the evolution of the gas, dust, and metals in high redshift galaxies, with a special application to SDSS J1148+5251 (hereafter ), a hyperluminous quasar at z = 6.4 . We find that an average supernova must condense at least 1 \ of dust to account for the observed dust mass in . Theoretically, this large yield can only be attainable if stars are formed with a top heavy initial mass function. Observationally, it is in excess of the largest dust yield of 0.02 \ found thus far in the ejecta of any SN.

The galaxy's star formation history is still uncertain, and current observations of the gas, metal, and dust contents of \ can be reproduced by either an intensive and short burst of star formation ( 10^3 ) with a duration of 10^8 yr, or a much lower star formation rate ( 100 ) occurring over the lifetime of the galaxy. Analysis of the spectral energy distribution of \ suggests an AGN luminosity of 7 10^ 13 , requiring the early formation of a supermassive black hole of 10^9 .

sources.summary: The paper argues that QSO J1148+5251 shows persuasive far-infrared ice emission, possibly tied to early enrichment, and that its dust composition affects inferred enrichment and submillimeter behavior.

quotes.quote: Ice emission in a high redshift quasar The Gunn-Peterson trough in QSO J1148+5251 ( z=6.42 ; berto2 ) suggests that this source may be participating in the last phases of reionization ( fan03 ). That the quasar exists indicates that the source has been contributing to reionization for some time and we may take the beginning of reionization as an estimate of it's age ( 500 Myr) which gives a high average accretion rate for a blackhole mass of 3 10^9 M _ ( willott ). Thus, it seems plausible that pair instability SN enriched material with a high ice content is being supplied toward the center of this activity as the blackhole and presumably its stellar system grow in mass.

Fig. 4 shows an indication that the rest frame FIR emission from QSO J1148+5251 is better explained by ice than refractory dust emission. Three models are compared to photometric observations: 1) a refractory model (dashed line T=55 K; _ 60 =0.3 ; =2 ) is similar to that given by , 2) a simple ice model (dot-dashed line; T=43 K; _ 60 =0.4 , V _m /V _c =5.6 amorphous), and 3) a more complex ice model described below (solid line). In Fig. 4, it is not possible to formally access the goodness of fit through a reduced _ ^2 calculation even for the refractory dust model (dashed line) since the number of free parameters (normalization, temperature, optical depth, and opacity index) is the same as the number of points to be fit.

sources.summary: The paper evaluates dust-origin scenarios for high-redshift quasar J1148+5251, comparing supernova and AGB-star production under different star formation histories and observational tests.

quotes.quote: Two distinct scenarios for the origin of the 4 10^8 \ of dust observed in the high-redshift ( z = 6.4 ) quasar J1148+5251 have been proposed. The first assumes that this galaxy is much younger than the age of the universe at that epoch so that only supernovae (SNe) could have produced this dust. The second scenario assumes a significantly older galactic age, so that the dust could have formed in lower-mass asymptotic giant branch (AGB) stars.

We show that AGB stars can produce the inferred mass of dust in this object, however, the final mass of surviving dust depends on the galaxy's star formation history (SFH). In general supernovae cannot produce the observed amount of dust unless the average SN event creates over 2 \ of dust in its ejecta. The two scenarios propose different origins for the galaxy's spectral energy distribution, different star formation efficiencies and stellar masses, and consequently different comoving number densities of type hyperluminous infrared (IR) objects.

sources.summary: The paper uses SDSS J1148+5251 as a z>5 quasar with FIR/mm excess, an SED-required FIR component, and compact host-galaxy star formation consistent with CII imaging.

quotes.quote: From our new photometry providing full SED information in the rest frame wavelength range 0.5-80 m, we have discovered seven quasars at z>5 with considerable FIR excess emission, including SDSS J1148+5251 for which strong FIR and mm emission has been reported previously (e.g. Bertoldi et al. 2003, Beelen et al. 2006). In order to extract additional information from the SEDs, we fitted the observed photometry with a linear combination of a power-law in the UV/optical/NIR (emission from the accretion disk), a clumpy torus model (AGN heated dust from H onig Kishimoto (2010)), and a modified black body (starburst-powered excess dust emission). The emmissivity value for this last component was fixed to a value of 1.6 to be comparable to previous studies at high z . In all cases the additional FIR component was required to describe the observed SEDs at the longest wavelengths (see Fig. 3 for a few examples).

For SDSS J1148+5251 the star-formation rate we determine from the SED fits is consistent with the results from a [ CII ] imaging study (Walter et al. 2009). These spatially resolved observations also show that the bulk of the detected star formation takes place in the innermost 1.5 kpc of the host galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper models SDSS J1148+5251 as a z=6.4 quasar whose host galaxy, black hole, dust, star formation, and massive gas outflow are tightly coupled by quasar feedback.

quotes.quote: The recent detection of a massive gas outflow in one of the most distant quasar, SDSS J1148+5251 at z=6.4 , presented by Maiolino et al. (2012) strongly supports this idea and suggests that strong quasar feedback is already at work at very early times. In a previous work, Valiante et al. (2011), we have presented a hierarchical semi-analytical model, GAMETE/QSOdust , for the formation and evolution of high-redshift quasars, and we have applied it to the quasar SDSS J1148+5251, with the aim of investigating the star formation history, the nature of the dominant stellar populations and the origin and properties of the large dust mass observed in the host galaxy. A robust prediction of the model is that the evolution of the nuclear black hole and of the host galaxy are tightly coupled by quasar feedback in the form of strong galaxy-scale winds.

This model has enabled us to constrain the star formation history (SFH) and the nature of the stellar populations (e.g. the stellar initial mass function, IMF) of the well studied high redshift quasar SDSS J1148+5251 (hereafter J1148) at z=6.4, by linking the evolution of the nuclear black hole (BH) with the chemical properties of the host galaxy. The main observed and inferred physical properties of J1148 are summarized in Table tab:j1148 . In V11 we have found that the evolutionary scenarios that allow us to reproduce the physical properties of the quasar J1148 and of its host galaxy, such as the mass of the nuclear BH, the dynamical, gas and dust masses, are tightly constrained.

sources.summary: The paper uses J1148 as the benchmark high-redshift quasar host, modeling its black hole, gas, dust, star formation, and stellar mass tension with dynamical limits.

quotes.quote: As a pilot study we have used this model to investigate possible evolutionary scenarios of one of the best studied quasars, SDSS J1148+5251 (hereafter J1148), observed at redshift z=6.4 . In Valiante et al. (2011) we pointed out that the the observed properties of J1148, such as the BH mass, the mass of gas and dust in the ISM, are reproduced only if distinct evolutionary paths are followed. In particular, if stars form according to a Larson Initial Mass Function (IMF), with a characteristic mass m_ ch =0.35 M_ (see Eq  eq:imf ), that we call standard IMF , at the end of its evolution, J1148 settles on the local M_ BH M_ star relation and the stellar mass at z = 6.4 exceeds the upper limit set by the observed dynamical mass by a factor of 3-10 (model B3).

Although a top-heavy IMF (model B1) enables to reproduce the observed dust mass and the deviation of J1148 from the local Mbh-Mstar relation, the predicted star formation rate at z = 6.4 is < 100 M _ / yr, more than one order of magnitude smaller than the observed value ( 3000 M _ / yr). This rate is too small to power the observed Far Infra-Red (FIR) luminosity (Schneider et al. 2014b). Hence, our studies suggest that J1148 has built up a large stellar mass of 4 10^ 11 M _ over its evolution and that its final M_ BH /M_ star is 0.007 .

sources.summary: The paper uses SDSS J1148+5251 as a high-redshift quasar target, modeling its black-hole and host-galaxy coevolution to reproduce observed black-hole, gas, metal, dust, and outflow properties.

quotes.quote: As in V11 we select as our target the quasar SDSS J1148+5251, observed at redshift z=6.4 . This is one of the best studied object at high redshift, hosting a BH mass of (2-6) 10^9 ( Barth03, Willott03 ). The other main observed properties of this quasar are summarized in V11 and V14 .

A value of _ BH = 50 is fixed to match the observed SMBH mass of J1148. A fraction of the energy released by supernova explosions and BH accretion is converted into kinetic energy of the gas in the host galaxy, thus driving gas outflows in the form of winds. In our previous study V12 we show that the BH-host galaxy co-evolution is regulated by quasar feedback, with SN-driven winds providing a negligible contribution to the mass outflow rate, in good agreement with observations of outflowing gas in J1148 M12,Cicone15 .

sources.summary: The paper uses SDSS J1148+5251 to argue that its z=6.08 transmission gap can be consistent with incomplete overlap when large HII regions reduce damping-wing absorption.

quotes.quote: SDSS J1148+5251 has a transmission spike at 8609 ( z=6.08 for a Ly line) with an apparent 2.5 , as well as a matching Ly peak white03 . White et al. point out that such a gap is not hard to imagine if one includes only the damping wing; however, they neglected resonant absorption. Because it treats each galaxy in isolation, the model of barkana02-bub essentially requires that this gap appears only well after overlap. As we have emphasised, our large HII regions reduce the severity of the damping wing and allow transmission spikes near the end of overlap.

This is entirely consistent with the observed gap; thus we argue that its existence does not require that overlap occurred before z=6.1 . Of course, it is still perfectly consistent with such a scenario.

sources.summary: The paper describes SDSS J114816.64+5251 as the most distant known quasar, with strong far-infrared emission, dense molecular gas, and a faint 350 micron detection.

quotes.quote: SDSS J114816.64+5251 , hereafter refered to as J1148+5251, is the most distant quasar ( z=6.42 ) known to date Fan2003 . An optically very luminous quasar, powered by a supermassive black hole, it also shows strong far-IR emission with an estimated luminosity of 1.2 10^ 13 \ L_ Bertoldi2003a , and an implied rate of star formation of 3000 \ M_ \ yr^ 1 . A massive reservoir of dense molecular gas ( 2 10^ 10 \ M_ ) is implied by the observed CO emission Walter2003, Bertoldi2003b and the recent detection of the [ CII ] emission line Maiolino2005 .

J1148+5251 as been detected both at 850 and 450 by Robson2004 , with flux densities of 7.8 0.7 \ mJy and 24.7 7.4 \ mJy respectively. Charmandaris2004 reported the detection of J1148+5251 with the Spitzer Space Telescope at 16 and 22 , revealing a hot dust component that could be heated by the AGN; however, it should be noted that these measurement are also compatible with the emission of the AGN extrapolated from the optical. We detected J1148+5251 at 350 with a flux density of 21 6 \ mJy at the optical position of the QSO.

sources.summary: The paper uses SDSS J114816.64+525150.3 as evidence that a 3e9 solar-mass black hole existed at z=6.42, challenging Eddington-limited growth and motivating dark-matter-assisted formation.

quotes.quote: Supermassive black holes of 3 10^ 9 M_ are already present at redshift z = 6.42 or 850 Myr after the big bang, as is evident from the recently discovered quasar SDSS J114816.64+525150.3. Unlike the galaxies, which are thought to assemble through hierarchical mergers of smaller galaxies , the supermassive black holes form anti-hierarchically, i.e. the larger black holes prior to the smaller ones. This is supported by the observation that the number of brighter quasars peaks at z 2 , while that of lower-luminosity active galactic nuclei peaks at z 0.6 . There is only a narrow window of about 485 Myr between the appearance of the first stars at z 11 , or 365 Myr after the big bang when reionization of the universe begins , and the appearance of the first quasars at z = 6.42 when reionization ends, during which at least some of the most massive black holes must have formed.

In this context, it is important to note that the mass of the black hole at the center of M87 is equal to that of the earliest quasar observed at z = 6.42. As these are both archetypical examples of the most massive black holes at vastly different epochs of the universe, one may infer that M_ max has not increased significantly from z = 6.42 to z = 0, or from 0.85 Gyr to 13.7 Gyr after the big bang.

sources.summary: The paper models SDSS J1148+5251 as a z=6.42 quasar host whose observed SED and dust properties are reproduced, with dust emission evolving from starburst to AGN-heated warm ULIRG.

quotes.quote: In particular, deep infrared and radio surveys (e.g., Robson2004, Bertoldi2003A, Carilli2004, Charmandaris2004, Beelen2006, Hines2006 ) have revealed a large amount of cold dust in SDSS J1148+5251 (hereafter J1148+5251), the most distant Sloan quasar detected at redshift z = 6.42 Fan2003 . The dust mass is estimated to be 1-7 10^8 .

By applying ART ^2 to the hydrodynamic simulations of , we reproduce the observed spectral energy distribution (SED) and inferred dust properties of SDSS J1148+5251, the most distant quasar detected in the Sloan survey. We find that the dust and infrared emission are closely associated with the formation and evolution of the quasar host. As the system evolves from a starburst to a quasar, the SED changes from being dominated by a cold dust bump (peaking at 50 m) to one that includes a prominent hot dust component (peaking at 3 m), and the galaxy evolves from a cold to a warm ultraluminous infrared galaxy (ULIRG) owing to heating and feedback from star formation and the active galactic nucleus (AGN).

sources.summary: The review uses J1148+5152 as a z=6.4 QSO with resolved CO emission, two central peaks, a low dynamical mass, and evidence that black holes may grow before hosts.

quotes.quote: An interesting particular result is the CO(3--2) map of one of the most distant QSOs, J1148+5152, at z=6.4 (Fig. fig_j1148_map ) walter04 . The map shows resolved emission extending over about 5 kpc and two central peaks separated by 1.7 kpc. From the velocity structure infer a dynamical mass of about 5 10^ 10 M_ . This mass is much lower than the mass of a few times 10^ 12 M_ that would be expected from the black hole mass derived for the same object (a few times 10^9 M_ ) if applying the local BH-bulge mass relation. This result would provide further support to the scenario where at high-z black holes grow faster then their host galaxies, while the latter reach the local BH-bulge relation only at lower redshifts.

sources.summary: The paper discusses SDSS J1148+5251 as a z=6.4 dusty infrared galaxy whose luminosity, dust mass, star-formation rate, and young age challenge dust-production models.

quotes.quote: For example, the galaxy SDSS J1148+5251 (hereafter ) located at z = 6.4 was observed at far-IR and submillimeter wavelength bertoldi03a,robson04,beelen06 . The average IR luminosity of the source is L_ IR 2 10^ 13 , and the average dust mass is M_d 2 10^8 . Using the relation, one can derive a star formation rate (SFR) of 3000 yr ^ 1 from the observed far-IR luminosity. For comparison, the Milky Way galaxy is about 10 Gyr old, has an average SFR of 3 yr ^ 1 , and contains about 5 10^7 \ of dust, a significant fraction of which was produced in AGB stars. At z=6.4 the universe was only 890 Myr old, using standard CDM parameters ( _m = 0.27 , _ = 0.73 , and H_0 = 70 km s ^ 1 Mpc ^ 1 ).

sources.summary: The paper presents J1148+5251 as the archetypal z>6 quasar: formerly brightest, extensively studied, detected in line, CO, and neutral carbon, and used as P036+03's benchmark.

quotes.quote: For more than a decade, the quasar SDSS J1148+5251 at z=6.42 (hereafter J1148+5251), discovered by , has been the brightest quasar known at z>6 ( M_ 1450 =-27.8 ). As such, it is by far the best studied quasar at these redshifts, being fundamental in shaping our current understanding of the universe when it was less than 1 Gyr old. It was the first source detected in \ at z>0.1 maiolino2005 , and for several years it remained the only z>6 quasar detected also in CO emission walter2003, walter2004,bertoldi2003b,riechers2009 . The \ line in this quasar has been studied in detail e.g., walter2009, maiolino2012 . Neutral carbon \ has also been detected riechers2009 , while deep limits on the \ line could also be placed walter2009b .

sources.summary: The model targets SDSS J1148+5251 at z=6.4, reproducing its quasar host properties and using its massive SMBH and feedback constraints to study first-seed growth histories.

quotes.quote: In this analysis we select the quasar SDSS J1148+5251, observed at z=6.4 , as our target. This is one of the best studied objects at z>6 and it is powered by a SMBH of (2-6) 10^9 ( Barth03, Willott03 ). We summarized the other main observed properties of this quasar and its host galaxy in V11 and V14 .
Still, the Eddington-limited growth of a SMBH of >10^9 M_ , as inferred for SDSS J1148+5251, relies on the formation of these few massive seeds.

Although they have similar wind efficiencies, in V12 we show that the SN-driven winds have a minor effect on the BH-host galaxy co-evolution history which is instead regulated by the AGN feedback, as observed in SDSS J1148+5251 Maiolino12,Cicone15 .

sources.summary: The paper searches for SDSS J1148+5251's host with NIRCam, finds no band detections, estimates mass from F360M limits, and notes high far-infrared star formation and obscuration.

quotes.quote: The final two quasars, SDSS J1148+5251 and ULAS J112001.48+064124.3 (J1120+0641) at z 6.4 and 7.1 respectively, are among the highest-redshift quasars known, and we obtained only NIRCam images to search for hosts via PSF subtraction. J1148+5251 and J1120+0641 (bottom two rows, the two highest-redshift quasars in our sample) are undetected in all bands.

For the galaxies with an F360M flux (J2239+0207, J1148+5251, and J1120+0641), we utilize that flux to calculate the mass. Effect of Intense Nuclear Star Formation Three members of our sample, J1148+5251, J1340+2813, and J2239+0207, have very large far-infrared luminosities indicative of star formation rates >> 1000 M _ yr ^ 1 Leipski2014, Izumi2019, Bertoldi2003 . J1340+2813, J2239+0207, and J1148+5251, however, have very high far infrared luminosities Leipski2014, Izumi2019, Bertoldi2003 and should have significant obscuration in their central regions.

sources.summary: The paper describes SDSS J1148+5251 as an extremely luminous z=6.43 quasar with strong Ly absorption, likely powered by a several-billion-solar-mass black hole if unlensed.

quotes.quote: At z^*=20.0 , J=18.3 and M_ 1450 = -27.6 ( model, see Table 3), SDSS J1148+5251 is extremely luminous. Note that at this redshift, more than half of the flux in the z band is absorbed by the Ly forest. The continuum magnitude measured from the spectrum redward of Ly emission ( AB_ 1280 = 19.10 ) is much brighter than the broad-band z band magnitude ( z^* = 20.0 ) which is affected by this absorption. If not gravitationally lensed (see 5), it is likely to be powered by a black hole of several billion solar masses, within a dark matter halo of order 10^ 13 M_ (see the discussion in Paper I). It is probably among the most luminous and massive objects in the universe at z>6 .

sources.summary: The paper images the z=6.42 quasar field, reports no detected foreground galaxies within five arcseconds, and connects its CO dynamical mass to lower-than-naive host halo expectations.

quotes.quote: One of these quasars, SDSS J1148+5251, has also been detected in molecular carbon monoxide transitions (Bertoldi et al. 2003b; Walter et al. 2003). The CO spectra have a relatively narrow velocity profile with width 280 km s ^ 1 . Walter et al. (2004) obtained high-resolution imaging of the CO emission and confirm it comes from a compact structure (a few kpc).
Nothing was detected within 5 of SDSS J1148+5251. Walter et al. (2004) use resolved CO emission to measure a dynamical mass for the inner 2.5 kpc of SDSS J1148+5251 to be 5 10^ 10 M _ .

sources.summary: The paper reports high-redshift [C II] emission from J1148+5251, indicating a metal-enriched, hyperluminous infrared quasar host undergoing an intense starburst.

quotes.quote: We report the detection of the ^2P_ 3/2 ^2P_ 1/2 fine-structure line of C^+ at 157.74 m in SDSSJ114816.64+525150.3 (hereafter J1148+5251), the most distant known quasar, at z=6.42 , using the IRAM 30-meter telescope. This is the first detection of the [ C II ] line at high redshift, and also the first detection in a Hyperluminous Infrared Galaxy ( L_ FIR > 10^ 13 L_ ). The [ C II ] line is detected at a significance level of 8 and has a luminosity of 4.4 10^9 L_ . The L_ [ CII ] /L_ FIR ratio is 2 10^ 4 , about an order of magnitude smaller than observed in local normal galaxies and similar to the ratio observed in local Ultraluminous Infrared Galaxies. The [ C II ] line luminosity indicates that the host galaxy of this quasar is undergoing an intense burst of star formation with an estimated rate of 3000 M_ yr^ 1 .

sources.summary: The paper treats SDSS J1148 as a z=6.4 QSO whose CO structure suggests a disk or merger, with dynamical mass far below the local black-hole bulge expectation.

quotes.quote: Walter et al. (2004) find an elongated structure in SDSS J1148 ( z = 6.4), which they interpret in terms of a disk with a radius = 2.5 kpc seen at an inclination i 65 degrees. Half the CO emission comes from two hot spots separated by 1.7 kpc, each containing 10^ 9.7 of molecular gas. The disk model gives an enclosed mass of M_ dyn 10^ 10.7 , which is largely accounted for by the black hole and the molecular gas. (For a merger model, SV05 quote M_ dyn 10^ 10.2 .) The balance of the measured dynamical mass falls far short of the bulge mass 700 10^ 12.2 prescribed by the local black hole -- bulge relationship (Kormendy Gebhardt 2001). This discrepancy remains qualitatively true after allowing for a bulge radius larger than the CO radius (Walter et al. 2004). This is consistent with our conclusions using \ as a measure of \ .

sources.summary: The paper models dust evolution for SDSS J1148+5251, a z=6.4 hyperluminous quasar with far-IR/submillimeter observations, very large infrared luminosity, dust mass, and inferred star formation rate.

quotes.quote: For example, the galaxy SDSS J1148+5251 (hereafter ) located at z = 6.4 was observed at far-IR and submillimeter wavelength . Figure dustspec depicts the observed fluxes and spectral fits of various dust emission models through the data. The average IR luminosity of the source is L_ IR 2 10^ 13 , and the average dust mass is M_d 2 10^8 . Using the Kennicutt relation , one can derive a star formation rate (SFR) of 3000 yr ^ 1 from the observed far-IR luminosity.

sources.summary: The paper observes the z=6.4 quasar host J1148+5251 in [C II] and CO, finding consistent line profiles and deriving confidence limits on redshift offsets and F/F.

quotes.quote: We use sensitive observations of three high redshift sources; [ C II ] ^2P_ 3/2 ^2P_ 1/2 fine structure and CO (J = 2 1 ) rotational transitions for the z=6.4 Quasar host galaxy (QSO) J1148+5251 taken with the Plateau de Bure Interferometer (PdBI) and Jansky Very Large Array (JVLA) respectively, and [ C II ] and CO (J = 5 4 ) transitions from the QSO BR1202 0725 and its sub-millimeter companion (SMG) galaxy at z=4.7 taken with the Atacama Large Millimetre Array (ALMA) and the PdBI. We use these observations to place constraints on the quantity z = z_ CO z_ C_ II for each source where z_ CO and z_ C II are the observed redshifts of the CO rotational transition and [ C II ] fine structure transition respectively, using a combination of approaches; 1) By modelling the emission line profiles using `shapelets' - a complete ortho-normal set of basis functions that allow us to recreate most physical line shapes - to compare both the emission redshifts and the line profiles themselves, in order to make inferences about the intrinsic velocity differences between the molecular and atomic gas, and 2) By performing a marginalisation over all model parameters in order to calculate a non-parametric estimate of z . We derive 99 confidence intervals for the marginalised posterior of z of (-1.9 1.3) 10^ 3 , (-3 8) 10^ 4 and (-2 4) 10^ 3 for J1148+5251, and the BR1202 0725 QSO and SMG respectively. We show the [ C II ] and CO (J = 2 1 ) line profiles for J1148+5251 are consistent with each other within the limits of the data, whilst the [ C II ] and CO (J = 5 4 ) line profiles from the BR1202 0725 QSO and SMG respectively have 65 and >99.9 probabilities of being inconsistent, with the CO (J = 5 4 ) lines 30 wider than the [ C II ] lines.

sources.summary: The paper cites SDSSJ1148+5251 as a z>6 QSO with [CII] gas outflows reaching 30 kpc and >1000 km/s, using it to compare modeled bubble radii and velocities.

quotes.quote: A remarkable example is given by the famous QSO SDSSJ1148+5251 fan03 , one of the first z>6 QSOs discovered by the SDSS survey, where observations of the [ CII ] 158 m line with the PdBI interferometer have revealed the presence of large-scale gas outflows extending out to 30 kpc from the QSO and moving with velocities of >1000 km s ^ 1 cicone15 . This large-scale outflow extends well beyond the characteristic size of the host galaxies of z 6 QSOs ( 1-3 kpc wang13,venemans17 ), and then expands on scales comparable with that of the hosting dark matter halo, possibly affecting the QSO local environment.

At t_9 the bubble radius is 60 kpc. That is, the outflow has reached scales comparable with those observed in the z=6.4 QSO SDSS J1148 cicone15 . As expected, the bubble size is smaller (by a factor of 4-5 ) than what was found in the case of a uniform density field since now the outflow is crossing a gas density that is on average a few dex higher. As for the velocity of the bubble expansion, this reaches v=450 km s ^ 1 at t=t_9 , whereas velocities up to 1000 km s ^ 1 have been observed for SDSS J1148.

sources.summary: The paper uses SDSS J1148+5251 as the observed z>6 quasar endpoint, matching its SMBH mass while modeling progenitor merger histories and AGN-regulated host evolution.

quotes.quote: Following V16, we focus our analysis on the progenitor BHs of the (2-6) 10^9 M_ SMBH which powers SDSS J1148+5251, one of the best studied luminous quasars at z>6 . A summary of the properties of SDSS J1148+5251 inferred from the observations can be found in V11,V14 .

The BH accretion rate is described by the Bondi-Hoyle-Lyttleton formula re-scaled by a factor _ BH =50 (to match the BH mass of SDSS J1148+5251) _ BH is a free parameter required in semi-analytic models and numerical simulations to account for the higher central densities around accreting BHs e.g. DiMatteo05 .

As observed in SDSS J1148+5251 Maiolino12, Cicone15 , in our model the evolution history of BHs and their host galaxies is mainly regulated by AGN feedback, with SN-driven winds having only a minor effect.

sources.summary: The paper uses SDSS J1148+5251 as a z=6.4 luminous-quasar prototype, reconstructing merger histories for its host halo and following black-hole and baryonic evolution through cosmic time.

quotes.quote: Following , in this work we focus on the evolution of a SDSS J1148+5251 (hereafter J1148)-like quasar at z=6.4 , one the best studied high- z luminous quasars Absolute AB magnitude of the continuum in the rest-frame at 1450 of M_ 1450 = −27.82 ( Fan2003 ). (e.g., Willott2003,Walter2004,Maiolino2005,Cicone2015 ), and we use it as a prototype for the general class of luminous z 6 quasars. The hierarchical merger history We first reconstruct different hierarchical merger histories (merger trees) of a M_ DM = 10^ 13 M_ DM halo hosting a J1148-like quasar at z=6.4 , using a binary Monte Carlo algorithm accounting for mass infall (e.g., Volonteri2003 ), based on the Extended Press-Schechter theory (e.g., Lacey1993 ).

Merger trees are used as an input to reconstruct the formation and evolution of the J1148-like quasar and its host galaxy through cosmic time. The baryonic evolution inside each progenitor galaxy is regulated by processes of star formation, BH growth and feedback, and followed consistently through the cosmic mass assembly.

sources.summary: The paper reports a host-galaxy non-detection for J1148+5251 but finds F356W diffuse emission that may trace nebular or line-dominated extended material.

quotes.quote: J1148+5251 (z=6.42) was initially reported by fan03 . The image fitting procedure returns a non-detection of the host galaxy. Nevertheless, the PSF-subtracted image in the F356W band exhibit diffused emission extending from the northeast to the southwest of the quasar. We notice that this emission is absent in the F200W and the F115W images, which suggests that the emission might be dominated by or nebular lines.

sources.summary: The paper presents J1148+5251 as a well-studied high-redshift QSO whose full SED modelling indicates substantial AGN heating of host-galaxy dust.

quotes.quote: SDSS J114816.64+525150.3 (hereafter J1148+5251) at z=6.42 was discovered by fan2003 who reported an absolute magnitude of M_ 1450 =-27.82 . It is one of the most studied QSOs at high- z . It was observed by Subary Telescope iwamuro2004 , Spitzer jiang2006, hines2006 and Herschel leipski2013 , and therefore this allowed a full modelling of the SED of this QSO li2008, valiante2011, schneider2015,carniani2019 . In particular, schneider2015 derived that the AGN contribution to the dust heating in this QSO can be between 30 and 70 .

sources.summary: The paper uses a Keck spectrum for J1148+5251, notes prior damping-wing studies, predicts an exceptionally strong damping wing, and cautions that atmospheric masking of C 4 makes the prediction less reliable.

quotes.quote: For three of the quasars in our sample, i.e. J1030+0524, J159--02, and J1120+0641, we use combined Magellan/FIRE and VLT/X-Shooter vernet_x-shooter_2011 spectra, while one other spectrum in our sample, i.e. J1148+5251, was observed with Keck/MOSFIRE mclean_design_2010,mclean_mosfire_2012 and Keck/ESI sheinis_esi_2002 .

Of particular interest might be the predicted continua of ULAS J1120+0641, J0252–0503, J1148+5251, and J1030+0524, as the damping wings of these objects have been studied using a variety of methods mortlock_luminous_2011,davies_predicting_2018,wang_significantly_2020,greig_igm_2022,greig_constraints_2019,schroeder_evidence_2013,mesinger_constraints_2007 , including QSANNdRA durovcikova_reionization_2020 .

On the other hand, J1148+5251 is predicted to have an exceptionally strong damping wing, in agreement with the SDSS nearest neighbor composite. Upon closer inspection, one can notice that the C 4 line is partially masked due to atmospheric absorption, which renders this prediction less reliable due to our inability to make out the full profile of this emission line.

sources.summary: The paper presents a near-infrared spectrum of SDSSJ114816.64+525150.3 at z=6.40, measuring MgII, noting possible CIV blueshift, and finding a prominent FeII bump.

quotes.quote: In Fig. qso64 we show the resulting spectrum of the highest redshift quasar SDSSJ114816.64+525150.3, at z=6.40 fan03 The best redshift fitting the observed wavelength of MgII in our spectrum is 6.40, in agreement with the redshift obtained by willot03 and barth03 . , along with the identification of the major spectral features. Some blue-shift is observed for the CIV line. This shift may be due to an imperfect correction of the atmospheric absorption dip which occurs at the same wavelength; however, barth03 also find the same effect, suggesting that the shift may be real. The spectrum of the highest redshift quasar shows a prominent FeII bump.

sources.summary: The review uses J1148 + 5251 as a high-redshift QSO example where CO and [C ii] detections reveal enriched gas and make [C ii] promising for ALMA studies.

quotes.quote: A prime example is the detection of CO in the highest known redshift QSO (J114816.64 + 525150.3 at z=6.419 - Walter et al. \ 2003; Bertoldi et al. \ 2003), which implies significant enrichment of the gas at a time when the Universe was only 1/16th of its present age and had only recently been reionized.

The detection of the [ C ii ] 158 m line towards J1148 + 5251 at z=6.42 (Maiolino et al. \ 2005), the first of its kind, indicated a more feasible pathway for ALMA to study z>7 objects. Not only is the [ C ii ] line observable with ALMA at these redshifts, but in J1148 + 5251 it is 5 brighter than the brightest CO line observed in this source ( J=6-5 ), suggesting that [ C ii ] 158 m will be the line of choice when studying the earliest objects with ALMA, and that a strong synergy will develop between ALMA ( [ C ii ] ) and the EVLA (low- J CO lines).

sources.summary: The paper identifies J1148+5251 as the best-studied z ~6 quasar host, noting detections in dust, radio continuum, and CO emission and ALMA detectability at sub-kpc resolution.

quotes.quote: The best studied case is J1148+5251 at z = 6.42. The host has been detected in thermal dust, non-thermal radio continuum, and CO line emission Bertoldi2003,Carilli2004,Walteretal2004 . ALMA will be able to detect the thermal emission from a source like J1148+5251 in a few seconds at sub-kpc resolution Carilli2008 .

sources.summary: The paper includes J1148+5251 in its quasar model grid, finds a higher neutral-fraction KS peak than MH07, and notes that low neutral fractions still cannot be excluded.

quotes.quote: Our model grid covers a different range of R_S for each quasar: in units of comoving Mpc, for J1148+5251, R_S = 37, 39, 41, 43, 45,47 , for J1030+0524, R_S = 52, 54, 56, 58, 60, 62 , and for J1623+3112, R_S = 37, 39, 41, 43, 45,47 .

A notable difference from MH07 can be seen in the case of J1148+5251: the KS probability peaks at a higher neutral fraction ( =0.88 vs. 0.16), though this value is still consistent with the 1/3 rd probability contour of MH07.
As in MH07, we also find that low values of can not be excluded for J1148+5251.

sources.summary: The paper presents J1148+5251 as a z=6.42 quasar host with strong millimeter, CO, and [C II] emission, intense compact star formation, and elevated SMBH-bulge mass ratio.

quotes.quote: Strong millimeter dust continuum, molecular CO, and [ C II ] 158 m fine structure line emission were first detected from the z=6.42 quasar SDSS J114816.64+525150.3 (hereafter J1148+5251, ; ; ; ), indicating intense star formation with a peak surface density of 1000 M_ yr^ 1 kpc^ 2 over the central 1.5 kpc region ( ). The dynamical mass estimated with the resolved CO line emission indicates SMBH-bulge mass ratios more than one order of magnitude higher than the typical value found in the present universe ( ). The millimeter observations of J1148+5251 suggest an early phase of SMBH-galaxy evolution and the dust continuum, molecular/atomic line emission have been searched in more quasars at the highest redshift (e.g. ; ; ).

sources.summary: SDSSJ1148+5251 is presented as a z=6.4 QSO whose [CII] line has broad wings from host-galaxy outflow, implying an exceptionally strong QSO-driven massive outflow.

quotes.quote: We also present PdBI observations of the [ CII ] emission line in SDSSJ1148+5251 (S1148), one of the most distant QSO known, at z=6.4. We detect broad wings in the [ CII ] emission line, indicative of gas which is outflowing from the host galaxy. In particular, the extent of the wings, and the size of the [ CII ] emitting region associated to them, are indicative of a QSO-driven massive outflow with the highest outflow rate ever found ( M > 3500 M_ yr^ 1 ).

sources.summary: The paper cites SDSS J1148+5251 as a z=6.4 luminous quasar with a large CII-traced outflow whose energetics provide a comparison for dusty feedback models.

quotes.quote: The prototype is the luminous quasar SDSS J1148+5251 at z = 6.4 , in which a high-velocity outflow extending on large scales (up to 30 kpc) has been traced by the broad wings of the [ CII ] emission line Maiolino_et_2012, Cicone_et_2015 . The associated momentum rate and kinetic power are of the order of p L/ and E _ k 10^ 3 L .

For comparison, we plot in Fig. Fig_energetics_t_model_obs the observational values of the outflow energetics for SDSS J1148+5251 (black star symbol) and the high-redshift QSO sample (black diamond symbol), as well as the typical range of momentum rates and kinetic powers reported in Bischetti_et_2018 .

gmw_00000521.name: PKS 0625--354
gmw_00000521.overview: PKS 0625--354 is a Fanaroff-Riley I radio galaxy [arXiv:2108.01331, arXiv:2310.10206] and LINER [arXiv:2108.01331, arXiv:1301.4384] hosted by a giant elliptical galaxy [arXiv:2310.10206] in the Abell 3392 cluster [arXiv:1509.06851, arXiv:2310.10206]. It features a one-sided parsec-scale jet [arXiv:1301.4384] with two extended radio lobes [arXiv:2108.01331, arXiv:2401.07071] and exhibits variable high-energy gamma-ray emission [arXiv:2108.01331, arXiv:1410.2733].

sources.summary: PKS 0625-35 is the sample's most distant radio galaxy, luminous and non-variable; its gamma-ray fit favors stronger attenuation/cascade effects and a harder injected spectrum.

quotes.quote: PKS 0625-35 is the most distant radio galaxy in our sample (228 Mpc), but with a luminosity that is more than an order of magnitude larger than that of the Milky Way. PKS 0625-35 does not exhibit any significant evidence of variability .

In the case of PKS 0625-35, the effects of gamma-ray attenuation and the resulting electromagnetic cascade are more evident. The best-fit is found for a radiation column depth that is 12 (5) times that of the Milky Way for E_ cut =10^ 11 GeV ( 10^6 GeV), although with large uncertainties. A radiation density in the range favored by our fit would not be surprising given the high luminosity of this galaxy.

Again, we are not able to significantly constrain the magnetic field energy density, although values in the vicinity of a few G provide the best fit. The injected spectral index of this source is somewhat harder than that observed from Centaurus A, =2.07 0.10 for E_ cut =10^ 11 GeV (and even harder for E_ cut =10^ 6 GeV, for which the best-fit is =1.85 ). We note that the cascade is more prominent in the case of PKS 0625-35 than in that of Centaurus A in large part because of its harder spectral index, which causes a larger fraction of the total emitted energy to be attenuated and transferred into lower-energy cascade photons.

sources.summary: The paper characterizes 0625 as an FR I/LINER radio galaxy with BL Lac-like optical emission and extended lobes, reporting very-high-energy variability that constrains its jet viewing angle below 24 degrees.

quotes.quote: Furthermore, IC 310 ( z=0.019 ) and PKS 0625 354 ( z=0.055 ), classified as radio galaxies in the literature, appear as AGN of unknown type. 0625 is a Fanaroff-Riley (FR) I radio galaxy , also classified as a LINER . While the optical spectrum ( [ OIII ] line luminosity) resembling that of a BL Lac object , the kpc radio morphology clearly shows two extended lobe structures typical for a FR I radio galaxy. Radio observations revealed superluminal motion with v_ app 3.0 0.5 . This corresponds to the limit on the observation angle of the jet 37^ .

Thus, the Doppler factor constraint from the TeV variability provides an upper limit on the viewing angle of 0625 of <24^ in comparison to the upper limit of <53^ , reported previously based on the flux of the jet and the counterjet at parsec-scale radio maps . For the first time we have observed variability in the very-high-energy band from the AGN 0625. The variability provides a possibility to get an estimate of the upper limit on the viewing angle of the object of <24^ which is a tighter constraint than limits from radio measurements.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes the November 2018 TeV flare of PKS 0625 354, classifying it as an intermediate AGN with blazar-like and radio-galaxy properties and SSC-modeled broadband emission.

quotes.quote: Here, we investigate the nature of the object PKS 0625 354, its ray flux and spectral variability and its broad-band spectral emission with observations from H.E.S.S., Fermi-LAT, Swift-XRT, and UVOT taken in November 2018. The H.E.S.S. light curve above 200 GeV shows an outburst in the first night of observations followed by a declining flux with a halving time scale of 5.9 h. The opacity constrains the upper limit of the angle between the jet and the line of sight to 10^ . The broad-band spectral energy distribution shows two humps and can be well fitted with a single-zone synchrotron self Compton emission model. We conclude that PKS 0625 354, as an object showing clear features of both blazars and radio galaxies, can be classified as an intermediate active galactic nuclei.

0625 is a Fanaroff-Riley FR, Fanaroff type I radio galaxy ojha10 , located at a redshift of z=0.056 Lauer14 , and its host galaxy is classified as a low-ionization nuclear emission-line region LINER lewis03 . While the optical spectrum is that of a BL Lac object wills04 , the kpc radio morphology clearly shows two extended lobe structures typical for a radio galaxy angioni19 . Radio observations revealed superluminal motion with v_ app (2.9 0.9) angioni19 .

sources.summary: PKS 0625 354 is presented with new Suzaku and Fermi analyses; the paper favors jet-origin X-rays, finds gamma-ray variability, and models a hard-LAT, soft-X-ray SED.

quotes.quote: The Suzaku spectra for five of those have been presented previously, while the remaining three (M 87, PKS 0625 354, and 3C 78) are presented here for the first time. Based on the Fe-K line strength, X-ray variability, and X-ray power-law photon indices, and using additional information on the [ O III ] line emission, we argue for a jet origin of the observed X-ray emission in these three sources. We also analyzed five years of Fermi Large Area Telescope (LAT) GeV gamma-ray data on PKS 0625 354 and 3C 78 to understand these sources within the blazar picture. We found significant ray variability in the former object.

Overall, we note that the Suzaku spectra for both PKS 0625 354 and 3C 78 are rather soft, while the LAT spectra are unusually hard when compared with other ray detected low-power (FR I) radio galaxies. We demonstrate that the constructed broad-band spectral energy distributions of PKS 0625 354 and 3C 78 are well described by a one-zone synchrotron/synchrotron self-Compton model.

sources.summary: The paper describes PKS 0625-354 as an FRI radio galaxy in Abell 3392 at z=0.055, detected by Fermi LAT and established by H.E.S.S. as a VHE gamma-ray source.

quotes.quote: PKS 0625-354 (RA = 06 ^ h 27 ^ m 06.7 ^ s DEC = -35 ^ d 29 ^ m 15 ^ s , J2000) is a radiogalaxy of type FRI located in the Abell Cluster 3392, with a redshift value z = 0.055 . It was detected by the Fermi LAT in the high-energy (HE; 100 MeV < E < 100 GeV) regime as a rather hard spectrum source _ 3FGL = 1.88 0.06 with a flux of F _ 1 GeV 100 GeV = (1.43 0.11) 10 ^ 9 ph cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 .

PKS 0625-354 was monitored with ATOM in the R band during the period of November-December 2012 and in February 2013. Observations carried with the H.E.S.S. phase I array in 2012 have established PKS 0625-354 as VHE gamma-ray source, which enlarges the extragalactic non-blazar source class. The available multiwavelength data as depicted in Figure fig4 , although not simultaneous, will be used in future in a more detailed SED study and offers the possibility to discriminate between different SED models. No significant variability of the VHE flux was noticed during the HESS observations period (see Fig. fig4 ).

sources.summary: The paper reports an IceCube neutrino flare from blazar PKS 0625-35, H.E.S.S. follow-up at prior coordinates, and multiwavelength SED comparison showing no neutrino-flare association.

quotes.quote: On April 16, 2022, IceCube detected a neutrino flare originating from the blazar PKS 0625-35. The detection pre-trial significance was 3.56 , with a false alarm rate of 0.003 per year. The three neutrinos were detected within an energy range of 63 TeV to 302 TeV. Given that the source had been previously observed by , a ToO observation was initiated on the night of April 19th, 2022 centered on the coordinates of the previous detection: RA: 6h26m58s, Dec: -35d29m50s.

During the same ToO, the source was also observed in optical and X-ray wavelengths. By comparing the flux distribution in April 2022 with archival data https://tools.ssdc.asi.it/SED/ and the data from 2018, it becomes evident that there was no variation in the shape of the spectral energy distribution (SED) , implying no association with the neutrino flare.

sources.summary: The paper describes PKS 0625--354 as a misaligned FR I radio galaxy/LINER with Fermi detection, BL Lac support, a one-sided parsec jet, and measured superluminal motion.

quotes.quote: The misaligned radio galaxy PKS 0625--354 ( z=0.55 ) has a large scale FR I structure , but it is also classified as a LINER . It has been detected by Fermi /LAT as a high luminosity and hard spectrum source, supporting a BL Lac classification . Our TANAMI observations revealed a single-sided, parsec-scale jet morphology (Fig. fig3 ). We identify the jet core as the optically thick and brightest component and use it as the reference point to determine the separation of each component from the core over time. The preliminary kinematic analysis results in a superluminal motion of v_ app 3.0 0.5c (Tr ustedt et al., in prep.) .

sources.summary: The paper describes QSO B0625-354 as an elliptical gamma-ray source with BL Lac/AGN classifications, Seyfert/WLRG alternatives, and an inefficiently accreting massive black hole.

quotes.quote: QSO B0625-354 (LEDA 2824793, PKS 0625-35) is the elliptical galaxy at z= 0.05459 ( M_V=-27.5 ) and a well-known source of gamma radiation that was included in the Online Gamma-Ray Catalog TeVCat with name TeV J0626-354. The discovery of gamma-ray emission from this source was announced by Dyrda et al. at the 34th International Cosmic Ray Conference (ICRC 2015) . The classification of this object in TeVCat has been BL Lac of unknown type and later it was updated to an AGN of unknown type following the discussion by Rieger and Levinson . It's alternative classification in NED is Seyfert 3 (LINER type 1) and Elliptical Weak-line radio galaxy (WLRG) . PKS 0625-354 has a black hole of mass approx. 10^9 M_ that is probably accreting in an inefficient mode .

sources.summary: The paper reports PKS0625-35 has strong non-thermal core contamination, a prominent optical jet, ALMA-scale jet/lobe/hotspot emission, and far-infrared flux dominated by a variable non-thermal core.

quotes.quote: Four of them (PKS0035-02, PKS0625-35, PKS1136-13, PKS1839-48) also have strong non-thermal contamination from their cores, as discussed below. In such objects, while the core contamination dominates, there may also be a contribution from extended jets, lobes or hotspots, based on their radio size and the extrapolation of the extended radio emission through to the infrared part of the SED. Indeed, two of these objects show prominent, one-sided jets in optical images PKS0625-35, PKS1136-13: ramos11a , and three (PKS0035-02, PKS0625-35, PKS1839-48) show relatively strong emission from their jet/lobes/hotspots on the scale of the Herschel PACS beam in our ALMA 100GHz images.

However, it may be apparent, for example, in the SED of PKS0625-35, which is dominated at all far-IR wavelengths by non-thermal core emission, but whose LABOCA and Herschel points -- well-represented by a single power-law -- fall above an interpolation of the ALMA and Spitzer MIPS points. This perhaps suggests that its non-thermal core emission was stronger in the period 2012 -- 2013 than it was either earlier or later.

sources.summary: The paper treats PKS 0625-35 as a TeV source with FR-I and BL Lac properties, superluminal jet motion, and a Doppler factor suggesting a misaligned blazar.

quotes.quote: PKS 0625-35, a TeV-detected source, displays both FR-I and BL Lac properties wil04,ran19 . Based on the Tracking Active Nuclei with Austral Milliarcsecond Interferometry (TANAMI) observations, mue12 found the presence of the superluminal motion of 3 0.5c in the jet of PKS 0625-35. The superluminal motion of 3 is also later confirmed by ang19 . The jet viewing angle for PKS 0625-35 is constrained to 15 from the X-ray variability hes18 , or <53 from the VLBI observations ang19 . Our Doppler factor estimation for PKS 0625-35, =6.85 , is similar to the median Doppler factor ( _ BL =7.26 ) of the 126 BL Lacs in this work, suggesting that this FR-I is a misaligned blazar producing a strong beaming effect.

sources.summary: The paper identifies 0625 354 as an FR I radio galaxy in a giant elliptical host, with TANAMI history, superluminal core motions, an S-band jet, and VHE detection.

quotes.quote: 0625 354: 0625 354 is an FR I radio galaxy, hosted by a giant elliptical galaxy in the center of Abell 3392 . First TANAMI images of the source at 8.4 GHz were published in ojha10 . Based on the kinematic analysis of nine epochs angioni19 found superluminal motions with apparent speeds reaching 3 from the radio core. In the S band the jet extends 20 mas to the southeast, but tapered images of ojha10 reveal a jet up to 40 mas. Among many other FR I radio galaxies, 0625-354 has also been detected at very high energies dyrda15 with an integral flux of 0.04 CU and photon index of =2.8 0.5 .

sources.summary: The paper describes PKS 0625 35 as a core-dominated radio galaxy with a one-sided jet, non-thermal nuclear X-rays, and a VLBI image consistent with its larger-scale structure.

quotes.quote: PKS 0625 35 (OH 342) The arcsecond scale emission for this radio galaxy is dominated by a bright core, with a one-sided jet in p.a. +160^ , embedded in a low brightness halo (Simpson 1994), which extends out to 4^ in the low resolution image of Ekers et al. (1989). X--ray BeppoSAX observations (Trussoni et al. 1999) are in agreement with the presence of a non-thermal hard X--ray component, likely to be of nuclear origin. The X--ray emission from the central region appears unabsorbed. These observational properties could be interpreted either as the source being oriented at a small angle to the line of sight or that it is not heavily affected by obscuration from a circumnuclear torus. Our VLBI image, shown in Figure fig:0625 , is in agreement with its arcsecond scale structure.

sources.summary: The paper reports PKS 0625 354 as one of two TANAMI radio galaxies detected by Fermi LAT, noting its FR I radio-galaxy morphology and BL Lac-like optical spectrum.

quotes.quote: The two detected radio galaxies are PKS 0625 354 (OH 342) and PKS 1322 428 (Cen A).

Cen A is the closest AGN and PKS 0625 354 is interesting because it exhibits an FR I radio-galaxy morphology whereas its optical spectrum is similar to that of a BL Lac object Wills2004 . Further TANAMI observations are necessary to determine properties of the inner radio jet of this source, such as its speed.

All BL Lac objects and a large fraction of quasars (71 ) are detected in the ray regime, whereas from the 11 radio galaxies only the closest one (Cen A) and one (PKS 0625 354) with an optical spectrum similar to a BL Lac object were detected with Fermi LAT.

sources.summary: The paper identifies G4Jy 614 as a LERG near Abell 3392, with a measured redshift, companion galaxies, and membership in several radio-source catalogs.

quotes.quote: G4Jy 614: lies close to the galaxy cluster Abell 3392 abell58,abell89,quintana95a,ebeling96 , originally detected in the X-rays by Trussoni et al. (1999) and then confirmed by Ramos Almeida et al. (2013), Ineson et al. (2015) and Mingo et al. (2017). The radio source is optically classified as a LERG, and it has a redshift estimate of z =0.054855 tadhunter93,wills04,jones09 . There are also several companion galaxies clearly detected in the optical image used to carry out our analysis. It is listed in both the MS4 sample burgess06a and in the CRATES catalog healey07 as well as in the 2 Jy one see e.g., wall85,morganti93,mingo14 .

sources.summary: The paper identifies 2FGL J0627.1 3528 with the BL Lac-like BCG in A 3392 and reports a stronger LAT detection with a consistent photon index.

quotes.quote: The source 2FGL J0627.1 3528 is associated with the BCG in A 3392, which is classified as a BL Lac object. It is listed with a 24-month detection significance of 12.6, 100 MeV E 100 GeV flux of (1.50 0.18) 10^ 9 ph cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 and photon index of 1.93 0.09. With 21 months more data (and in an energy band 300 MeV E 300 GeV ) we calculate an increased detection significance of 14.9 , and a consistent (within errors) photon index, as expected.

sources.summary: The paper describes PKS 0625-25 as a VHE-detected radio galaxy with a hard photon index and a possible cutoff near 100 GeV.

quotes.quote: PKS 0625-25: This is one of the radio galaxies detected in the VHE band dyrda . Its photon index, =1.89 0.04 , is unusually harder as compared with those of other radio galaxies. Around 100 GeV, there is a hint of substantial deviation from PL (black curve in Fig. sed_all [ d ] ), but the PLEC model with =1.83 0.06 and E _ cut =131.2 88.04 GeV (Fig. sed_all [ d ] , red curve) is preferred over the simple PL only at the 2 level, so that the PL can not be rejected.

sources.summary: The paper includes PKS 0625-35 despite possible BL Lac classification, finding variability, hard emission above 30 GeV, and noting its TeV detection.

quotes.quote: We have included PKS 0625-35 in the list of selected radio galaxies; however, as discussed in a recent paper Abdalla-2018-MNRAS , there is evidence to suggest that this galaxy could be a BL Lac object and thus its classification as a ``misaligned blazar'' may need to be reconsidered.
We find statistical evidence of flux variability on 6 month timescales for seven of the 26 radio galaxies analysed: 3C 111, 3C 120, 3C 264, IC 4516, NGC 1218, NGC 2892 and PKS 0625-35.
Six of the radio galaxies studied, 3C 264, 4C +39.12, B2 1447+27, NGC 1218, NGC 2329 and PKS 0625-35, have particularly hard spectra and emission above 30 GeV.

gmw_00000522.name: 1ES 0229+200
gmw_00000522.overview: 1ES 0229+200 is an extreme high-frequency-peaked BL Lacertae object [arXiv:1109.3628, arXiv:2310.01635] hosted by an elliptical galaxy at a redshift of z = 0.1396 [arXiv:1509.04470]. The active nucleus exhibits a hard very-high-energy gamma-ray spectrum [arXiv:2208.03176] and displays flux variability across X-ray and TeV bands [arXiv:1109.3628, arXiv:1509.04470].

sources.summary: The paper characterizes 1ES 0229+200 as an extreme high-frequency-peaked BL Lac with hard X-rays/TeV emission, UV synchrotron cutoff, and an unusually narrow high-energy electron distribution.

quotes.quote: The blazar 1ES 0229+200 is a high frequency peaked BL Lac object with a hard TeV spectrum extending to 10 TeV . Its unusual spectral characteristics make it a frequently used probe for intergalactic radiation and magnetic fields. With new, simultaneous observations in the optical, ultraviolet (UV) and X-rays, the synchrotron emission is probed in great detail. The X-ray emission varies by a factor of 2 in 2009, while being rather stable in 2010. The X-ray spectrum is very hard ( 1.8 ) and it shows an indication of excess absorption above the Galactic value.

The X-ray emission is detected up to 100 keV without any significant cut-off, thus 1ES 0229+200 belongs to the class of extreme blazars. The simultaneous measured, host galaxy- and extinction-corrected optical and UV fluxes illustrate that the cut-off of the low energy part of the synchrotron emission is located in the UV regime. Other extreme blazars have similar synchrotron peak frequencies but much softer TeV spectra, hence 1ES 0229+200 has one of the highest inverse Compton (IC) peak frequency and the narrowest electron distribution among the extreme blazars known to date.

sources.summary: The paper describes 1ES 0229+200 as an HBL blazar in an elliptical host at z=0.1396, then presents 2004-2013 H.E.S.S. monitoring showing VHE variability and TeV/X-ray correlation supporting an SSC model.

quotes.quote: 1ES 0229+200 is a blazar, and a member of the high frequency peaked BL Lac objects (HBL) subclass. It is hosted in an elliptical galaxy located at a redshift z = 0.1396. It was first discovered in X-rays by the Einstein IPC Slew Survey in 1992 . Its synchrotron emission reaches 100 keV and possibly up to 200 keV http://bat.ifcaf.it/100m_bat_catalog/100m_bat_catalog_v0.0.htm .

The 10 years long H.E.S.S. monitoring of the blazar 1ES 0229+200 between 2004 and 2013 has been presented for the first time in a MWL context. Clear variability is detected at VHE on monthly and yearly timescales. A hint of correlation between TeV and X-ray emission comes from the contemporaneous observations with XMM- and with in 2009: the fluxes increase in all energy bands in similar way over four months. This supports an SSC emission model for the X-ray and VHE emission in this source.

sources.summary: The paper uses lES 0229+200 as a nearby high-energy ray source, shifts its deabsorbed spectrum to higher redshifts, and finds EBL fluctuations could affect detectability through small slope changes.

quotes.quote: As a starting point we use the deabsorbed spectrum of lES 0229+200 (cf. Fig. fig:slop ) which is a nearby high ray energy source. We then shift this spectrum to various redshifts by ``absorbing'' it, i.e. multiplying it by (z,E)_ mean . This is the reverse process to deabsorbing it. Figure fig:CTA shows the absorbed data points. Purple diamonds, red stars and green circles correspond to redshifts, z =0.3 , 0.5 , and 1.0 , respectively. For comparison we copy the observed and the deabsorbed data as already displayed in Figure fig:slop .

The spectrum of the source shifted to z=0.5 , for instance, drops below the CTA sensitivity for ray energies above 700 GeV. It is interesting to note that the slope of the spectrum is very similar to the slope of the sensitivity limit. Thus a slight change in the slope of the observed spectrum, possibly due to fluctuations in the EBL, can have an effect on the detectability of the ray source.

sources.summary: The paper identifies 1ES 0229+200 as the best hard-TeV BL Lac example, with an extreme synchrotron peak, full NuSTAR-band detection, and UV excess challenging one-zone SSC modeling.

quotes.quote: In addition to archival data on 1ES 0229+200 ( z =0.140), the best example of this new class of BL Lacs, new observations were performed on 1ES 0347-121 ( z =0.188), 1ES 0414 + 009 ( z =0.287), RGB J0710 + 591 ( z =0.125), 1ES 1101-232 ( z =0.186) and 1ES 1218 + 304 ( z =0.182). In total, we analyzed contemporaneous and observations of six hard-TeV BL Lacs , three of which are characterized historically by hard X-ray spectra, and three by steep X-ray spectra.

The peak energy is around 10 keV for 1ES 0229+200, and in the range 1--4 keV for the other four objects. 1ES 0229+200 therefore confirms its most extreme character in the synchrotron as well as Compton emission. It is the only object in our sample detected over the entire band up to 79 keV, and it is also characterized by the hardest slope in the soft X-ray band ( _X 1.5 , with a systematic uncertainty of 0.1 due to the uncertainty on the Galactic N _ H values).

This scenario is corroborated by direct evidence in the X-ray data of 1ES 0229+200 and RGB J0710+591. Their UV flux is in excess of the extrapolation of the soft X-ray spectrum to lower energies. The model can be made to reproduce well either the UV data (over-estimating the soft-X spectrum) or the soft-X spectrum (under-estimating the UV flux), but not both.

sources.summary: The paper uses 1ES 0229+200 as the reference EHBL, emphasizing its hard TeV spectrum, low variability, record synchrotron peak, and successful TeV extrapolation test.

quotes.quote: Finally, in the case of 1ES 0229+200, that is one of the most studied TeV gamma-ray detected EHBLs, the hard TeV spectrum was successfully interpreted also in the previously mentioned intergalactic cascades scenario Murase . Interestingly, EHBLs do not show high and rapid variability, as other blazars like HBLs do (e.g. the low variability of 1ES 0229+200 on the long-term lightcurve in VHE gamma rays of Cologna:2015nia ).

The archetypal EHBL is 1ES 0229+200 - the source with the highest synchrotron peak ever found Kaufmann:2011-1es0229 . Its archival SED has been enriched by several multi-wavelength (MWL) observational campaigns during the last years, and for this reason from now on we adopt it as our reference EHBL. Such hard spectra similar to 1ES 0229+200 one have been reported on few other sources, like for example 1ES 0347-121 0347discovery , RGB J0710+591 0710discovery , and 1ES 1101-232 1101discovery . Such objects have been named ``hard-TeV blazars'' by Nustar_EHBLs due to the high energy peak position located above about 10 TeV.

In fig:1ES0229+200_extrapolation_with_TeV_data we show an example of our extrapolation on the 1ES 0229+200, showing both the extrapolation and the TeV data obtained by the SSDC website 0229_hess_points . Considering in this plot E_ cut-off =12 TeV as reported in Nustar_EHBLs , as expected we see that our extrapolation matches the real TeV data (not EBL de-absorbed).

sources.summary: The appendix describes 1ES 0229+200 as an HBL/EHBL hosted by an elliptical galaxy, with H.E.S.S. and VERITAS VHE detections, yearly variability, hard X-ray emission, and a hard Fermi LAT spectrum.

quotes.quote: The blazar 1ES 0229+200 was initially classified as HBL in 1995 from X-ray to radio flux ratio after its discovery in 1992 by the Einstein Slew Survey . The source is hosted by an elliptical galaxy at redshift z = 0.139 . The first VHE -ray emission from 1ES 0229+200 was discovered by the H.E.S.S. telescopes during 2005--2006 observations above an energy threshold of 580 GeV . The observed VHE spectrum was described by a power law with a spectral index of 2.50 0.19 in the energy range 500 GeV--15 TeV. The long term monitoring of 1ES 0229+200 by the VERITAS telescope during 2009--2012 detected a significant VHE -ray emission from the source with the observed spectrum described by a simple power law of spectral index 2.59 0.12 in the energy range 300 GeV--16 TeV .

The VHE emission detected with the VERITAS telescope indicated a long term variability on a yearly timescale. Near simultaneous X-ray observations suggested a hard spectrum with an index 1.7 and the position of synchrotron peak at energy above 10 keV in the SED. The Fermi LAT observations suggested that the high energy spectrum of the source can be described by a hard power law with a spectral index of 1.36 0.25 in the energy range 1--300 GeV .

sources.summary: The review identifies 1ES 0229+200 as the prototypical extreme-TeV blazar, notes hard TeV emission and variability, and discusses leptonic and proton-synchrotron modeling.

quotes.quote: The record for the highest ray peak energy observed is held by the prototypical extreme-TeV blazar 1ES 0229+200 (see Fig. Fig:observation ), with h _ 10 TeV and an intrinsic spectrum with _ 1.5 . Most extreme-TeV sources appear steady over years at TeV energies, although large statistical uncertainties make it difficult to rule out flux variations such as those seen in X-rays over similar timespans. The only firm evidence was found in 1ES 1218+304, with a fast flare over a few days reaching 20 of the Crab nebula flux . There are also indications of a factor of 2-3 variations in 1ES 0229+200 on year timescales . In both cases, the TeV spectrum remained hard, indicating no change in the extreme-TeV character of these objects.

As an example, the model for 1ES 0229+200 in Fig. Fig:observation and Fig. Fig:models assumes a moderate _ min = 100 , a very hard particle spectrum with index p =1.4 , a high Doppler factor _D = 50 and a low magnetic field of 2 mG. The electron energy density dominates over the magnetic one by a factor of 10^5 .

As an example, Fig. Fig:models shows a one-zone proton synchrotron model and an external cascade model applied to 1ES 0229+200. The proton synchrotron model requires a much larger magnetic field ( B' 20 G) than the leptonic model, a comparable value for _ min 100 and a smaller Doppler factor, _ D 30 . The particle spectra are again very hard and the considerable energy of the protons leads to a jet power at the Eddington limit. This illustrative proton synchrotron model predicts a peak neutrino flux of 10^ 15 erg cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 around 10^ 19 eV.

sources.summary: The paper models 1ES 0229+200 among six extreme BL Lacs, explaining its broadband SED with a two-zone lepto-hadronic scenario involving inner-blob photohadronic and outer-blob SSC/EC emission.

quotes.quote: Finally, using the parameters listed in Table tab_model_parameters we model successfully the broadband SED of six known extreme BL Lac 1ES 0229+200, RGB J0710+591, 1ES 0347-121, 1ES 1101-232, 1ES 1218-304, 1ES 0414+009. The result of our model is shown in Figure ( fig_SED_result ). It is important to mention that in this work, we have not included the contribution observed in the optical band produced by the host galaxy Costamante2018 . The extreme BL Lac behavior presents challenges for a pure leptonic or hadronic model with one-zone emission. These objects demand atypical parameters for explaining the hard spectrum shape, especially in a VHE gamma-ray band.

In particular cases, we described the six best-known extreme BL Lacs using our proposed model, and we found a feasible explanation that relaxes the atypical values required by the one-zone models. We showed that the annihilation line radiation produced in a mildly relativistic outflow pair-plasma and the DT play essential roles in forming the hard spectrum observed in the VHE gamma-ray bands. In our model, this gamma-ray bump is produced mainly in two regions: i) the inner blob, which is responsible for the highest energy contribution above TeV-energies via photo-hadronic interactions with the annihilation line photons from the pair-plasma, and ii) the outer blob, where the very-high-energy gamma rays are created by EC with seed photons from the DT, and by the SSC mechanism.

sources.summary: The paper models 1ES 0229 + 200 as a hard-TeV BL Lac whose multi-TeV emission should be detectable and spectrally characterized by ASTRI Mini-Array observations.

quotes.quote: 1ES 0229+200 The VHE ray emission of 1ES 0229 + 200 was discovered by H.E.S.S. in 2007 2007A&A...475L...9A . Later, the source was observed also by VERITAS aliu14 and MAGIC mag19 . By correcting the observed spectrum taking into account the EBL absorption effect, a very hard intrinsic spectrum with a photon index _ intr = 1.5 is obtained. Thus, the ray emission peak results to be located in the multi-TeV range. Due to its spectral characteristics, this source is classified as a hard-TeV BL Lac.

The TS values derived ensure a solid detection ( > 5 ) in each of the simulated energy bins. From this study, we expect to detect this source with observations of less than 100-h duration; observations of about 200 h will allow a good characterization of the spectrum. Fig. fig:1es0229_simul shows the results of our simulation for 200-h observations with the ASTRI Mini-Array compared to existing datasets.

sources.summary: The paper reports TESS detection of a rapid optical flare in 1ES 0229+200, constraining emission-region size and favoring a compact jet or reconnection origin.

quotes.quote: The blazar 1ES 0229+200 was first detected in the Einstein Imaging Proportional Counter (IPC) Slew Survey 1992ApJS...80..257E and subsequently classified as a BL Lacertae object 1993ApJ...412..541S .
