(galaxies), BL Lac Objects -- Individual: 1ES 0120+340, RGB J0710+591, 1ES 1101-232, 1ES 1741+196, 1ES 2322-409 Blazars are a subclass of active galactic nuclei (AGN) that emit non-thermal, strongly polarized and variable continuum emission from a jet of relativistic plasma directed along or close to the line of sight RogerDBlandford_1978, 1995PASP..107..803U .

This paper reports on recent observations carried out using \ and \ of five sources: 1ES 0120+340 (redshift z=0.272 ), RGB J0710+591 ( z=0.125 ), 1ES 1101-232 ( z=0.186 ), 1ES 1741+196 ( z=0.084 ), and 1ES 2322-409 ( z=0.1736 ), each displaying a unique range of spectral characteristics. Among these, RGB J0710+591 and 1ES 1101-232 are well-known for being extreme TeV sources with hard intrinsic TeV spectra.

The low photon indices ( _ LAT 1.6 ) of the observed spectra for 1ES 0120+340, RGB J0710+591 and 1ES 1101-232 indicate that the high-energy peak may be located at energies above 1 TeV, leading to their classification as extreme TeV blazars.

sources.summary: The fit for 1ES 1101-232 differs from the other modeled extreme TeV BL Lacs, requiring a larger region, weaker magnetic field, and higher particle and turbulence powers.

quotes.quote: We apply the procedure described above to four Extreme TeV BL Lacs: 1ES 0229+200, 1ES 0347-121, RGB J0710+591, and 1ES 1101-232 taken from . All these EHBLs have a well-characterized SED with a synchrotron peak well-tracked by Swift and NuSTAR and a high-energy peak covered by Fermi and Cherenkov telescopes.

For 1ES 1101-232 the fit provides a radius much larger than the value derived for the other sources ( R 1 pc ), together with a smaller magnetic field and higher electron and turbulence powers. These differences are probably connected to the steeply decreasing X-ray spectrum (i.e. a relatively low synchrotron peak frequency), a feature absent in the other sources. A sub-pc-sized emission region can be obtained by applying a larger relativistic Doppler factor ( 40 ).

sources.summary: The paper treats 1ES 1101-232 as one of two H.E.S.S. blazars whose unexpectedly hard TeV spectra imply a low optical/near-infrared EBL close to resolved galaxy-count limits.

quotes.quote: The recent H.E.S.S. results on the blazars 1ES 1101-232 and H 2356-309 represent a breakthrough on this issue. Their unexpectedly hard spectra allow an upper limit to be derived on the EBL in the optical/near-infrared range, which is very close to the lower limit given by the resolved galaxy counts. This result seems to exclude a large contribution to the EBL from other sources (e.g. Population III stars) and indicates that the intergalactic space is more transparent to rays than previously thought.

Showing an unexpected hardness for their redshift, the combined spectra of the HBLs 1ES 1101-232 and H 2356-309 (now supported by two further TeV sources) provide strong circumstantial evidence for a low density of the EBL. With minimal assumptions on the blazar properties, the spectra allow the derivation of an upper limit on the EBL in the Opt-NIR range which is very close to the lower limit given by the integrated light of resolved galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper describes 1ES 1101-232 as an X-ray selected BL Lac hosted by an elliptical galaxy, with a one-sided radio jet, very low Suzaku X-ray flux, and curved hard-X-ray spectrum.

quotes.quote: The X-ray selected BL Lac object (XBL) 1ES 1101-232, hosted by an elliptical galaxy at redshift z=0.186 with estimated magnitude m_R=16.41 , one of the brightest BL Lac hosts known so far, is presumably located in a galaxy cluster.
The radio maps of 1ES 1101-232 show a one-sided, not well-collimated jet structure at a few kpc distance from the core .

Our Suzaku observations, made approximately 10 months later, found the lowest ever measured X-ray flux from 1ES 1101-232. So far, essentially all sensitive X-ray observations of 1ES 1101-232 prefer a curved or broken power-law representation instead a simple power-law , independent of flux level and - as demonstrated in this work - extending into the hard X-ray range.

sources.summary: The paper uses 1ES 1101-232 as a distant TeV blazar constraining the EBL; under the AHA0.65 scenario its absorption-corrected intrinsic spectral index is 1.28.

quotes.quote: Imran 1 1 Department of Physics and Astronomy, Iowa State University, Ames, IA, 5011-3160, USA, e-mail: krennrich@iastate.edu 2 Observational Cosmology Laboratory, Code 665, NASA Goddard Space Flight Center, Greenbelt, MD 20771, e-mail: eli.dwek@nasa.gov We combine the recent estimate of the contribution of galaxies to the 3.6 \ intensity of the extragalactic background light (EBL) with optical and near-infrared (IR) galaxy counts to set new limits on intrinsic spectra of some of the most distant TeV blazars 1ES 0229+200, 1ES 1218+30.4, and 1ES 1101-232, located at redshifts 0.1396, 0.182, and 0.186, respectively. The new lower limit on the 3.6 \ EBL intensity is significantly higher than the previous one set by the cumulative emission from resolved \ galaxies. Correcting for attenuation by the revised EBL, we show that the differential spectral index of the intrinsic spectrum of the three blazars is 1.28 0.20 or harder. These results present blazar emission models with the challenge of producing extremely hard intrinsic spectra in the sub-TeV to multi-TeV regime.

By searching for the combination of intrinsic spectra of these three sources that yield the softest spectral indices, one finds that the AHA0.65 scenario gives a spectral index _ i = 1.28 0.20 for 1ES 1101-232, _ i = 1.30 0.38 for 1ES 1218+30.4, while 1ES 0229+200 gives _ i = 2.40 0.13 .

sources.summary: The paper treats 1ES 1101-232 as a distant VHE blazar for testing EBL attenuation, reporting that a modified EBL density can leave its inferred intrinsic spectrum fitted with a power-law index of at least 1.5.

quotes.quote: Ref. krennrich:2008a (KDI08) argued that for the VHE source 1ES 0229+200 ( z = 0.1396 ), 1ES 1218+304 ( z = 0.182 ), 1ES 1101-232 ( z = 0.186 ) such a high EBL density should always lead to intrinsic spectra harder than =1.28 0.20 .

We will, therefore, only consider the spectra of the blazars 1ES 1101-232 and 1ES 0229+200 . In addition, we will also show results for 1ES 1218+304 to facilitate the comparison with the results from KDI08.
The resulting intrinsic spectra are shown in Fig. fig:spectra and the fit results are given in Tab. ta1 . All intrinsic spectra all well fitted with power laws with 1.5 or larger.

sources.summary: The paper models 1ES 1101-232 as a hard-spectrum distant VHE source whose weak flux and strong attenuation limit new low- or high-energy leverage for EBL tests.

quotes.quote: Shown are results for 1ES 1101-232 ( z=0.186 ), a hard spectrum ( _ VHE 3 ) distant source, whose discovery at VHE energies enabled to derive strong limits on the EBL density in the optical to near-infrared , and PKS 2155-304, which is a fairly strong VHE source (about 20 Crab in the quiescente state) with a softer spectrum ( _ VHE 3.3 ) located at an intermediate redshift of z=0.116 . In the case of 1ES 1101-232 the hard measured spectrum in combination with the larger distance leads to a very hard intrinsic spectrum (i.e. harder than anticipated by simple leptonic and hadronic models), even for the low EBL model. Such a hard intrinsic spectrum coupled with the relative weakness of the source ( 2 Crab) leads to a simulated spectrum which does not largely increase the energy range covered compared to the measurement of 1ES 1101-232: neither at low energies (due to the hard spectrum) nor at high energies (the strong EBL attenuation suppresses the signal below the NGCTS sensitivity). Even for the case of the two very different assumed EBL models, no strong difference in the simulated spectra is apparent, which could be used to differentiate between the models.

sources.summary: The paper models 1ES 1101-232 as an elliptical-hosted HBL with HESS detections, hard spectrum, quiescent low X-ray state, and Fermi/LAT upper limit.

quotes.quote: 1ES 1101-232. A HBL object hosted by an elliptical galaxy, it was detected by HESS in March-June 2004 and 2005 with a very hard spectrum and no significant variation was found (Aharonian et al. 2007a). A multiwavelength campaign data, including observations of VHE by HESS and X-ray by RXTE satellite in 2005 and XMM-Newton in 2004, were obtained (red squares in Fig. Fig:1 , Costamante 2007). The Suzaku observation simultaneously covered with the HESS measurement was carried out in 2006 May (blue squares in Fig. Fig:1 , Reimer et al. 2008). No significant X-ray or gamma-ray variability was detected during this program, and the object was in a quiescent state with the lowest X-ray flux ever measured.

sources.summary: The paper identifies 1ES1101-232 as one of seven high-frequency-peaked blazars whose optically thick measurements contribute comparably to the excess, with a 2.8-sigma leave-one-out relevance.

quotes.quote: The seven sources with measurements extending to >2 contribute about equally to the overall excess. Removing each source spectrum and re-calculating the significance given in the following list provides an estimate on the relevance of each source: Mkn 421 ( S( Mkn 421 )=3.8 ), Mkn 501 ( S( Mkn 501 )=3.7 ), H 1426+428 ( S( H 1426+428 )=3.5 ), 1ES1101-232 ( S( 1ES1101-232 )=2.8 ), 1ES0347-121 ( S( 1ES0347-121 )=4.6 ), 1ES0414+009 ( S( 1ES0414+009 )=3.6 ), 3C279 ( S( 3C279 )=3.8 ). The result demonstrates that the different objects (6 high frequency peaked Blazars and 1 flat spectrum radio quasar) at widely different redshifts ( z=0.031 , 0.034 , 0.129 , 0.186 , 0.188 , 0.287 , and 0.536 ) contribute about equally to the overall significance. A systematic effect based upon the type of source or its distance is not evident.

sources.summary: The paper observes 1ES 1101-232 as a TeV HBL with multi-epoch VLBA imaging, finding a 25 mJy core and a short northeastern jet represented by one component.

quotes.quote: We observed the five TeV blazars 1ES 1101-232, Markarian 180, 1ES 1218+304, PG 1553+113, and H 2356-309 at five epochs each from 2006 to 2009 with the VLBA under observation code BP146, at frequencies from 5 to 22 GHz, for a total of 25 new VLBA images produced for this paper.
All sources were detected with direct fringe fitting at all epochs, with peak flux densities ranging from 14 to 148 mJy beam ^ 1 .

1ES 1101 232 Observations of this blazar at a redshift of z =0.186 were made at frequencies of 5 and 8 GHz. Its structure consists of a 25 mJy core, and a short jet extension to the northeast that is represented as a single 5 mJy component in our modeling.

sources.summary: The paper compares Fermi and HESS data for 1ES 1101-232 with cascade simulations and finds compatibility with zero IGMF despite poorly fitted high-energy VHE structure.

quotes.quote: The VHE data set of 1ES 1101-232 consists of 3 periods of observations spanning from April 2004 - March 2005, for a total of 43 hours. The time-averaged dFED is reported for 10 bins over the energy range 225 GeV - 4 TeV. The Fermi LAT dFED for 1ES 1101-232 is especially weak, and in fact, the first bin allows a determination of only an upper limit (TS < 9) for all simulated models tested.

Figures 1es1101_B0 a and b illustrate the compatibility of the 1ES 1101-232 VHE and HE data sets with H0 , despite the fact that the previously described feature in the highest energy bins of the VHE regime is not well fitted by the models.

sources.summary: The paper includes 1ES 1101-232 as a hard-TeV BL Lac; its UV and X-ray spectra can be modeled together, though WISE data and Compton-peak constraints complicate the fit.

quotes.quote: However, there are cases of objects which are extreme only in Compton but not in synchrotron, such as 1ES 1101-232 gerd1101 , 1ES 1218 + 304 veritas1218 and 1ES 0414 + 009 hess0414 . Others are extreme in synchrotron but not in Compton, such as 1ES 1426+428 (which is characterized by a steep VHE spectrum after correction for absorption with recent EBL calculations, see e.g. dominguez13 ). To reproduce a hard TeV spectrum most scenarios rely on electron distributions with hard features, in the form of ``pile-up'', narrow-peaked distributions or low-energy cutoffs at high energy.

For two other objects, 1ES 0347-121 and 1ES 1101-232, this scenario is not strictly necessary: the UV and X-ray spectra can be fitted with a single synchrotron component (though not the WISE data in 1ES 1101-232). However, the resulting gamma-ray emission tends to slightly underestimate the frequency of the Compton peak with respect to the indication of the VHE spectra (i.e. below 1 TeV instead of above it, though the statistical uncertainty is large). The Compton peak can be better reproduced allowing again for a harder synchrotron spectrum below the X-ray band (compare for example curves a and b in Fig seds ).

sources.summary: The paper describes 1ES 1101-232 across 2005, 2010, and 2015-2016, tracking changes in peak energy, luminosity, and curvature and matching earlier 2005 results.

quotes.quote: 1ES 1101-232 had an average peak energy of 2.5 keV, an average peak luminosity of 10 ^ 45.6 \ erg \ s ^ 1 , and a curvature value of 0.5 in 2005. In 2010, its peak energy increased to 4.5 keV, peak luminosity decreased to 10 ^ 45.3 \ erg \ s ^ 1 , and curvature increased to about 1. Between 2015 and 2016, its peak energy decreased to about 2 keV, peak luminosity increased to 10 ^ 45.4 \ erg \ s ^ 1 , and the curvature decreased to 0.3 (similar to that in 2005). Our result based on the 2005 \ data is consistent with that of \ based on the 2005 \ data.

sources.summary: The paper identifies 1ES 1101-232 as a hard-spectrum TeV BL Lac with stable 2005 VHE flux and one of three spectra better fit by the GCA model.

quotes.quote: 1ES 0229+200, 1ES 1101-232, 1ES 0347-121 and PKS 1424+240, may be hard spectrum sources with an intrinsic photon index _ VHE 1.5 .

Except for 1ES 1101-232(2004) and 1ES 0229+200(2005), the Fermi-LAT data and IACTs data in each spectrum were measured (quasi) contemporaneously. We note that no significant variation of the VHE ray flux was found in 1ES 0229+200 during its observed period of 2004 and in 1ES 1101-232(2005) .

Compare to the GEBL model, the GCA model spectra achieve a significant better fit to only three spectra, i.e. PKS 1424+240, 1ES 1101-232, and the former achieve a acceptable fit for each spectrum, see Fig. fig:4 and Fig. fig:5 .

sources.summary: The paper treats 1ES 1101-232 as an extreme TeV blazar whose SED can match hadronic-cascade models, while its point-like angular distribution disfavors that model.

quotes.quote: Indeed, some blazars such as 1ES 0229+200 Aharonian2007a,Aliu2014 , 1ES 1101-232 Aharonian2006,Aharonian2007b and 1ES 0347-121 Aharonian2007c may be classified as both ETBs (see Dzhatdoev2017 , Fig. 12, 14, 17) and EHBLs. Compared to well-known nearby blazars such as Mkn 501 and Mkn 421, ETBs, as a rule, reveal weak and slow variability in the high energy (HE, E > 100 MeV) and the very high energy (VHE, E > 100 GeV) spectral bands.

In particular, the Fermi-LAT Collaboration have recently performed a search for spatial extension in some high-latitude ( |b| > 5^ ) sources, including the blazars 1ES 1101-232 and 1ES 0347-121 Biteau2018 . Thus, the intergalactic hadronic cascade model for the blazars 1ES 1101-232 and 1ES 0347-121 predicts observable spectra similar to those measured by the H.E.S.S. Collaboration, but this model is not favoured in view of observed angular distributions typical for point-like sources.

sources.summary: The paper models 1ES 1101--232 as a hard-spectrum TeV blazar whose emission can be fit by a one-zone SSC model with a high low-energy electron cutoff.

quotes.quote: Accepted 2006 January 31; Received 2005 November 16; in original form 2005 November 16 firstpage - lastpage 2005 Recent gamma--ray observations of the blazar 1ES 1101--232 (redshift z=0.186 ) reveal that the unabsorbed TeV spectrum is hard, with spectral index 0.5 [ F( ) ^ ] . We show that simple one--zone synchrotron self--Compton model can explain such hard spectra if we assume a power law energy distribution of the emitting electrons with a relatively high minimum energy. In this case the intrinsic TeV spectrum can be as hard as F( ) ^ 1/3 , while the predicted X--ray spectrum can still be much softer. The observations of 1ES 1101--232 can therefore be reconciled with relatively high intensities of the infrared background, even if some extreme background levels can indeed be excluded.

sources.summary: The paper identifies 1ES 1101-232 as a distant hard-spectrum TeV blazar whose spectrum gives the strongest individual optical-to-near-infrared EBL constraints.

quotes.quote: The most distant TeV blazar discovered so far with a published energy spectrum is 1ES 1101-232 at a redshift of z = 0.186 . Its spectrum is hard and ranges from 160 GeV to 3.3 TeV.

Distant source, hard spectrum: 1ES 1101-232 The spectrum from 1ES 1101-232 gives the strongest constraints for all individual spectra, even though slightly less EBL shapes (7 706 625, 95.57 of all shapes) are excluded than in the case of Mkn 501.
The strongest constraints result from the 1ES 1101-232 spectrum in the wavelength range from 0.8 and 10 m, mainly due to the hard spectrum and its large distance to earth.

sources.summary: The paper cites 1ES 1101-232 as a BL Lac TeV source whose hard spectrum constrains EBL interpretations and motivates the internal-absorption model.

quotes.quote: This condition is safely satisfied, given the constraints on the minimum EBL flux imposed by galaxy counts, for blazars with redshifts z 0.15 like 1ES 1101-232 and for nearby objects with z 0.03 like Mkn 501.

Namely, the HESS collaboration reported, based on the detection of TeV gamma-rays from the BL Lac object 1ES 1101-232, that any significant deviation from the lower limits of EBL determined by the integrated light of galaxies resolved by the Hubble telescope , would lead to very hard intrinsic gamma-ray spectrum with a slope characterized by a photon index _0 1.5 .

Thus, the gamma-ray observations of 1ES 1101-232 and 1ES 0229+200 can be interpreted as an argument that the galaxies resolved by the Hubble and Spitzer telescopes provide the bulk of the EBL flux from optical to mid infrared wavelengths.

sources.summary: The paper treats 1ES 1101-232 as one of four TeV blazars with adequate data, deriving lower UHECR power limits and small Auger South detection prospects.

quotes.quote: While for a 2.2 injection spectrum, the lower limits on the powers emitted in UHECRs by 1ES 0229+200, 1ES 1101-232 and 1ES 0347-121 are lower than their respective synchrotron luminosities ( 10^ 46 erg s ^ 1 ); in the case of 1ES 1426+428 it exceeds the corresponding synchrotron luminosity by up to an order of magnitude. Currently there are four such TeV blazars with adequate data, namely 1ES 1426+428 hess1426 , 1ES 0229+200 hess0229,veritas0229 , 1ES 1101-232 hess1101 and 1ES 0347-121 hess0347 . The detection prospect for UHECRs with = 2.2 from the other 3 blazars within the field-of-view of the Auger South is much smaller, N_ evt = 0.04 , 0.03 and 0.02 respectively for 1ES 0229+200 ( = +20.27^ ), 1ES 1101-232 ( = -23.50^ ) and 1ES 0347-121 ( = -11.98^ ); for L_p = 10^ 45 erg/s and E = 9,000 km ^2 yr sr.

sources.summary: The paper studies 1ES 1101-232 as a hard-TeV blazar for which proton-synchrotron modeling yields allowed parameter space only at a large blob radius under observational constraints.

quotes.quote: Here we study two typical hard-TeV spectrum blazars, which are 1ES 0229+200 and 1ES 1101-232. In the _ D - B diagrams of Figs. 0229 and 1101 , under the observational constraints, it can be seen that only when setting R=1 10^ 17 cm the parameter space can be found. Especially for 1ES 0229+200, its parameter space is restricted strictly. In the modeling, the high-energy hump is dominated by the proton synchrotron radiation, and the SSC radiation of electrons can be ignored, since electrons mainly lose energy through the synchrotron radiation, as shown by the cooling timescales in Figs. 0229 and 1101 . Please note that, the UV data points of 1ES 0229+200 are suggested as the emission from the host galaxy , therefore we do not fit them with the jet model.

gmw_00000525.name: 1ES 1727+502
gmw_00000525.overview: 1ES 1727+502 is a high-frequency-peaked BL Lacertae blazar [arXiv:1308.3498, arXiv:1807.02926] located at a redshift of z = 0.055 [arXiv:1308.3498] within a bright host galaxy [arXiv:1308.3498]. It hosts a central supermassive black hole [arXiv:1807.02926] and features a radio jet structure [arXiv:0711.1158] that exhibits long-term multiband variability [arXiv:2206.12506].

sources.summary: The paper reports 1ES 1727+502 as a z=0.055 high-frequency-peaked BL Lac detected by MAGIC at very high energies, with optical monitoring accounting for a bright host galaxy.

quotes.quote: The BL Lac 1ES 1727+502 is a blazar, an active galactic nuclei (AGN) with a relativistic jet pointing towards the observer. Currently there are about 50 very high energy (VHE, E > 100 GeV) ray emitting blazars http://tevcat.uchicago.edu/ , constituting the most numerous class of extragalactic sources in this energy range. 1ES 1727+502, located at a redshift z = 0.055 , was observed with the Whipple 10-m ray telescope and with the MAGIC-I telescope , without leading to a significant detection. It is also a source of high energy (HE, 300 MeV < E < 100 GeV) rays.

KVA optical observations 1ES 1727+502 has been observed continually in the optical R band as part of the Tuorla blazar monitoring program http://users.utu.fi/kani/ for more than ten years, starting from 2002. The observations were carried out with the 1 m Tuorla telescope and 35 cm KVA telescope in La Palma. The brightness of the object was inferred from calibration stars in the same CCD-frames as 1ES 1727+502 using differential photometry and comparison star magnitudes from . The magnitudes are converted to fluxes using the standard formula and values from . 1ES 1727+502 has a bright host galaxy, contributing >50 to the flux in the optical R band .

To derive the F _ in the optical band, this contribution is subtracted from the measured flux and in addition the brightness was corrected for galactic absorption by R =0.079 mag . The average F _ during the MAGIC observations corresponds to (4.93 0.2) 10^ 12 erg cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 .

sources.summary: The paper describes OT 546 as a nearby HBL BL Lac object with a massive black hole, stable long monitoring, small flares, and strong BWB color behavior.

quotes.quote: OT 546 was first noticed by 1966ApJ...143..192Z and was classified as a BL Lac object by 1980ARA&A..18..321A . The redshift of z = 0.0554 0.0003 was measured by 1978ApJ...219L..97O . It was a nearby HBL 2015ApJ...808..110A and was discovered as a very high energy (VHE) source by MAGIC 2014A&A...563A..90A . OT 546 was also found to have a massive black hole 5.4 10^8 M_ at its center 1995Ap&SS.229..157F .

The monitoring of OT 546 can be divided into two phases. Phase 1 is from JDs 2453444 to 2453919. The object was observed on 55, 54, and 52 nights in the e , i , and m bands, respectively. Phase 2 is from JDs 2454134 to 2455713. The object was observed on 113, 121, and 110 nights respectively in the , i , and o bands. The brightness of this object was quite stable during our monitoring period.

sources.summary: The paper identifies 1ES 1727+502 as a LHAASO-detected HSP blazar and models its averaged multi-wavelength SED during a low, non-flaring state.

quotes.quote: Recently, the Large High Altitude Air Shower Observatory (LHAASO) collaboration present the first LHAASO catalog of VHE ray sources, five of which are AGNs 2023arXiv230517030C . These five AGNs include four high-synchrotron-peaked HSP; 2010ApJ...710.1271A blazars, which are Mrk 421, Mrk 501, 1ES 1727+502 and 1ES 2344+514, and a Liner-type AGN named NGC 4278.

In this section, we present the multi-wavelength observations of Mrk 421, Mrk 501, 1ES 1727+502, 1ES 2344+514, and NGC 4278 from 2021 March 5 to 2022 September 30 during the operation of the Water cherenkov Detector Array (WCDA) of LHAASO and the processes of data reduction. The detailed information of these five LHAASO AGNs is given in Table  tabel:agn .

These five LHAASO AGNs do not show significant flare during the same observation period of LHAASO in all bands, as shown in Fig. fig:lc . Therefore, we use the averaged flux of each band to construct SEDs. As mentioned above, the first LHAASO catalog report five AGNs including four HSP blazars and one Liner-type AGN.

sources.summary: The paper reports MAGIC observations detecting VHE gamma-ray emission from the BL Lac 1ES 1727+502 and models its multiwavelength SED without finding short-term variability.

quotes.quote: Motivated by the Costamante Ghisellini (2002) predictions we investigated if the blazar 1ES 1727+502 ( z=0.055) is emitting very high energy (VHE, E > 100 GeV) rays. We observed the BL Lac object 1ES 1727+502 in stereoscopic mode with the two MAGIC telescopes during 14 nights between May 6th and June 10th 2011, for a total effective observing time of 12.6 hours. For the study of the multiwavelength spectral energy distribution (SED) we use simultaneous optical R band data from the KVA telescope, archival UV/optical and X-ray observations by instruments UVOT and XRT on board of the Swift satellite and high energy (HE, 0.1 GeV - 100 GeV) -ray data from the Fermi LAT instrument. We detect, for the first time, VHE -ray emission from 1ES 1727+502 at a statistical significance of 5.5 .

The integral flux above 150 GeV is estimated to be (2.1 0.4) of the Crab Nebula flux and the de-absorbed VHE spectrum has a photon index of (2.7 0.5) . No significant short-term variability was found in any of the wavebands presented here.

sources.summary: The paper studies 1ES 1727+502 as a BL Lac with a low-flux VHE night, no preferred log-parabola spectrum, stationary MOJAVE components, and radio-optical correlation behavior.

quotes.quote: For 1ES 1727+502, there is one night (MJD 57309, 2015 October 14) when the VHE gamma-ray flux was 52 of the average flux. The VHE gamma-ray flux during MJD 57309 was 3.3 away from the average flux computed using all of the five nights of observation. However, the VHE gamma-ray spectrum could not be computed using the observations of this single night.

1ES 1727+502 has been observed five times between 2013 and 2015. 2018ApJ...853...68P fitted four components to the MOJAVE data of 1ES 1727+502 and they were all consistent with no motion ( 2019ApJ...874...43L reports 0.041 0.043 as maximum jet speed). The polarisation of the core is not significant, and therefore the EVPA cannot be derived.

sources.summary: The paper studies long-term multiband variability of the TeV blazar 1ES 1727+502 and fits its optical-UV to very-high-energy spectrum with a one-zone SSC model.

quotes.quote: In this work, we studied the broadband temporal and spectral properties of a TeV blazar 1ES 1727+502 and explore the one-zone synchrotron-self Compton (SSC) model to fit the broadband spectral energy distribution (SED). We collected the long-term (2014-2021) multiband data which includes both the low and high flux states of the source. The entire light curve is divided into three segments of different flux states and the best-fit parameters obtained by broadband SED modeling corresponding to three flux states were then compared.

Here, we have used one-zone SSC modeling to explain the broadband SED for all the three segments. For the SED modeling we have used the specific period from each segment as marked with navy blue color patch in Figure fig3 . The period chosen for SED modeling during Segment-1 2 is based on high X-ray flux state while in Segment-3, it is chosen for high optical-UV flux state, in order to compare their fitted SED parameters.

sources.summary: The paper describes 1727+502 as a candidate TeV BL Lac with a northwest kpc jet/halo, prior VLBP polarization, and new spine-sheath-like polarization structure.

quotes.quote: 1727+502 1727+502 is a candidate TeV BL Lac Costamante02 . The kpc-scale radio structure consists of an unresolved core and diffuse, asymmetrical halo, with evidence for a jet emerging to the northwest WardleMoore84,Giroletti04a . The previous 5 GHz VLBP image of Kollgaard96 show a jet extending to the northwest, roughly aligned with the kpc-scale structure. Polarization was detected in several jet components, with the implied B field being either transverse or lontudinal in the inner jet, depending on interpretation, then becoming clearly longitudinal further from the core. Our new VLBI images (Fig.  fig:1727 ) clearly show regions of polarization corresponding to longitudinal B fields offset from the jet ridge line, forming a sort of `sheath' of polarization.

sources.summary: The paper describes 1ES 1727+502 as a VHE BL Lac source in its monitoring sample and reports its optical/radio flux level, variability trends, and correlation behavior.

quotes.quote: 1ES 1727+502 : The source is rather weak with mean flux density below the median flux density of the sample in both optical and radio bands. Both light curves show significant increasing flux density trend throughout the period. There are two major optical outburst in the source, around MJD 55350 and MJD 56300, which are visible also in the radio light curve, and DCF finds a significant correlation between the two bands (see Fig. Fig:lc26 ). At the time of the detection of VHE rays from the source Alek14c , there is no flare in optical or radio.

sources.summary: The paper describes a 2015 outburst in 1ES 1727+502, lower peak luminosity in a low-flux state, weak curvature increase, and higher 2015 luminosity than 2017.

quotes.quote: 1ES 1727+502 showed its peak luminosity increasing from 10 ^ 44 to 10 ^ 44.5 erg \ s ^ 1 or even higher, and peak energy increasing from 0.8 to 3.5 keV during a large outburst in 2015. In the relatively low-flux state, its peak luminosity decreased to 10 ^ 43.5 erg \ s ^ 1 . From 2015 to 2017, its curvature had a very weak increase. It had a similar peak energy range in 2015 and 2017, but had a larger peak luminosity in 2015 than that in 2017.

sources.summary: The paper identifies BZB J1728+5013 as a TeV BL Lac candidate found in several previous selections, previously studied through X-ray spectral curvature, and regarded as the most promising candidate.

quotes.quote: We note that 2 sources appear in Stecker et al. (1996) list of candidates and 8 in that of Costamante Ghisellini (2002), with 2 objects, BZB J0326+0225 and BZB J1728+5013, present in both selections. In Massaro et al. (2011c) we also propose a X-ray based selection and BZB J0326+0225, BZB J1136+6737, BZB J1417+2543, and BZB J1728+5013, were deeply investigated and selected as TBCs on the basis of their X-ray spectral curvature. In particular, BZB J1728+5013 is present in all the previous selections with the only exception of Tavecchio et al. (2010), making it the most promising TBC.

gmw_00000526.name: 1ES 2344+514
gmw_00000526.overview: 1ES 2344+514 is a high-synchrotron-peaked BL Lacertae object [arXiv:0704.3533, arXiv:2308.10200] located at a redshift of z = 0.044 [arXiv:0704.3533, arXiv:1109.6808]. The active galactic nucleus features a compact radio core with a straight southeast jet [arXiv:1009.2269] and exhibits flux variability across the infrared [arXiv:0704.3533], X-ray [arXiv:1109.6808], and very-high-energy gamma-ray bands [arXiv:1908.10089].

sources.summary: The paper reports TACTIC and MIRO observations of the BL Lac 1ES2344+514, finding no significant TeV signal but near-infrared variability and a compact emitting region.

quotes.quote: We have observed the BL Lac object 1ES2344+514 (z = 0.044) in Very High Energy (VHE) gamma-ray and near-infrared wavelength bands with TACTIC and MIRO telescopes respectively. The observations were made from 18th October to 9th December 2004 and 27th October 2005 to 1st January 2006. Detailed analysis of the TACTIC data indicates absence of a statistically significant gamma-ray signal both in overall data and on a nightly basis from the source direction.

During 2004 IR observations, 1ES2344+514 shows low level ( 0.06 magnitude) day-to-day variation in both, J H bands. However, during 2005 observation epoch, the source brightens up by about 0.41 magnitude from its October 2005 level J magnitude = 12.64 to J = 12.23 on December 6, 2005. It then fades by about 0.2 magnitude during 6 to 10 December, 2005. The variation is seen in both, J H, bands simultaneously. The light travel time arguments suggest that the emission region size is of the order of 10^ 17 cms.

sources.summary: The paper describes 1ES 2344+514 as a faint HBL/VHE source and reports campaign variability in radio and X-rays, including a steep Swift XRT flux increase.

quotes.quote: 1ES 2344+514, an HBL located at a redshift of z = 0.044, has been discovered in the VHE regime by Whipple in 1995 . Though being the third extragalactic VHE source to be detected, due to its faintness only one MW campaign including a VHE detection has been published up to now . The source is listed in the Fermi LAT 1-year Catalog , but EGRET did not detect the source . In 2000, BeppoSAX reported strong flux changes on time scales of 5000 s and spectral changes in X-rays. During the highest flux state, the synchrotron peak was detected at energies > 1 keV for this object , adding it to the small but recently growing list of so-called 'extreme blazars' .

The light curve of 1ES 2344+514 (see Figure fig:LC_2344 ) showed significant variability at radio (but IRAM) and X-ray wavelengths, whereas in the optical and VHE, variability could not be established (constant flux fit: ^2 / d.o.f. = 13.1/29 and 5.5/14, respectively). Significant variability was also not apparent in the Fermi LAT 1-year Catalog public light curve. The Swift XRT measurements showed a steep flux increase by 50 within 2 days, followed by a rather slow decline, halving the flux in about 8 days.

sources.summary: The paper reports an August 2016 multiwavelength flare of 1ES 2344+51.4, with strong VHE detection, daily-scale variability, and flux near the historical Whipple maximum.

quotes.quote: In this work, we report multiwavelength observations of 1ES 2344+51.4 during an intense flaring state that occurred in August 2016. The flare is characterized by hard VHE and X-ray spectra that we interpret as a renewal of extreme behaviour in these two energy bands. 1ES 2344+51.4 is a nearby blazar located at a redshift of z=0.044 , and it is among the first extragalactic sources detected in the VHE band.

MAGIC observed 1ES 2344+51.4 for two consecutive nights, MJD 57611 MJD 57612 (August 11 and August 12 2016), for a total of 1.1 hr, after quality cuts. A strong detection, significant at the 12 level, was obtained.
We report multiwavelength observations of a strong flaring episode of 1ES 2344+51.4. The VHE flux was around 0.55 C.U. above 300 GeV, comparable to the historical maximum observed by Whipple in 1995.

sources.summary: The paper studies the BL Lac 1ES 2344+514 during a 2016 flare, using multiwavelength data to characterize its SED, inverse-Compton peak, VHE spectrum, and renewed extreme synchrotron state.

quotes.quote: The TeV detected BL Lac 1ES 2344+514 belongs to the latter group. So far, this object showed a _ s significantly above 10^ 17 Hz only during flaring state, as reported by 2000MNRAS.317..743G . Located at a redshift of z=0.044 1996ApJS..104..251P , 1ES 2344+514 was discovered by the Einstein Slew Survey 1992ApJS...80..257E in the 0.2 keV--4 keV energy range. The first detection in VHE rays was obtained in 1995 by the Whipple 10 m telescope during an intense flare, with a flux corresponding to 60 of the Crab Nebula flux above 350 GeV 1998ApJ...501..616C,2005ApJ...634..947S . In 1996, 1ES 2344+514 showed a very variable behaviour in the X-ray band 2000MNRAS.317..743G on a timescale of approximately 5 ks when the source was at its brightest state: impressive rapid changes of the X-ray spectrum slope, together with a large shift by a factor of 30 or more of _ s , put this source for the first time in the EHBL family.

In August 2016, 1ES 2344+514 was detected with the ground-based ray telescope FACT during a high ray state, triggering multi-wavelength (MWL) observations. We studied the MWL light curves of 1ES 2344+514 during the 2016 flaring state, using data from radio to VHE rays taken with OVRO, KAIT, KVA, NOT, some telescopes of the GASP-WEBT collaboration at the Teide, Crimean, and St. Petersburg observatories, Swift UVOT, Swift XRT, Fermi LAT, FACT and MAGIC. With simultaneous observations of the flare, we built the broadband SED and studied it in the framework of a leptonic and an hadronic model.

sources.summary: The paper finds 1ES 2344+514 has weak optical QPO hints near 5-5.5 years and a possible 1.2-year modulation, but available V, polarized, gamma, and radio coverage is insufficient.

quotes.quote: 1ES 2344+514 The REDFIT and PDM analyses show a possible QPO of 5 years in both the R and V optical bands, as well as the optical polarized emission of 1ES 2344+514. No hint or presence of quasi-periodicity is observed in the first analysis step in the ray emission. Moreover, there are no radio data available for this object to test the quasi-periodicity in this band (see Figure 1es2344_lcs ). The optical V-band and optical polarized LCs extend for roughly 10 years, approximately twice the value of the possible period. Therefore, extracting a reliable conclusion with such short time coverage is not possible in these bands.

The R-band emission of 1ES 2344+514 presents a weak ( 2 ) QPO hint with a value of 5.5 years. Moreover, a possible faster period ( 1.2 years) is also reflected in the R-band LS periodogram and WWZ, indicating a second possible quasi- periodic modulation. The optical V and polarized bands, as well as the HE ray band, do not have the temporal coverage needed to reliably test this period.

sources.summary: The paper describes 1ES2344+514 as a TeV AGN reported from 1995 observations, highly variable in hard X-rays, with HBL-like behavior and later confirmations.

quotes.quote: 1ES2344+514: This AGN was reported by the Whipple group in 1998 as a TeV source based on observations made in 1995. Most of the reported signal came in one night so, if real, the source is highly variable. Indeed, Beppo SAX observations of 1ES2344+514 have revealed it to be highly variable at hard X-ray energies with the overall X-ray spectral shape varying with intensity. Its synchrotron peak frequency was seen to shift by a factor of 30 between observations taken in 1996 and in 1998. This behaviour is typical of HBLs and has, for example, been also observed in Mrk 501 .
A confirmation of 1ES2344+514 has been reported by the HEGRA group and by the Whipple group . No spectrum has been reported. At the peak of its flaring activity it was 60 of the strength of the Crab.

sources.summary: The paper identifies 1ES 2344+514 as a BL Lac at redshift 0.044, describes host-galaxy optical and infrared contributions, and reports variable X-ray and VHE gamma-ray emission including a strong 1995 flare.

quotes.quote: 1ES 2344 was identified as a BL Lac object in BLLacsFromEinsteinSS_Perlman96 . The non-contemporaneous spectral energy distribution of 1ES 2344 is shown in Figure fig:1es2344_sed . Observations at all wavelengths show 1ES 2344 to be an unresolved point source. The central black-hole mass is 10^ 8.80 0.16 M _ , derived from stellar velocity dispersion measurements Barth03_Black_hole_masses .

In the optical regime, a point source with an underlying elliptical host galaxy can be fitted with a radius (half-width at half-maximum) of r_e=7.12 0.02 kpc ( H_0 =50 km s ^ 1 Mpc ^ 1 and q_0 =0) HST2000 . The optical and far-infrared emission from 1ES 2344 contains significant contributions from the host galaxy. The total photometry by the 2 Micron All Sky Survey 2MASS_Jarrett03 and by the Hubble Space Telescope (HST) HST2000 , labeled "Galaxy light" in Figure fig:1es2344_sed , lie well above the value expected from pure synchrotron emission in the jet.
In the VHE band, the object was observed in a flaring state during the night of 20 December, 1995, with a significance of 5.3 , the strongest gamma-ray flare measured from this object to date.

sources.summary: MAGIC confirmed 1ES 2344+514 as a BL Lac VHE source in a quiescent state, measuring a clear excess, a low Crab-level flux, and a soft power-law spectrum.

quotes.quote: 1ES 2344+514 was identified as a BL Lac object by , who also determined a redshift of z=0.044 from absorption line measurements, while no evident emission lines were found. In VHE rays, the source was observed by the Whipple collaboration during the 1995/96 observing season, yielding a signal on the 5.8 level . Over 1/3 of the measured excess was recorded during the night of December 20, 1995, with corresponding photon flux above 350 GeV of 64 of the Crab nebula flux. The VHE ray emission of 1ES 2344+514 was confirmed on a significance level of 4.4 by the HEGRA collaboration , who found a quiescent flux level approximately 50 times lower than during the 1995 flare.

The MAGIC observation of 1ES 2344+514 (Table tab:1 ) yielded a clear excess with the significance of 11.5 . The measured flux corresponds to 6 of the Crab Nebula flux. No strong evidence for flux variability on times scales of days or weeks was found. The differential energy spectrum can be fitted by a simple power law with a photon index of 2.96 0.12 .

sources.summary: MAGIC observed 1ES 2344+514 as a BL Lac AGN in a low very-high-energy gamma-ray state, confirming earlier detections and measuring a steep TeV spectrum without significant variability.

quotes.quote: The MAGIC collaboration has observed very high energy gamma ray emission from the AGN 1ES 2344+514. A gamma-ray signal corresponding to an 11 excess and an integral flux of (2.38 0.30_ stat 0.70_ syst ) 10^ 11 cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 above 200 GeV has been obtained from 23.1 hours of data taking between 2005 August 3 and 2006 January 1. The data confirm the previously detected gamma-ray emission from this object during a flare seen by the Whipple collaboration in 1995 and the evidence (below 5 significance level) from long-term observations conducted by the Whipple and HEGRA groups. The MAGIC observations show a relatively steep differential photon spectrum that can be described by a power law with a photon index of =-2.95 0.12_ stat 0.2_ syst between 140 GeV and 5.4 TeV. The observations reveal a low flux state, about six times below the 1995 flare seen by Whipple and comparable with the previous Whipple and HEGRA long-term measurements.

A good candidate for detailed studies is 1ES 2344+514. This AGN belongs to a type of blazars in which the synchrotron emission peaks at UV/X-ray frequencies (the so-called high-energy peak BL Lacs [ HBLs ] e.g. Urry Padovani 1995), as opposed to the blazars with the synchrotron peak located at IR/visible frequencies. Along with Mkn 501 and H 1426+428, it represents extreme BL Lac objects, in which the synchrotron peak energy exceeds 10 keV, in particular during strong flares Costamante .

sources.summary: The paper reports first 43 GHz VLBA images of 1ES 2344+514, finding a compact weak core, straight southeast jet, new inner component, and low core polarization.

quotes.quote: 1ES 2344+514 Figure 7 shows the 43 GHz images of 1ES 2344+514 from 2008 and 2009. At the distance of 1ES 2344+514 ( z=0.044 ), the linear scale of the images is 0.9 pc mas ^ 1 . These are the first VLBA images of this source at 43 GHz, and thus they are the highest resolution images obtained for this source to date (by a factor of about three, since we had not previously observed this source at frequencies higher than 15 GHz).

This source is at the VLBA's detection limit at 43 GHz, with a data rate of 512 Mbps, and has a total compact flux of only about 50 mJy at this frequency. The images in Figure 7 show a compact core with a flux of about 40 mJy, and a jet extending to the southeast, in the direction of the jet seen at 15 GHz (Piner Edwards 2004). The jet in this source is fairly straight at these scales. The model fitting at 43 GHz shows jet component C3 at about 0.7 mas from the core, and a new component, C4, at 0.1 to 0.2 mas from the core.

sources.summary: The paper identifies 1ES 2344+514 as a LHAASO-detected HSP blazar and models its averaged multi-wavelength SED during a low, non-flaring state.

quotes.quote: Recently, the Large High Altitude Air Shower Observatory (LHAASO) collaboration present the first LHAASO catalog of VHE ray sources, five of which are AGNs 2023arXiv230517030C . These five AGNs include four high-synchrotron-peaked HSP; 2010ApJ...710.1271A blazars, which are Mrk 421, Mrk 501, 1ES 1727+502 and 1ES 2344+514, and a Liner-type AGN named NGC 4278.

In this section, we present the multi-wavelength observations of Mrk 421, Mrk 501, 1ES 1727+502, 1ES 2344+514, and NGC 4278 from 2021 March 5 to 2022 September 30 during the operation of the Water cherenkov Detector Array (WCDA) of LHAASO and the processes of data reduction. The detailed information of these five LHAASO AGNs is given in Table  tabel:agn .

These five LHAASO AGNs do not show significant flare during the same observation period of LHAASO in all bands, as shown in Fig. fig:lc . Therefore, we use the averaged flux of each band to construct SEDs. As mentioned above, the first LHAASO catalog report five AGNs including four HSP blazars and one Liner-type AGN.

sources.summary: The paper discusses 1ES 2344+514 as a BL Lac with daily VHE variability, similar spectral shapes across states, slow MOJAVE jet motion, and weak core polarization.

quotes.quote: 1ES 2344+514 showed variability on daily timescale but no shorter variability timescale was detected in the VHE gamma-ray band. 2344fact performed a detailed analysis on different emission states of this source and found the spectral shape to be similar during different observational epochs despite different levels of the VHE gamma-ray flux. The results of the VHE gamma-ray flux study of our sample are summarised in Table tab_magic_flux .

1ES 2344+514 has been observed 3 times between 2013 and 2018. 2016AJ....152...12L reported one component with _ app =0.055 0.036 and the most recent measurements are in line with this result _ app =0.037 0.012 , 2019ApJ...874...43L . The polarisation of the core is not significant 2018ApJ...862..151H , while the jet position angle is 130-145 2004ApJ...600..115P .

sources.summary: The paper reproduces the first low-state simultaneous SED of 1ES 2344+514 with a pp model, where pp interactions improve the VHE fit and exceed WCDA sensitivity.

quotes.quote: 1ES 2344+514 1ES 2344+514 is the third extragalactic source discovered at VHE band after Mrk 421 and Mrk 501 1998ApJ...501..616C . The day-scale flares in VHE band are usually detected 1998ApJ...501..616C, 2011ApJ...738..169A . The first low-state simultaneous radio to VHE observations of 1ES 2344+514 is presented by Ref.  2013AA...556A..67A .

In Figure  2344 , we reproduce its SED with the one-zone pp model. In our modeling, the multi-wavelength emission is dominated by the leptonic emission from primary electrons. The emission from pp interactions has a significant contribution to the highest energy data point, which improves the fitting of VHE spectrum and exceed the one-year sensitivity of WCDA of LHAASO.

sources.summary: The paper uses 1ES 2344+514 VHE detections, MAGIC and VERITAS/Swift campaigns, Fermi/LAT constraints, and variability timescales to model low and high SED states.

quotes.quote: 1ES 2344+514. The third BL Lac object was detected at the VHE band with Whipple. The detection of VHE emission mostly came from an apparent flare on 1995 December and the average flux above 350 GeV was (6.6 1.9) 10^ 11 cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 , 63 of the Crab flux (Catanese et al. 1998). The observation of MAGIC between 2005 August 3 and 2006 January 1 presented a steep spectrum with photon index =-2.95 and a flux 6 times below the 1995 flare, indicating that the source was in low state (Albert et al. 2007b). No evidence for variability was found during the MAGIC observations.

sources.summary: The paper describes 1ES 2344+514 as a VHE BL Lac source in its monitoring sample and reports its optical/radio flux level, variability trends, and correlation behavior.

quotes.quote: 1ES 2344+514 : The radio flux density is close to median for this sample, while the optical flux density is one of the faintest in our sample. The visual inspection of the radio light curve shows slowly increasing flux density starting MJD 54850 and continuing for 1000 days. The same increasing trend is visually present also in the optical light curve (see Fig. Fig:lc32 ). The trend analysis shows a highly significant trend in both light curves and the DCF shows a significant correlation with optical leading by 70 days.

sources.summary: The paper reports a December 2007 outburst in 1ES 2344+514, with high peak luminosity and energy, lower 2009-2010 states, and little curvature change.

quotes.quote: 1ES 2344+514 underwent a large outburst in December 2007, and the observation with the highest peak luminosity and peak energy in the \ plot corresponds to the peak position of this flare. During this period, its peak luminosity reached 10 ^ 44.6 erg \ s ^ 1 and peak energy reached 8 keV. The three observations between 2009 and 2010 represented the low-flux states, whose peak luminosity decreased to 10 ^ 43.3 erg \ s ^ 1 and peak energy decreased to 1 keV. In addition, its curvature displayed no significant changes.

sources.summary: The paper gives 1ES 2344+514 an individual blazar variability note, summarizing TeV-band detections, GeV or hard-X-ray behavior, and variability classification.

quotes.quote: 1ES 2344+514 : This source was sporadically detected in the TeV band light curve and no significant flux variability was noticed (Table tab:basic_info ). In the GeV band also, it has exhibited a moderate level of flux variations. This source has been detected several times with various Cherenkov telescopes 2013A&A...556A..67A,2017MNRAS.471.2117A,2020MNRAS.496.3912M . A comparison of the TeV and hard X-ray bands light curves indicated no significant flux variability.

sources.summary: The paper lists 1ES 2344+514 as an unconfirmed TeV AGN detection, places it at redshift around 0.03, and says it was found during a flare.

quotes.quote: There are also several unconfirmed AGN detections: 1ES 2344+514 and PKS 2155-304 .
Mrk421, Mrk501, and 1ES 2344+514 are relatively close with redshifts of order 0.03, while PKS 2155-304 lies at a redshift of 0.117.
The AGN 1ES 2344+514 was discovered during an apparent flare, however the quiescent emission is too low to monitor the long-term behavior of this source .

gmw_00000527.name: 1ES 1959+650
gmw_00000527.overview: 1ES 1959+650 is a nearby high-frequency-peaked BL Lacertae object [arXiv:1807.02926, arXiv:2002.00129] hosted by an elliptical galaxy with a disk and an absorption dust lane [arXiv:0801.4029]. The active galactic nucleus features a compact radio jet [arXiv:1009.2269] and exhibits multiwavelength variability, including very-high-energy gamma-ray flares [arXiv:2002.00129].

sources.summary: The paper describes 1ES 1959+650 as an interesting VHE source because of its orphan TeV flare, observed by MAGIC since 2004 and monitored in a faint low state.

quotes.quote: The observation of an ''orphan'' TeV flare (i.e. not accompanied by a corresponding X-ray flare) in 2002 made 1ES 1959+650 one of the most interesting VHE ray sources. MAGIC has been observing this object since 2004 , a regular monitoring was first performed in the 2005/2006 season . Unfortunately, the source position allows observations only under a relatively high zenith angles (35-50 deg). Moreover, when not in a high state the source is rather faint compared to those discussed above.

MAGIC observed 1ES 1959+650 from April 2007 till October 2008 for 27 hours (including 13 hours of data taken during moonlight or twilight). Observations were performed in wobble mode. After rejection of data due to poor quality 3.4 hours of data taken in 2008 were analyzed. The overall significance of this sample is 2.5 , which allows us to set an upper limit on the flux above 300 GeV of 2.53 10^ 11 ph/cm ^2 /s at 90 C.L.

sources.summary: MAGIC monitored 1ES 1959+650 for 27 hours in 2007-2008, detecting a clear 2007 ray signal but low mean flux, no major flux changes, and a usual quiescent state.

quotes.quote: MAGIC monitored 1ES 1959+650 from April 2007 till October 2008 for 27 hours under large zenith angle (35 ^ 50 ^ ) conditions. The source was observed in wobble mode. After quality selection mainly based on bad atmospheric conditions and unstable, low event rates, 12.7 hours of data taken in 2007 and 3.6 hours of data taken in 2008 were analyzed. In 2007 a clear ray signal of 9.0 is seen. However, the mean 1ES1959+650 ray flux above 300 GeV during this observations period with MAGIC was only (0.92 0.12) 10^ 11 ph/cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 , i.e. 10 Crab.

The light curve indicates no major changes of the flux level and no significant flares. The overall significance of the data sample from 2008 is 2.6 , which allows us to set an upper limit on the flux above 300 GeV of 1.54 10^ 11 ph/cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 at 90 C.L. In comparison to previous observations it can be concluded that MAGIC observed 1ES1959+650 in 2007/2008 during its usual quiescent state.

sources.summary: The paper reports first 43 GHz VLBA images of 1ES 1959+650, resolving a compact core, southeast jet, new component, and spine-sheath polarization structure.

quotes.quote: 1ES 1959+650 Figure 5 shows the 43 GHz images of 1ES 1959+650 from 2008 and 2009. At the distance of 1ES 1959+650 ( z=0.047 ), the linear scale of the images is 0.9 pc mas ^ 1 . These are the first VLBA images of this source at 43 GHz, and thus they are the highest resolution images obtained for this source to date.

The jet structure is well-represented by two circular Gaussians, designated C2 and C3, with C3 being a newly detected component about 0.2 mas from the core at a position angle of about 155 . The VLBA polarization image at 22 GHz from 2007 is shown in the collection of images in Figure 8. The polarization properties of the core and jet component C2 are given in Table pfittab . The core and component C2 are both about 4 polarized, and the EVPA is parallel to the jet at the location of C2. Although we do not detect any significant transverse jet structure in the total intensity images (Figure 5), this source does display the same spine-sheath structure in both EVPA and fractional polarization that is also displayed by Mrk 421 and Mrk 501 (easily noted around the location of C2 in Figure 8), that is analyzed in detail in transversepol .

sources.summary: The paper models 1ES 1959+650 as a high-peaked BL Lac object whose 2002 orphan TeV flare can be explained by hadronic interactions in the blazar jet.

quotes.quote: The 1ES 1959+650 is a high-peaked BL Lacertae object. On 4th of June, 2002, it exhibited a strong TeV flare without any low energy counterpart, providing for the first time an example of an orphan flare from a blazar. Observation of this orphan flare is in striking disagreement with the predictions of the leptonic models thus challenging the conventional synchrotron self-Compton (SSC) interpretation of the TeV emission.

The AGN 1ES 1959+650 was first detected in the Einstein IPC Slew Survey and classified as a HBL subclass, based on its X-ray to radio flux ratio . It has a redshift of z=0.047 with a luminosity distance of d_L=210 Mpc and the mass of the central black hole is estimated to be 1.5 10^8 M_ . Because of the HBL subclass and low redshift, it had long been considered a potential candidate for TeV ray source.

We have employed the hadronic model to interpret the TeV emission from the orphan flaring event of June 2002, from the blazar 1ES 1959+650. In this picture, Fermi-accelerated protons of energy 100 TeV interact with the low energy (1-8) MeV tail of the SSC photons in a very compact and confined region of the jet to produce resonance and its subsequent decay to photons through neutral pion decay.

sources.summary: The paper models the 2016 flare of the TeV blazar 1ES 1959+650, finding multiwavelength variability, correlated X-ray/gamma-ray behavior, spectral hardening, and two SSC emission zones.

quotes.quote: The nearby TeV blazar 1ES 1959+650 (z=0.047) was reported to be in flaring state during June - July 2016 by Fermi-LAT, FACT, MAGIC and VERITAS collaborations. We studied the spectral energy distributions (SEDs) in different states of the flare during MJD 57530 - 57589 using simultaneous multiwaveband data to understand the possible broadband emission scenario during the flare. The UV/optical and X-ray data from UVOT and XRT respectively on board Swift and high energy ray data from Fermi-LAT are used to generate multiwaveband lightcurves as well as to obtain high flux states and quiescent state SEDs.
A decent correlation is seen between X-ray and high energy ray fluxes. The spectral hardening with increase in the flux is seen in X-ray band.

Two zone SSC model satisfactorily fits the observed broadband SEDs. The inner zone is mainly responsible for producing synchrotron peak and high energy ray part of the SED in all states. The second zone is mainly required to produce less variable optical/UV and low energy ray emission.

sources.summary: The paper characterizes 1ES 1959+650 as a nearby TeV HBL/BL Lac with extreme 2016 VHE flares, hard X-ray/gamma spectra, rapid variability, and challenging emission models.

quotes.quote: 1ES 1959+650 is a bright TeV high-frequency-peaked BL Lac object exhibiting interesting features like ``orphan'' TeV flares and a broad emission in the high-energy regime, that are difficult to interpret using conventional one-zone Synchrotron Self-Compton (SSC) scenarios. We report the results from the Major Atmospheric Gamma Imaging Cherenkov (MAGIC) observations in 2016 along with the multi-wavelength data from the Fermi Large Area Telescope (LAT) and Swift instruments. MAGIC observed 1ES 1959+650 with different emission levels in the very-high-energy (VHE, E > 100 GeV) ray band during 2016. In the long-term data, the X-ray spectrum becomes harder with increasing flux and a hint of a similar trend is also visible in the VHE band. An exceptionally high VHE flux reaching 3 times the Crab Nebula flux was measured by MAGIC on the 13th, 14th of June and 1st July 2016 (the highest flux observed since 2002).

During these flares, the high-energy peak of the spectral energy distribution (SED) lies in the VHE domain and extends up to several TeV. The spectrum in the ray (both Fermi LAT and VHE bands) and the X-ray bands are quite hard. On 13th June and 1st July 2016, the source showed rapid variations of the VHE flux within timescales of less than an hour.

sources.summary: The paper studies the z=0.048 HSP blazar with Swift and XMM observations from 2018-2020, finding intra-day and long-term variability and significant low-amplitude XMM flux variability.

quotes.quote: 1ES 1959+650 is at a redshift of z=0.048 Perlman_1996 and is a prominent high synchrotron peaked blazar in which the synchrotron peak of the SED appears in the UV-X-ray band ( Krawczynski_2004, Kapanadze2016, Abdo_2010 ).

It has shown strong flux variability in optical, X-ray and TeV energy bands . High X-ray flaring activity of the source has been reported by and using XMM--Newton and RXTE-PCA observations in 2002--2003. reported frequent X-ray flares of this source during 2005--2014 using Swift--XRT observations. 1ES 1959+650 underwent an unprecedented X-ray flaring activity during August 2015--January 2016.

We observed the blazar 1ES 1959 + 650 during the period June 2018--December 2020 when the source showed different flux states (high/low) to study their flux and spectral variability on intra-day and long term timescales using SWIFT satellite. The source is studied for intra-day flux variability in total 125 nights and found significant variability in only five nights. We did not find any spectral variability during these observations. The source is studied using two observations of XMM--Newton held on 5th July 2019 and 16th July 2020 and found significant flux variability in both of these observations with low amplitude variation of 1.95 and 3.12 respectively.

sources.summary: The paper describes 1ES 1959+650 as a nearby HSP TeV blazar and uses AstroSat soft/hard X-ray light curves to show harder-when-brighter behavior, strong correlations, and zero-to-few-hour lags.

quotes.quote: 1ES 1959+650 is a relatively nearby ( z=0.048 ) HSP blazar perlman1996 . It was one of the first few blazars to be detected at the TeV energies catanese1998,nishiyama1999,horns03 . The most recent TeV observation has been by the MAGIC telescope magic20 .

We study simultaneous soft ( 0.7-7 keV) and hard ( 7-20 keV) X-ray light curves at a total of eight epochs during 2016-2019 of two TeV blazars Mrk 421 and 1ES 1959+650 observed by the SXT and LAXPC instruments onboard AstroSat . The light curves are 45-450 ks long and may be sampled with time bins as short as 600-800 sec with high signal to noise ratio. The blazars show a harder when brighter trend at all epochs. Discrete cross-correlation functions indicate that the hard and soft X-ray variability are strongly correlated. The time lag is consistent with zero in some epochs, and indicates hard or soft lag of a few hours in the rest.

sources.summary: The paper describes 1ES1959+650 as a variable TeV blazar with 2002 confirmations, strong flaring, optical/X-ray correlations, spectral states, and an orphan flare.

quotes.quote: 1ES1959+650: This was first reported in conference proceedings by the Telescope Array group operating in Dugway, Utah in 2000. Upper limits were reported by other groups . In 2002, the HEGRA Collaboration reported their detection of 1ES1959+650 . The detection was dramatically confirmed later in 2002 when an outburst was seen by several groups ; ; . Although the quiescent level was only about 5 of the Crab, when it flared its flux was 5 times that of the Crab.

Extensive multiwavelength observations on 1ES1959+650 were carried out during May-July 2002 . During this time, a TeV gamma-ray flare was observed which had no counterpart at X-ray energies. This ``orphan flare'' is difficult to explain in terms of one-zone synchrotron self-Compton (SSC) models.

sources.summary: The paper reviews 1ES 1959+650 as a VHE source with a prior orphan flare, then reports MAGIC detection during commissioning and a quiescent-state power-law spectrum down to 180 GeV.

quotes.quote: The Seven Telescope Array in Utah reported for the first time a VHE ray signal from 1ES 1959+650 in 1998 with the significance of 3.9 . In May 2002, when the X-ray flux of the source had significantly increased, both the Whipple and HEGRA collaborations subsequently confirmed 1ES 1959+650 as a VHE ray source. An interesting aspect of the source activity in 2002 was the observation of a so-called orphan flare , i.e. a VHE ray activity is observed in the absence of high activity in X-rays. Orphan flare in VHE rays are not expected within the SSC mechanism in relativistic jets.

1ES 1959+650 was observed by MAGIC during the co-missioning phase in 2004 (Table tab:1 ). The analysis of the data gave a detection of VHE rays on a significance of 8.2 . We obtained an integral VHE flux above 180 GeV of (3.73 0.41) 10 ^ 11 ph cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 . The energy spectrum between 180 GeV and 2 TeV is compatible with a power law with a photon index = 2.72 0.14 and is consistent with the quiescent state measured by HEGRA .

sources.summary: The paper uses multi-frequency VLBA observations of 1ES 1959+650 to identify its core and refine jet kinematics, measuring component C2 as stationary.

quotes.quote: The blazar 1ES 1959+650 was observed at three epochs at frequencies of 15 and 22 GHz. Spectral index information from these dual-frequency observations was used to definitively identify the core of the parsec-scale structure.

For 1ES 1959+650 and PKS 2155 304, the current observations are combined with the VLBA observations from our earlier paper to yield improved apparent speed measurements for these sources with greatly reduced measurement errors. The new apparent speed measured for component C2 in 1ES 1959+650 is 0.00c 0.04c (stationary), and the new apparent speed measured for component C1 in PKS 2155 304 is 0.93c 0.31c .

1ES 1959+650 was observed with the VLBA during three 6-hour sessions between 2003 June and 2004 February, under observation code BE030. The observations were split between 15.4 and 22.2 GHz, with approximately 2.5 hours spent on source at each frequency at each epoch (with approximately one hour of calibration scans and slewing). This source was observed at two frequencies in order to obtain spectral index information between the 15 and 22 GHz images, to help resolve potential ambiguities in the source structure discussed in Paper I.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes 1ES 1959+650 in the RXTE/ASM HBL sample, deriving its flux-distribution flare states and finding it flares more rarely, especially at high rates.

quotes.quote: We apply the MLBs for the first time on the long term X-ray light curves (RXTE/ASM) of Mkn 421, Mkn 501, 1ES 1959+650 and 1ES 2155-304, which consist of more than 10 years of observational data (1996-2007). Using the MLBs interpretation of RXTE/ASM data, the integrated time flux distribution is determined for each single source considered. We identify in these distributions the characteristic level as well as the flaring states of the blazars All the distributions show a significant component at negative flux values, most probably caused by an uncertainty in the background subtraction and by intrinsic fluctuations of RXTE/ASM.

The X-ray time behavior of Mkn 421, 1ES 1959 +650, Mkn 501 and 1ES 2155 304 has been characterized using approximately 10 years of data from RXTE/ASM. The characteristic level and flaring states have been defined and values are reported in Tab. tab:ASM flares . Mkn 421 is the source that flares most often amongst those studied: for 40 of the RXTE/ASM observations the source was in an active state (above R_ 1 ) and for 18 of these it was in a very high state (above R_ 3 ). The probability that a flare state is caused by a fluctuation of the instrumental noise is marginal. This confirms the well known fact that Mkn 421 is an extremely variable HBL and quantifies for the first time its duty cycle even if this is only valid for RXTE/ASM. Mkn 501 flares often at low rates ( 26 ) and less often at high rates ( 10 ). Also in this case, the intrinsic fluctuations do not significantly affect the flare states. 1ES 1959+650 flares more rarely in particular at high rates (only 2.6 ). The systematic component affects one tenth of flares.

sources.summary: The paper describes 1ES 1959+650 as a nearby BL Lac blazar with orphan TeV flares, modeling its flare spectrum through photohadronic interactions and estimating neutrino output.

quotes.quote: The blazar 1ES 1959+650, localized at a distance of D_z =210 Mpc (z=0.047), is one of the best studied high broad-line Lacertae objects (HBL) 2006A&A...455..773V,2014ApJ...797...89A . This BL Lac object has a central BH mass of 2 10^8 M_ 2003ApJ...595..624F . It was first detected in X-rays during the Slew Survey made by Einstein satellite's Imaging Proportional Counter 1992ApJS...80..257E .

In 2002 May, the X-ray flux increased significantly and both the Whipple 2003ApJ...583L...9H and High Energy Gamma Ray Astronomy (HEGRA) 2003A&A...406L...9A collaborations subsequently confirmed an increasing ray flux as well. An interesting aspect of the source activity in 2002 was the observation of a so-called "orphan" flare, recorded on June 4 by the Whipple Collaboration 2004ApJ...601..151K,2005ApJ...621..181D which was observed in rays without any increased activity in other wavelength simultaneously. Within the limited statistics, a pair of hours of observations showed the same spectral shape, the fit of a single power law dN/dE=N_0 (E/TeV)^ to the differential energy spectrum results in N_0=(7.4 1.3_ stat 0.9_ sys ) 10^ 11 TeV ^ 1 cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 and a spectral index of =2.83 0.14_ stat 0.08_ sys .
Using eq. ( pgamma_fit ), we found the best set of parameters for p interaction, as shown in Table 1.

sources.summary: The paper identifies 1ES 1959+650 as a z=0.047 HBL and TeV source, reporting multi-band monitoring with variability and no detected inter-band lag.

quotes.quote: 1ES 1959+650, with a redshift of z = 0.047 1993ApJ...412..541S , was classified as a HBL because its synchrotron bump peaked at UV--X-ray frequencies 2010ApJ...719L.162B . 2003ApJ...583L...9H found it to be a TeV emission source. The mass of its black hole was estimated to be 1.5 10^8 M_ 2002ApJ...569L..35F .
We observed this source in the , i , and o bands on 81, 81, and 71 nights, respectively, between 2009 July 03 and 2011 June 13.

The variation amplitude tends to decrease with decreasing frequency (see Table observations ). This is consistent with the result of 2000ApJ...537..101F . The correlation analysis did not find any inter-band time lag at long and short timescales (see Table alldelay ). No systematic spectral variability was detected in the second WEBT campaign covered the autumn and winter of 2007-2008 2009ApJ...694..174B . It can be seen from Figure fig:all , the spectral index became smaller (the spectrum became flatter) when the brightness of the source increased. One can see from Figure fig:allcolor that 3C 66A shows significant BWB trends (p 0.05) in all three colors (see Table color ), which is in accord with the previous results mentioned above. PKS 0735+178 PKS 0735+178 was first classified as a BL Lac object by 1974ApJ...190L.101C . The broadband spectrum suggested that it is a LBL 1996ApJ...463..424U . 2012A&A...547A...1N derived a redshift of 0.45 0.06 for this object by using the host galaxy as a standard candle. Historical optical data over a century show its violent long-term variations with an amplitude of 3-4 mags 1979AJ.....84.1658P,1997A&AS..125..525F . It probably has periods of 4.89 and 14.2 years 1988AJ.....95..374W,1997A&AS..125..525F . 2007A&A...467..465C found 3 characteristic timescales of about 4.5, 8.5 and 11-13 years. For short timescales, some IDV and inter-day variations were reported in its optical history. However, 2009MNRAS.399.1622G reported an unusual quiescence of IDV between 1998 and 2008. Optical variations of this source often show larger amplitudes than those in the infrared 2000ApJ...537..101F , which indicates a BWB trend. We observed this source on 13, 15, and 10 nights in the , i , and o bands, respectively, between 2009 December 18 and 2010 November 30. The recorded maximum brightness of 15.819 mags in the i band occurred on 2010 January 10 (JD 2455207). The brightness dropped by 0.58 mags at the end. The variation amplitude decreases in the wavelength sequence of , i , and o (see Table observations ). No IDV was detected because of the low sampling. As can be seen in Figure fig:all , the spectral index varied slightly between 1.1 and 1.8. One can see from Figure fig:allcolor , PKS 0735+178 exhibits different levels of positive correlations for all its observed colors. Significant BWB trends were found for the i and o colors, while the i-o color had significance less than 95 . These BWB behaviors are consistent with decreasing amplitude at decreasing frequency observed in this object, as mentioned above. 2000ApJ...537..101F reported the similar color trends. On the other hand, this type of correlation appears to be weak 2006A&A...450...39G or opposite 2000ApJS..127...11G in other observations. OI 090.4 OI 090.4 was first identified as a BL Lac object by 1977ApJ...215L..71T . Cross identification of optical and radio source was accomplished by 1977AJ.....82..692C . A tentative redshift z = 0.66 was proposed by 1986ASSL..121..675P and was then questioned by 1991MNRAS.252..482A . 1996MNRAS.283..241F suggested z 0.3 on the basis of the host galaxy properties. 2003A&A...412..651C identified [ OII ] 3727 and [ OIII ] 5007 indicating redshift z = 0.266 0.001. 1995ApJS..100...37G suggested it was a LBL by its multi-frequency spectrum. 2009A&A...507L..33P reported its viewing angle of jet at about 6.9 degs. Its optical variability was studied by a number of authors e.g. 1980PASP...92..156B,1981A&A...103..342Z,1988AJ.....96.1215P,1991A&AS...88..225S,1991A&AS...87..461X,1994A&AS..106..361X,2000A&AS..143..357K . 1981A&A...103..342Z reported 2 mags fluctuations in the B band. 1988AJ.....96.1215P observed 0.8 mags changes in the B band on a 2 year timescale. 1991A&AS...88..225S detected 0.9 mags variations in the V band from 1984 to 1989. 1994A&AS..106..361X claimed 1.04 mags variations both in the V and B bands from 1985 to 1991, and a rapid optical flare of 0.56 mags in the B band within 80 mins on 1988 December 11 was also reported by 1991A&AS...87..461X . There were no systematic color changes with brightness in the results of 1997AJ....113.1995N . And they argued the underlying emission mechanism of this source was of a non-thermal nature. We observed this source on 19, 21, and 18 nights in the , i , and o bands, respectively, between 2006 March 14 and 2011 March 30 (Table sources ). Due to the few data points, brief statistical results are listed for this source. No IDV was found because of the low sampling. The amplitude of the variations is systematically larger at higher frequencies (see Table observations ). As can be seen in Figure fig:all , the average spectral index was 1.46. One can see from Figure fig:allcolor , OI 090.4 shows a stronger BWB trend with i-o color than with the i and o colors. The low confidence levels (p > 0.1) for the i and o colors may relate to the few data points and big errors. The observed BWB trend is inconsistent with the results from 1997AJ....113.1995N . Mrk 421 Mrk 421 is one of the nearest blazars and it has a redshift of z = 0.03 2005ApJ...635..173S . It was first reported as an elliptical galaxy with a highly condensed nucleus emitting non-thermal continuum radiation but no emission lines in the optical regime 1975ApJ...198..261U . It is classified as a HBL due to its synchrotron peak higher than 0.1 keV 2012AJ....143...23G . In optical region, 1975ApJ...201L.109M found variations of 4.7 mags from 1899 to 1975. Observations on 17 nights between 2009 November 15 and 2011 May 2 were made by 2012MNRAS.425.3002G , which showed a maximum brightness of 12.46 in the V band on JD 2455684. 1999ApJS..121..131F found that the variations in the J , H , and K bands were much smaller than those in the optical. 2004A&A...422..505G reported typical brightness variations of 0.4 mags and a maximum variation of 0.89 mags in the J band from February to March, 2003. A BWB behavior was claimed for this source 1988A&AS...72..347S,1997A&AT...14..119F,1998A&A...339...41T,2014Ap&SS.349..909C . 1997A&AT...14..119F and 1998A&A...339...41T claimed that this BWB chromatism may be due to the thermal contribution of the host galaxy. But 2014Ap&SS.349..909C argued that only a host galaxy contribution was not sufficient, and some intrinsic mechanism was also needed. We observed this source on 15, 5, and 16 nights in the , m , and o bands, respectively, between 2005 January 30 and 2008 May 05. The observed maximum brightness of 13.159 mags in the band occurred on 2008 March 2 (JD=2454528). As can be seen from Table observations , the amplitude in this band was larger than the one in the o band for the same period. We only observed Mrk 421 simultaneously in the and o bands for several days. So we didn't calculate its spectral index. One can see from Figure fig:allcolor , the positive correlation derives that Mrk 421 exhibits a strong BWB trend with the o color, which is in accordance with the previous results mentioned above. ON 231 ON 231 was discovered as a variable star by 1916AN....202..415W , and was reported as a new quasar by 1971Natur.231..515B . The detection of a few weak emission lines in its spectrum made it possible to determine a redshift of z = 0.102 1985ApJ...292..614W . The synchrotron peak frequency log _ peak = 14.84 was calculated by 2006A&A...445..441N and it is classified as an IBL. In the optical regime, the optical outbursts in 1940, 1953, and 1968 were observed by 1998A&AS..130..109T . A series of 2 mags outbursts were claimed in 1975-1976, and this type of rapid activity continued through 1986 and early 1987 1988AJ.....95..374W . A variation with a timescale of 6 days was detected in 1983 1987A&AS...67...17X . 1991A&AS...87..461X reported three outbursts with similar profiles from 1987 to 1989. 1999A&A...342L..49M observed an extraordinary outburst from April to May, 1988, and the magnitude in the R band reached a maximum brightness of 12.2. In 1997, the R magnitude showed an oscillating behavior between 14 and 14.5 for about two months 2002A&A...395...11T . 2013MNRAS.429.2773C claimed the larger variation amplitude at higher frequency and the BWB chromatism. They also found that the variability at the R band lagged that at the B band by 1200 s on March 17. Our optical , i , and o bands observations of ON 231 were carried out for 76, 80, and 78 nights, respectively, between 2009 May 23 and 2011 May 15. At the begin, the object was at a relatively high state. Then it got fainter and showed some oscillations at the second and third stages. At the last stage, the brightness dropped gradually from JDs 2455572 to 2455680 and raised again after that. One can see from Table observations that the variation amplitude decreases towards longer wavelengths, which is in accord with 2013MNRAS.429.2773C . We did not find any IDV because of the low sampling on each single night. No inter-band time lags were found at long and short timescales for this source (see Table alldelay ). One can see from Figure fig:all , the spectral indices decreased with increasing luminosity. However, 2006MNRAS.371.1243H reported the spectral index decreased with increasing luminosity. ON 231 shows strong BWB behavior in all three color-magnitude diagrams (Figure fig:allcolor ). This is in agreement with the results of 1998A&AS..130..109T and 2014A&A...562A..79S . PG 1553+113 Located in the Serpens Caput constellation, PG 1553+113 was classified as a BL Lac object based on its featureless spectrum 1990PASP..102.1120F and was classified as a HBL blazar 1994ApJS...93..125F,1995A&AS..109..267G,2002A&A...383..410B . Evidence of very-high-energy ray emission from this source was first reported by H.E.S.S. in 2005 2006A&A...448L..19A and was later confirmed by observations above 200 GeV with the MAGIC telescope at a significance level of 8.8 2007ApJ...654L.119A . Due to its featureless optical spectrum, the redshift of PG 1553+113 remains highly uncertain. Measurements using the Hubble Space Telescope (HST) Cosmic Origins Spectrograph (COS) yielded a lower limit of 0.395 2010ApJ...720..976D . The small statistical uncertainties of the VERITAS energy spectrum provided a robust upper limit of 0.62 2015ApJ...799....7A . 2011MNRAS.416..101G claimed the detection of its IDV in the optical region.
