A general BWB trend was found in the optical observations by a WEBT campaign from April to August of 2013 2015MNRAS.454..353R . However, 2015A&A...573A..69W did not find a definite long-term trend when analyzing the color behavior in 2007-2012. We observed this source on 28 nights in the , i , and o bands between 2009 May 26 and 2010 March 28. One can see from Figure fig:all that there is an outburst of about 0.2 mags on a timescale of 23 days (from JD 2454988 to JD 2455011). The source got fainter at the second stage and changed into a relatively high state at the last. Since we only got the differential magnitudes, we did not calculate the spectral indices. The overall variation amplitudes were = 0^ m .475 , i = 0^ m .47 , and o = 0^ m .462 , which showed a decrease trend with decreasing frequency. No IDV was observed on each single night. Although we did not collect much data for this object, it showed a complete outburst at the beginning. So we made a correlation analysis for it. As can be seen from Table alldelay , however, no inter-band time lags were found for this source. OT 546 OT 546 was first noticed by 1966ApJ...143..192Z and was classified as a BL Lac object by 1980ARA&A..18..321A . The redshift of z = 0.0554 0.0003 was measured by 1978ApJ...219L..97O . It was a nearby HBL 2015ApJ...808..110A and was discovered as a very high energy (VHE) source by MAGIC 2014A&A...563A..90A . OT 546 was also found to have a massive black hole 5.4 10^8 M_ at its center 1995Ap&SS.229..157F . Its optical variations were discovered by 1967ApJ...150L.177S . From 1975 to 1987, the lightcurves from Rosemary Hill Observatory observations showed variations with an overall amplitude of 0.8 mags and an average magnitude of 16.7 in the B band 1988AJ.....96.1215P . The monitoring from 1996 to 1997 in Tuorla Observatory displayed that the brightness in the V band varied between 15.76 and 16.12 2000A&AS..143..357K . The monitoring of OT 546 can be divided into two phases. Phase 1 is from JDs 2453444 to 2453919. The object was observed on 55, 54, and 52 nights in the e , i , and m bands, respectively. Phase 2 is from JDs 2454134 to 2455713. The object was observed on 113, 121, and 110 nights respectively in the , i , and o bands. The brightness of this object was quite stable during our monitoring period. There are four flares at around JDs 2453650, 2454800, 2454960, and 2455330. The last flare has an amplitude of about 0.2 mags in the i band. As can be seen from Table observations , the variation amplitude decreases towards longer wavelengths in both phases. The overall amplitudes are less than 1 mags and this is in agreement with the results of 1988AJ.....96.1215P and 2000A&AS..143..357K . We did not find any IDV on each single night because of the low sampling. One can see from Figure fig:all that the spectral indices fluctuated slightly around 1.8. From the results in Figure fig:allcolor and Table color , strong BWB chromatisms were found for the i , o , e-i , and e-m color behaviors, at 99.99 confidence. Meanwhile, BWB trends with the i-o and i-m colors were detected at a relatively low confidence level. This may be because of the closely separated frequencies and the differences between the variation amplitudes at different wavelengths. On the other side, 1999ApJS..121..131F claimed a RWB trend from their observations. 1ES 1959+650 1ES 1959+650, with a redshift of z = 0.047 1993ApJ...412..541S , was classified as a HBL because its synchrotron bump peaked at UV--X-ray frequencies 2010ApJ...719L.162B . 2003ApJ...583L...9H found it to be a TeV emission source. The mass of its black hole was estimated to be 1.5 10^8 M_ 2002ApJ...569L..35F . Previous studies suggested that 1ES 1959+650 was highly variable in the optical wavelengths e.g. 2000A&AS..144..481V,2004ApJ...601..151K,2009ASPC..408..266K,2013ApJS..206...11S . 2000A&AS..144..481V obtained a decrease of 0.28 mags within 4 days. 2009ASPC..408..266K reported a maximum variability of R = 1.07 mags within 358 nights. Sorcia et al. (2013) found that the maximum and minimum R magnitudes were 14.08 and 15.20, respectively, from 2009 to 2010. 2012MNRAS.420.3147G did not detect the genuine IDV, whereas IDV was observed on two nights in 2009 and no quasi-periodicity was found in the long-term lightcurves of 2015AJ....150...67Y . 2017MNRAS.469.1682Z also claimed no detection of IDV for 38 intra-day light curves and they argued that the larger variation amplitude in the higher frequency can be demonstrated by the BWB trend. We observed this source in the , i , and o bands on 81, 81, and 71 nights, respectively, between 2009 July 03 and 2011 June 13. There was one outburst at the first stage. After that, the light curves were characterized by continuous increase in brightness and the i magnitude ran up to 14.760 on 2010 September 13 (JD 2455453). Several data points were recorded at the last stage of our observation and the object turned faint. During the whole period of our monitoring, the light curves are quite "clean" and no fast flares were overlapped on long-term variability. The variation amplitude tends to decrease with decreasing frequency (see Table observations ). No IDV was claimed because of the low sampling for this source and actually this is agree with no appearance of fast flares for our observations. We did not detect any inter-band time lags (see Table alldelay ).

sources.summary: The paper studies X-ray and UV AstroSat observations of the BL Lac 1ES 1959+650, describing its redshift, VHE detections, exceptional flaring, and correlated X-ray/ray behavior.

quotes.quote: 1ES 1959+650 is an HBL source located at redshift z=0.048 1996ApJS..104..251P . This source was first observed in radio band using NRAO Green Bank Telescope 1991ApJS...75.1011G , and later the X-ray emission from it was detected during the Einstein IPC Slew Survey 1992ApJS...80..257E . The VHE emission from 1ES 1959+650 was first detected by the Seven Telescope Array group in 1999 1999ICRC....3..370N and it became among the first extra-galactic sources detected at VHE energies 2003ApJ...583L...9H .

Numerous exceptional flaring events has been reported from 1ES 1959+650 across the electromagnetic spectrum (EMS) including the intense activity at very high energies (GeV/TeV). In addition to broad emission like features, 1ES 1959+650 also exhibits orphan TeV flares 2004ApJ...601..151K, 2014ApJ...797...89A . During the simultaneous multi-wavelength flaring, the X-ray and ray fluxes of 1ES 1959+650 are well correlated, which is is usually explained in-terms of one-zone Synchrotron Self-Compton (SSC) models.
In this work, we report the X-ray and UV analysis of AstroSat observations of 1ES 1959+650 during October 2017.

sources.summary: For 1ES 1959+650, the paper finds significant spectral-index versus gamma-ray correlation but not optical-gamma flux correlation, and treats a possible lag as likely sampling-driven.

quotes.quote: Two objects (1ES 1959+650; HSP, and A0 0235+164; LSP) demonstrate significant spectral index vs ray flux correlations, but not spectral index vs optical flux correlations. Furthermore, 1ES 1959+650 shows no significant correlation between the optical and ray fluxes whereas A0 0235+164 does. In the case of 1ES 1959+650, the correlations indicate that optical colour variability from jet emission may be present, but would not be temporally consistent with any optical flux, but rather consistent with preceding or delayed ray activity.

Our time lag result for this source hinted at such behaviour but was ultimately discouraged due to unevenly sampled optical data. More observations of this object with regular sampling need to be obtained, ideally during a range of activity states, in order to make more definitive conclusions. The 1ES 1959+650 light curve shows that from MJD 57500--57560 (while in an optically fainter state) the data were obtained at a much higher cadence (one observation every two days) than the rest of the observations (one observation every two weeks). After correcting for the potential lag, this period then aligns with an active state in the ray light curve.

sources.summary: The paper models 1ES 1959+650 as a TeV blazar with an orphan TeV flare about two weeks after an ordinary flare, challenging simple leptonic models.

quotes.quote: However, the recent observations of 1ES 1959+650 kra-et04,dan05 found that there is a TeV ray flare that is not accompanied by a X-ray flare, which was observed on June 4, 2002 (MJD 52,429) by the Whipple telescope. This is called the ``orphan'' TeV ray (OTG) flare. The OTG flare occurred about 15 days after a usual flare in which X-ray and ray flares were observed contemporaneously. More recently another OTG flare might have been observed from Mrk 421 bla05 , although the X-ray flux seems to have peaked about 1.5 days before this TeV flare and this may not be a true OTG flare. The existence of the OTG flares is challenging for the leptonic models.

The observed delay time between the OTG flare and the previous flare is about two weeks in 1ES 1959+650 kra-et04 . The delay time between the primary and OTG flares in the comoving frame of the jet is much shorter than the synchrotron cooling time of nonthermal electrons/positrons (see sec:lumi for detail), if the magnetic field in the jet is about 0.1 G. Although high energy electrons/positrons injected into the primary flare are rapidly cooled, nonthermal electrons/positrons that emit TeV rays in a quiescent state may be still continuously injected into the jet and they may contribute to the secondary flare.

sources.summary: The paper presents simultaneous multiwavelength observations of the TeV blazar, finding high optical/X-ray states, low VHE emission, X-ray spectral curvature, and a one-zone SSC interpretation.

quotes.quote: We present the results from a multiwavelength campaign on the TeV blazar 1ES 1959+650, performed in May, 2006. Data from the optical, UV, soft- and hard-X-ray and very high energy (VHE) gamma-ray ( E > 100 GeV) bands were obtained with the and satellites, with the telescope and other ground based facilities. The source spectral energy distribution (SED), derived from and observations at the end of May 2006, shows the usual double hump shape, with the synchrotron peak at a higher flux level than the Compton peak. With respect to historical values, during our campaign the source exhibited a relatively high state in X-rays and optical, while in the VHE band it was at one of the lowest level so far recorded.

In the optical band it is highly variable and shows a complex structure composed by an elliptical galaxy (M _R =-23 , z =0.048) plus a disc and an absorption dust lane (Heidt et al. 1999). The mass of the central black hole has been estimated to be in the range 1.3-4.4 10^8 M_ as derived either from the stellar velocity dispersion or from the bulge luminosity (Falomo et al. 2002).

sources.summary: The paper observes 1ES 1959+650 photometrically and polarimetrically, emphasizing its resolved host galaxy, lack of intra-night variability, inter-night variability, and about 1 percent host-galaxy polarization dilution.

quotes.quote: Here we present simultaneous photometric and polarimetric observations of two relatively nearby blazars, 1ES 1959+650 and HB89 2201+044 , that were obtained using the Calar Alto Faint Object Spectrograph mounted at the 2.2 m telescope in Calar Alto, Spain. An outstanding characteristic of these two blazars is the presence of well resolved host galaxies. This particular feature allows us to produce a study of their intrinsic polarization, a measurement of the polarization state of the galactic nucleus unaffected by the host galaxy. To carry out this work, we computed photometric fluxes from which we calculated the degree and orientation of the blazars polarization.

We find that the two blazars do not present intra-night photo-polarimetric variability, although we do detect a significant inter-night variability. Comparing polarimetric values before and after accounting for the host galaxies, we observe a significant difference in the polarization degree of about 1 in the case of 1ES 1959+650 , and 0.3 in the case of HB89 2201+044 , thus evidencing the non-negligible impact introduced by the host galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes 1ES 1959+650 during a 2016 flaring state, selecting three VHE flux epochs and noting compact, weak MOJAVE behavior with stable polarization angle.

quotes.quote: 1ES 1959+650 was in a flaring state during 2016. We selected three different nights based on the level of VHE gamma-ray flux of the source during 2016 and availability of the simultaneous MWL observations at lower energy bands. The highest, intermediate and lowest VHE gamma-ray flux was observed on 2016 June 14 (MJD 57553), June 8 (MJD 57547), and November 20 (MJD 57711), respectively.

1ES 1959+650 was dropped from the MOJAVE programme in 2009 as it is too compact and weak at 15 GHz and there is no data from the source between 2013 and 2018. In the earlier data the source showed a polarisation degree of 2.6-4.5 and its polarisation angle was pretty stable at 144-160 . The position angle of the jet is 140-160 .

sources.summary: The paper treats 1ES 1959+650 as a high-state BL Lac blazar and models how binary-star transits could create detectable gamma-ray absorption features in its spectrum.

quotes.quote: The first active galaxy is a BL Lac object: 1ES \ 1959+650. Recently it was detected in the high state by the Fermi LAT in the multi-GeV energies and by MAGIC in sub-TeV energies (Acciari et al. 2020b). We include the effects of absorption of ray spectra observed from 1ES \ 1959+650 in the case of transits of two binary systems with likely binary system and transit parameters (see left columns of Fig. 10).

For the stronger source, 1ES1959+650 we use short term (30 min) sensitivity IRF, while for a weaker M87 we use the corresponding mid-term (5 hr) IRF. Moreover, for 1ES1959+650 we also take into account the absorption in the extragalactic background light following Dom nguez et al. (2011) model, which affects the observed emission from the source in the multi-TeV range. In the case of 1ES \ 1959+650 it is clear that even very short ( 30 min) exposure can be used for very accurate probing of the source spectrum and reproducing the absorption feature with high details.

sources.summary: The paper models 1ES 1959+650 with a wide possible Doppler boosting range from flare-state SED studies and finds its flare timescale high but not inconsistent with jet wobbling.

quotes.quote: We selected 4 bright blazars from the Fermi 4LAC catalog , with well covered Swift XRT light curves and reliably determined mass: Mrk 421, Mrk 501, 1ES 1959+650 and 3C 273.

1ES 1959+650 From two multivawelength flare light curves in 2016, was constrained by a leptonic one-zone SSC model as (30, 60) . That is consistent with the results of other authors, deriving for high state = 20 , or 40 . Low state SED were also modeled in a few cases, giving = 25 or 30 . Provided that the most flares occurred in very active high state of the source, and that we have no other limits on , we need to consider relatively wide interval of possible boosting (20, 60) for this source.

For 1ES 1959+650, the timescale of blazar flares constrained by observations tends to be higher, even though the consistency with the jet wobbling scenario cannot be ruled out as the lower confidence limit is / t_ g 1.2 10^4 .

sources.summary: The paper models the host galaxy of 1959+650 in infrared images, estimating structural parameters and host-galaxy contributions used to correct polarization data.

quotes.quote: These deep images were also used to model the host galaxy of 1959+650 in H and K bands. To make a model of the host galaxy, we used a photometric decomposition technique, which allows one to find an analytical model of an object and separate the light coming from its components. The distance to the object does not allow us to resolve it in great detail on our IR images, so in this work we adopted a rather simple model consisting of two components: a Sersic Sersic1963, Sersic1968 function to describe the host galaxy, and a point source for the active nucleus.

Our estimates of the host galaxy structural parameters are as follows: ellipticity e=0.31 0.05 , position angle PA=72.9 13.1 , Sersic index n=1.55 0.5 , effective radius r_e(K)=2.53 0.46'', r_e(H)=2.84 0.35'' , effective surface brightness _e(K)=16.98 0.39'' , and _e(H)=17.65 0.27'' mag per square arcsecond. The rather large uncertainties, especially in the Sersic index value, are attributed to poor resolution of the object: the observed object effective radius is close to the PSF FWHM, which results in a degradation of the decomposition quality Trujillo2001 . We have employed the model parameters to estimate contributions of the host galaxy of 1959+650 within an aperture of diameter 5'' that are equal to 14.28 0.09 mag and 13.71 0.05 mag in H and K bands, respectively.

sources.summary: The paper models the June 2002 orphan TeV flare of 1ES 1959+650, estimating its neutrino flux and finding AMANDA-II should see too few events.

quotes.quote: For a redshift z = 0.047 the blazar lies at a distance of d_L 210 Mpc. demonstrated that a Compton optical depth of _m = 0.1 for the cloud is in agreement with a BL Lac classification of 1ES 1959+650 concerning its emission line luminosity, and typical cloud sizes and densities in the pc-scale vicinity of the central engine. SEDfig shows the resulting spectral energy distribution that provides a reasonable representation of the observed Whipple orphan flare spectrum of 1ES 1959+650 as rays from the ^0 decay for a maximum proton Lorentz factor of '_ p, max = 10^4 with a _p=2 proton injection spectral index and using various cosmic background models.

For an _p = 2 proton spectrum and using the Primack04 background model we find the expected integrated neutrino flux at E_ > 100 GeV to be 2.2 10^ 9 cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 with a neutrino flavor ratio of _ : _e: _ =2:1:0 . We presented calculations of the neutrino flux and _ event rate from the expected neutrino flare that was accompanied by the ray orphan flare of 1ES 1959+650 in June 2002, within the recently proposed hadronic Synchrotron Mirror model. Given the short duration of this flare, the expected rate in AMANDA-II is of order 10^ 4 _ events per hour and thus insufficient to explain the recent event observed by AMANDA-II from the direction of 1ES 1959+650 at the time of the June 2002 orphan TeV-flare.

sources.summary: The paper reports that 1ES 1959+650 was detected during optical flares but had low VHE emission in spring 2006, making it the clear non-correlation case.

quotes.quote: In 11 cases the source was detected by MAGIC. These 11 flares were detected in 9 sources: Mrk 421, 1ES 1959+650, PG 1553+113, 3C 66A, 3C 279, BL Lac, Mrk 180, 1ES 1011+496, S5 0716+714 (see Fig. 2).
For 1ES 1959+650 the optical flare occured in spring 2006 (MJD 53800 , see Fig. 2), when the source was in a very low state at VHE ray regime .

Therefore, the only source which shows clear evidence that the VHE ray flux does NOT follow the optical flux states is 1ES 1959+650. Interestingly, for this source also lack of correlation between X-rays and VHE rays has been observed .

sources.summary: The paper studies 1ES 1959+650 as an HBL/blazar that underwent strong multiwavelength flaring in 2015, with optical, UV, X-ray, and gamma-ray outbursts and short-term variability.

quotes.quote: We present the results from a multiwavelength study of the flaring activity in HBL, 1ES 1959+650, during January 2015-June 2016. The source underwent significant flux enhancements showing two major outbursts (March 2015 and October 2015) in optical, UV, X-rays and gamma-rays. Normally, HBLs are not very active but 1ES 1959+650 has shown exceptional outburst activity across the whole electromagnetic spectrum (EMS). We used the data from Fermi-LAT, Swift-XRT UVOT and optical data from Mt. Abu InfraRed Observatory (MIRO) along with archival data from Steward Observatory to look for possible connections between emissions at different energies and the nature of variability during flaring state. During October 2015 outburst, thirteen nights of optical follow-up observations showed brightest and the faintest nightly averaged V-band magnitudes as 14.45(0.03) and 14.85(0.02), respectively.

sources.summary: The review describes 1ES 1959+650 as an HBL AGN with TeV outbursts, including a 2002 orphan flare that challenges simple leptonic SSC interpretations.

quotes.quote: 1ES1959+650 The AGN 1ES 1959+650 ( z=0.047 ) was first detected in the Einstein IPC Slew Survey and classified as a HBL subclass, based on its X-ray to radio flux ratio with a luminosity distance of d_L=210 Mpc and the mass of the central black hole is estimated to be 1.5 10^8 M_ . In 1998, VHE gamma-ray from 1ES 1959+650 was observed by the Seven Telescope Array in Utah and later on other observations were also reported. In May 2002, 1ES 1959+650 had a strong TeV outburst which was observed by Whipple and HEGRA experiments as well as in the X-ray range by RXTE experiments. The X-ray flux smoothly declined throughout the following month. However, during this smooth decline period, a second TeV flare was observed after few days (on 4th of June) of the initial one without a X-ray counterpart .

sources.summary: The paper finds large 2002 and 2016 outbursts in 1ES 1959+650, higher 2016 luminosity at comparable peak energy, and significant positive spectral correlations.

quotes.quote: 1ES 1959+650 was in large outbursts in 2002 and 2016. In 2002 ( \ observations), its peak luminosity increased from 10 ^ 44.3 to 10 ^ 45.5 erg \ s ^ 1 and peak energy increased from 0.7 to 30 keV. In 2016 \ observations of 2018MNRAS.473.2542K , its peak luminosity increased from 10 ^ 44.5 to 10 ^ 45.3 erg \ s ^ 1 and peak energy increased from 0.4 to 13 keV. In the same peak energy range, the peak luminosity in 2016 was five times larger than that in 2002. For the observations in both 2002 and 2016, there is a significant positive correlation between \ and \ as well as between \ and .

sources.summary: The paper reports IXPE detections of variable X-ray polarization in 1ES 1959+650, including significant 2-8 keV polarization and accompanying flux and spectral variations.

quotes.quote: Two times out of four IXPE observations for 1ES 1959+650 detect the significant polarization in 2--8 keV band, and one more in 2--4 keV band. The detected highest polarization degree is _ X =12.4 0.7 with an electric vector polarization angle _ X =19.7 1.6 in 2--8 keV band. The X-ray polarization of 1ES 1959+650 exhibits the obvious variability, and is accompanied by the variations of _ X , flux, and spectrum.

1ES 1959+650 z=0.047 ; 1996ApJS..104..251P and PKS 2155--304 z=0.116 ; 1993ApJ...411L..63F are two TeV HBLs 1999ApJ...513..161C,2003ApJ...583L...9H and are also the targets of the IXPE observations. In this paper, we report the first X-ray polarimetric observations of the two HBLs.

sources.summary: The paper says 1ES 1959+650 covers a narrower spectral-parameter region than Mrk 421, with mostly 2006 observations and one high-curvature pointing during fading flux.

quotes.quote: The observations of 1ES 1959+650 cover a narrower subregion of both the E_p^*-L_p^* and the E_p^*-b plane relative to Mrk 421, as shown by Fig 6. These observations were mostly performed within ten days during 2006. The observation performed on 29 May 2006 (circled) is peculiar as it yields a very high curvature value. This pointing took place at the end of a set of 6 observations, in which the flux was decreasing; this may represent a phase dominated by cooling, when the estimated value of the curvature could well be affected by an exponential cutoff close to the observed energy range.

sources.summary: The paper presents 1ES 1959+650 as an HBL with strong 2002 TeV outburst, multiwavelength campaigns, tentative X-ray/gamma correlation, and separate SED states.

quotes.quote: 1ES 1959+650. A HBL object with a strong TeV outburst in May 2002 was detected by VERITAS (Holder et al. 2003), HEGRA (Horns et al. 2002) and CAT (Aharonian et al. 2003). During the outburst, the flux level reached to 3 times the Crab flux and there was evidence for strong variability. The multiwavelength campaign in radio, optical, X-ray and TeV gamma-ray bands was performed from 2002 May 18 to August 14 (Krawczynski et al. 2004). A time-averaged spectrum corresponding to the TeV gamma-ray high state is considered in this work (blue squares in Fig. Fig:1 ).

sources.summary: The paper describes 1ES 1959+650 as a VHE BL Lac source in its monitoring sample and reports its optical/radio flux level, variability trends, and correlation behavior.

quotes.quote: 1ES 1959+650 : The visual inspection of the light curves reveals similar flaring behaviour in the radio and optical band with quasi-simultaneous outbursts starting around MJD 54900, 55350, 55700, 56200 and 56500 (see Fig. Fig:lc28 ). The linear regression analysis shows a highly significant flux density increase in both radio and optical bands throughout the observing period, but similarly to Mrk 501 the DCF does not find a significant correlation. This is probably due to multiple time scales as well as varying ratio of the amplitude of the outbursts. The polynomial subtraction suggests that at least 15 the optical flux would originate from common emission component.

sources.summary: The paper selects 1ES 1959+650 for detailed RXTE/PCA blazar analysis, extracts one flare, finds most peak energies below 30 keV, and reports harder-when-brighter X-ray spectra.

quotes.quote: As such, among 32 TeV blazars, we singled out 5 objects (i.e., Mrk 421, Mrk 501, PKS 2155--304, PKS 2005--489, and 1ES 1959+650; hereafter ``the five sources''; see Table table:allta ) and for which RXTE /PCA data allow us to obtain high-quality spectra (detailed analysis of the other 27 TeV blazars will be presented in a future study). Furthermore, the flares were selected with the following criteria: 1) individual flares lasted for several days and were covered by at least two observations in both the rise and decay periods; and 2) the minimum total count rate (summed over available PCUs) in 3--25 keV is above 30 /s. In addition, adjacent outbursts following or followed by those flares were also included. Finally, we picked out 20.5 flares for Mrk 421 (an outburst with observations only in the rise or decay period was considered as 0.5 flare), 7 flares for Mrk 501, 4 flares for PKS 2155--304, and only one flare for both PKS 2005--489 and 1ES 1959+650 from the 16-year data (see Figures fig:spectrum and fig:pfevo for the typical flares of each source; also see the observations annotated with `` '' in Table table:allta .

Combining the 3--25 keV spectral shape information and the synchrotron peak energy obtained by fitting all PCA spectra with the log-parabolic model detailed in Section sec:peakes , we found that: for Mrk 421, the peak energy of all the spectra is below 6 keV, which is consistent with the results in 2004A&A...413..489M , 2004ApJ...601..759T and 2007A&A...466..521T (but 2009A&A...501..879T shows that its peak energy could be up to 30 keV); for Mrk 501, the peak energy of most spectra is above 3 keV, and 2008A&A...478..395M shows that its peak energy could be up to 100 keV; for PKS 2005--489 and PKS 2155--304, the peak energy of spectra is below 3 keV; for 1ES 1959+650, the peak energy of most spectra is below 30 keV.

The X-ray spectra, characterized by , display a harder-when-brighter trend during a number of flares of the five brightest sources (i.e., Mrk 421, Mrk 501, PKS 2155--304, PKS 2005--489, and 1ES 1959+650), which is consistent with previous studies.

sources.summary: 1ES 1959+650 contributes about as many flares as Mkn 501, but its cumulative behavior differs because it entered a brighter, more variable X-ray state after 57000 MJD.

quotes.quote: Inspection of the cumulative curves of 1ES 1959+650 (index 6) and Mkn 501 (index 38) shows that a similar total number of flares ( N_ fl 90 ) contributes to the total expected number of neutrinos for each source. However, the gradient of the two curves is very different, suggesting a different temporal behaviour between these two sources. Indeed, as shown in Fig.  fig:lc , 1ES 1959+650 appears to have entered a state characterized by higher average X-ray flux and more variability after 57000  MJD, while Mkn 501 was more active at earlier times 55000-57000  MJD.
For instance, if 1ES 1959+650 were observed only after 57000  MJD, its mean flux would be 1.4 times higher than the mean flux estimated from all its measurements (see Fig.  fig:lc ).

sources.summary: The paper gives 1ES 1959+650 an individual blazar variability note, summarizing TeV-band detections, GeV or hard-X-ray behavior, and variability classification.

quotes.quote: 1ES 1959+650 : This source is a bright VHE emitting blazer e.g., 2013ApJ...775....3A , and the detection of an orphan TeV flare was also reported 2004ApJ...601..151K . This is one of the few sources studied in this work that were consistently detected in the TeV band throughout the 15 years of the LAT operation. The TeV band light curve of this blazar has indicated significant flux variability, which was also accompanied by the flux variations seen in the GeV band light curve. In particular, a 4 years long ( MJD 57022 58462) ray flare was identified in both the TeV and GeV band light curves which was confirmed by the Bayesian block analysis.

sources.summary: The paper models the orphan TeV flare of 1ES 1959+650 as proton interactions with reflected synchrotron photons, constraining relativistic protons in the blazar jet.

quotes.quote: The recent observations by the Whipple collaboration of an ``orphan'' TeV flare of 1ES 1959+650 (without simultaneous X-ray flare) is very hard to reconcile with the standard leptonic SSC model which is routinely very successfully employed to explain the SED and spectral variability of TeV blazars. In this paper, an alternative scenario is suggested in which the ``orphan'' TeV flare may originate from relativistic protons, interacting with an external photon field supplied by electron-synchrotron radiation reflected off a dilute reflector. While the external photons will be virtually ``invisible'' to the co-moving ultrarelativistic electrons in the jet due to Klein-Nishina effects, their Doppler boosted energy is high enough to excite the resonance from relativistic protons with Lorentz factors of _p 10^3 - 10^4 . This model is capable of explaining the ``orphan'' TeV flare of 1ES 1959+650 with plausible parameters, thus constraining the number and characteristic energy of relativistic protons in the jet of this blazar.

sources.summary: The paper flags 1ES 1959+650 as having catalog tension tied to variability, unusually bright GeV flux, and HE-VHE spectral deviations that probably shape its IC peak.

quotes.quote: In some cases (e.g. in 1ES 1959+650) there is some tension with respect to the 2WHSP catalog that we attribute to the high variability of the sources and to the fact that these estimations are all based on not-simultaneous archival data.
All sources show total fluxes of the same order of that of 1ES 0229+200, except for 1ES 1959+650 and the two Markarians that are particularly bright.

Some other sources like the two Markarians, 1ES 1959+650, and 1ES 2344+514, present strong deviations from this linear model, meaning that the HE and the VHE spectra have different slopes and they are probably shaping the IC peak (as in fig:TeV-fits-b ).

sources.summary: The paper treats 1ES 1959+650 as a well-known HBL showing EHBL-like multi-TeV flares, shifted synchrotron/SSC peaks, and behavior explained by a two-zone photohadronic scenario.

quotes.quote: The nearest and the extensively studied HBLs Markarian 421 (Mrk 421), Markarian 501 (Mrk 501) and 1ES 1959+650 have also shown EHBL-like behavior with harder TeV spectra in which the synchrotron and SSC peaks are shifted towards higher energy during some flaring episodes Ahnen:2018mtr,Hayashida:2020wez,Aleksi__2015 .

Also, during a multiwavelength (MW) campaign of 1ES 1959+650 between 29th April to 21st November 2016, the MAGIC telescopes observed multi-TeV flaring events during the nights of 13th, 14th June and 1st July 2016 when the position of the synchrotron peak was in EHBL range and exhibiting extreme HBL-like behavior.

As discussed in the introduction, similar behavior has also been observed from Mrk 501 and 1ES 1959+650 which are well known HBLs, and there spectra are explained very well with the two-zone photohadronic scenario Sahu:2020tko,Sahu:2020kce .

gmw_00000528.name: H 1426+428
gmw_00000528.overview: H 1426+428 is an extreme high-energy-peaked BL Lacertae object [arXiv:2108.05460, arXiv:1608.08860] located at a redshift of z = 0.129 [arXiv:2108.05460, arXiv:1005.4475]. Its parsec-scale radio structure consists of a compact core surrounded by a faint halo [arXiv:1105.3345] and a faint jet extending to the northwest [arXiv:0801.2749].

sources.summary: The paper summarizes long-term and 2021 VERITAS observations of H 1426+428, finding a brighter 2021 VHE state after historically lower fluxes.

quotes.quote: H 1426+428 ( z = 0.129 ) is an extreme HBL with synchrotron peak located at 10 ^ 18.1 Hz . It was routinely detected before 2002 with VHE fluxes between 5 and 20 Crab. However, since this time the reported VHE fluxes are significantly less ( < 2-3 Crab).

During 2021 monitoring observations, H 1426+428 was found to be in a bright VHE state. This triggered an intense VERITAS and MWL campaign including Swift and NuStar. Overall, 45 h of VERITAS data were acquired. A preliminary analysis of these data yields a 19 detection and a time-average spectrum ( 2.6 ) consistent with the long-term measurement. The preliminary flux, F( > 250 GeV) = (5.6 0.4_ stat ) 10^ 12 cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 , or (3.3 0.2) Crab, is steady throughout 2021 but higher than the 2008-16 average.

sources.summary: The paper presents four-epoch VLBA imaging of H 1426+428, finding a compact core, faint northwest jet, no counterjet, and an apparent component speed near 2c.

quotes.quote: We present Very Long Baseline Array (VLBA) observations of the TeV blazars H 1426+428, 1ES 1959+650, and PKS 2155 304 obtained during the years 2001 through 2004. We observed H 1426+428 at four epochs at 8 GHz, and found that its parsec-scale structure consisted of a 17 mJy core and a single 3 mJy jet component with an apparent speed of 2.09c 0.53c .

Our four 8.4 GHz VLBA images of H 1426+428 are shown in Figure 1. The top row of Figure 1 shows the naturally weighted images, while the bottom row shows the uniformly weighted images. Parameters of the images are given in Table imtab . The linear resolution of these images at the distance of H 1426+428 is approximately 2.3 pc mas ^ 1 . The images all show a compact core with a flux density of 17 mJy, and a faint jet extending to the northwest, with a position angle of about 25 .

sources.summary: The paper uses H1426+428 as a northern TeV blazar for cascade simulations; rejection probabilities rise for stronger IGMF but stay lower than PKS 2155-304 because of northern-instrument limitations.

quotes.quote: For this study we considered two different blazars: PKS2155-304 visible from the southern hemisphere and H1426+428 visible from the northern hemisphere, which combine, in principle, good properties for developing an extended component. The corresponding TeV names for these sources as shown in Figure fluxvsz are TeV-J2158-3013 and TeV-J1428+4240.

Figures rejection_PKS_t and rejection_H1426 show the resulting rejection probabilities for PKS2155-304 and H1426+428 respectively, as a function of the IGMF intensity and for a low energy threshold of 75 GeV and for 50, 100, and 150 hours of observation time. In general, the probability is considerably higher IGMF intensities above 10 ^ 14 G, where it increases with longer observation time windows. The rejection ratio then drops down for weaker magnetic fields in both the southern and northern sites, however, in the latter the ratios are smaller and the drop is more abrupt, probably because of the differences in the instrument performance. The drop in the rejection ratio for weak magnetic fields responds to the fact that in these scenarios, the cascade component is too weak and/or not broadened enough to be differentiated from the PSF distribution and its fluctuations.
The source H1426+428 corresponding to the northern site shows a similar trend, although in this case rejection ratio values are lower, probably due to the instrumental limitations.

sources.summary: The paper treats 1ES 1426+428 as a TeV blazar/ray source whose EBL-corrected spectrum has a 1 TeV bump, explaining its hardening tail with two gluon-condensation source components.

quotes.quote: 1ES 1426+428 is a TeV ray source that has been noticed earlier. The TeV rays from this source arrive the earth after the EBL absorption. However, the measured spectrum looks significantly different compared to the other blazar spectra since it has a bump at E_ 1 TeV [ 36 ] . We use the double GC-sources to explain 1ES 1426+428 spectrum.

The parameters _ ^ II =0 is much smaller than that _ ^ I =0.96 and E_ ^ GCII =10 TeV E_ ^ GCI =0.3 TeV . This result can be understood as follows: the first GC-source produced in the heavy nuclear collisions deep in the star with a smaller E_ ^ GC , the spectrum has non-zero value of _ ^I , while the second GC-source produced by the light nuclear collisions with a larger E_ ^ GC at the surface of this star, where the photons go directly into the sky without passing through the polluted range, therefore, its spectrum has _ ^ II 0 . One can find that superposition of the first intrinsic spectrum at E_ >E_ ^ GCI (where the index _2^I-2>0 ) and the second intrinsic spectrum at E_ <E_ ^ GCII (where the index _1^ II 2<0 ) causes a hardening tail (see red dashed curve in figure 8) even considering the EBL correction.

sources.summary: The review describes 1426+428 as a newly confirmed TeV BL Lac at z about 0.13, useful for testing infrared-background absorption despite preliminary spectra.

quotes.quote: Recently the BL Lac 1426+428 was detected with WHIPPLE horan-ic and confirmed with HEGRA and CAT (in both cases communicated only at the conference). This source is interesting for its redshift of z=0.129 which is about four times that of Mkn 421 and Mkn 501, thus allowing studies of the effect of absorption by the infrared background.

At this conference the HEGRA team has presented a preliminary spectrum of 1426+428, which appears to be surprisingly well defined given the statistical significance of the detection as such. As shown in Fig. 14tev-fig , none of the three models of the cosmic infrared background is in conflict with the data. Preliminary though they are, the results suggest that the intrinsic spectrum of 1426+428 is harder than a power law E^ 2 up to about 10 TeV. Clearly, a better defined spectrum must be measured before definitive conclusion can be made. Nevertheless, with the confirmed detection of an AGN at a redshift of z 0.13 meaningful studies of the cosmic infrared background radiation become feasible by means of TeV astronomy.

sources.summary: The paper reports HEGRA TeV detection of blazar H 1426+428 at z=0.129, derives its absorbed spectrum, and highlights its importance for blazar physics and diffuse extragalactic background studies.

quotes.quote: P uhlhofer, Gerd.Puehlhofer@mpi-hd.mpg.de The detection of TeV rays from the blazar H 1426+428 at an integral flux level of (4 2_ stat 1_ syst ) 10^ 12 erg cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 above 1 TeV with the HEGRA imaging atmospheric Cherenkov telescope system is reported. H 1426+428 is located at a redshift of z =0.129, which makes it the most distant source detected in TeV rays so far. The TeV radiation is expected to be strongly absorbed by the diffuse extragalactic background radiation (DEBRA). The observed energy spectrum of TeV photons is in good agreement with an intrinsic power law spectrum of the source E^ 1.9 corrected for DEBRA absorption.

Most recently, statistically significant TeV signals have been reported also for H 1426+428, by the VERITAS Whipple1426Gamma2001 and HEGRA Talk1426HEGRAICRC2001,TalkFelixICRC2001 collaborations. The relatively large redshift ( z =0.129) of H 1426+428, as well as the fact that it is the third 100 keV blazar ever found ExtremeBLLacsBeppoSaX2001 , make this object an extremely important target for future multiwavelength studies of the nonthermal processes of particle acceleration and radiation in AGN jets, as well as for probing the DEBRA in the near infrared region. In this letter we report the HEGRA observations of H 1426+428 performed in 1999 and 2000, and briefly discuss some apparent astrophysical implications suggested by the obtained results.

sources.summary: The paper identifies H1426+428 as the most distant confirmed TeV source then known, an extreme HBL with weak variable emission and a steep spectrum.

quotes.quote: H1426+428: This source is of interest primarily because, at a redshift of 0.129, it is the most distant confirmed source of TeV gamma rays; three different groups have reported significant detections ; ; . It is weak source (typically 6 of the Crab) and, having its synchrotron peak located at higher frequencies than any of the other TeV blazars, is classified as an ``extreme'' HBL . It was not seen by EGRET. The initial detection by the VERITAS group was at the 5.8 level of significance and was based on 44.4 hours of observation. The source is also significant in that it was predicted to be a detectable TeV emitter based on its hard X-ray spectrum .
The source is definitely variable on time-scales of a year and maybe on times as short as a day. The energy spectrum, which has been derived by three groups, is found to be quite steep.

sources.summary: The paper presents MAGIC and Swift analysis of the EHBL 1ES 1426+428, finding hard VHE/X-ray spectra, variable X-rays, and model-dependent jet energetics.

quotes.quote: Among these sources, only 1ES 1426+428 was already known as TeV emitter . In Table tab:1426_obs we list the details of 1ES 1426 + 428 observations and the results of the data analysis for the three different MAGIC observation campaigns, in 2010, 2012, and 2013 respectively. A signal with 6.0 significance was found from the analysis of 9 hours of 2012 data.

1ES 1426+428 is the most variable source in the X-ray band of our sample. In this paper, we presented the results of the VHE gamma-ray and X-ray analysis for the EHBL 1ES 1426+428. Both the VHE and the X-ray spectra are hard, with only modest variations in the X-ray band over nine years of monitoring.

sources.summary: The paper treats 1ES 1426+428 as a high-redshift extreme BL Lac whose detected TeV emission is strongly absorbed by the cosmic infrared background and may require revised blazar models.

quotes.quote: Because of the relatively large redshift of 1ES 1426+428 ( z=0.129 ), the TeV radiation from this extreme BL Lac object suffers severe IG-absorption. Therefore the discovery of TeV rays from this source by two independent (VERITAS and HEGRA) groups came, to a certain extent, as a pleasant surprise. The spectrum deformation factors calculated for 3 different CIB models show that above 300 GeV the IG-absorption leads to significant steepening of the ray spectrum, but starting from 1 TeV to several TeV the spectrum is deformed only slightly, although the suppression of the absolute flux may be as large as a factor of 100 (Fig. 9). This effect has a simple explanation discussed in Sec. 3. Remarkably, the preliminary spectrum of 1ES 1426+428 derived from the HEGRA CT system data taken in 1999 and 2000 (G otting et al. , 2001), does show a steep slope at low energies with a tendency for a flattening above 1.5 TeV.

Independent of details, the pure fact of detection of TeV rays from 1ES 1426+428 is an indicative of a need for revision of the current conceptual view of TeV blazars, according to which the synchrotron (X-ray) peak in the SED dominates over the inverse Compton (TeV) peak (see e.g. Fossati et al. 1998).

sources.summary: The paper describes 1ES 1426 + 428 as a z=0.129 X-ray-bright blazar with a resolved host galaxy, and reports only weak red-noise evidence in its Fourier spectra.

quotes.quote: 1ES 1426 + 428 The blazar 1ES 1426 + 428 now thought to be at z=0.129 was discovered in the medium X-ray band (2--6 keV) with the Large Area Sky Survey experiment (LASS) on HEAO-1 (Wood et al. \ 1984). It was classified as a BL Lac object on the basis of its featureless optical spectrum. Observations made with the MDM 2.4 m Hiltner telescope constrained its redshift to z 0.106 (Finke et al. \ 2008) and its host galaxy was resolved by Urry et al. \ (2000).

In X-rays, 1ES 1426 + 428 is bright, with a 2--6 keV luminosity 10 ^ 44 ergs sec ^ 1 (assuming isotropic emission), which is typical of HEAO-1 (High Energy Astronomical Observatory) BL Lac objects (Schwartz et al. \ 1989).

We have found that the Fourier power spectra for four of the seven observations of 1ES 1426 + 428 blazar do not display any significant red-noise components, with only possible indications of red-noise at the lowest few bins in temporal frequency.

sources.summary: The paper adds an 8 GHz VLBA image of H 1426+428, finding a northwest jet component similar to previous epochs and updating the component apparent speed.

quotes.quote: H 1426+428 We show our single new image of H 1426+428 at 8 GHz from 2006 Oct 22 in Figure 3. The linear resolution is about 2.3 pc mas ^ 1 , for a redshift of z=0.129 . The source shows a 3 mJy jet component (identified with component C1 seen previously) located about 2 mas northwest of the 20 mJy core. The structure is quite similar to the four previous epochs for this source shown in P08. An updated apparent speed based on the new measured position of C1 is given in update . t Markarian 501 Our VLBA images of Mrk 501 from the 43 GHz VLBA observations in 2008 and 2009 are shown in Figure 4.

sources.summary: The paper models 1ES 1426+428 as a TeV blazar and UHECR candidate, predicting detectable cosmic-ray protons and noting unusually high required jet power.

quotes.quote: Currently there are four such TeV blazars with adequate data, namely 1ES 1426+428 hess1426 , 1ES 0229+200 hess0229,veritas0229 , 1ES 1101-232 hess1101 and 1ES 0347-121 hess0347 . The location of the Pierre Auger Observatory auger04 at latitude 35.2^ S makes it insensitive to the nearest and most powerful UHECR candidate source 1ES 1426+428 with a Declination = +42.67^ . The proposed Auger North detector http://www.augernorth.org/ at latitude 39^ N, however, will be sensitive to 1ES 1426+428.

Thus the total number of UHECR events expected from 1ES 1426+428, for = 2.2 source spectrum, in the proposed Auger North detector is N_ evt &=& E ( ) _ E_ th ^ E_ mx J(E) dE & & 3 L_p 10^ 46 erg/s E 9,000 km^2 yr sr , above a threshold energy E_ th = 4 10^ 19 eV and below the maximum energy E_ mx = 10^ 20 eV. Thus 1ES 1426+428 should be clearly visible, if an UHECR source as modeled here, with the same exposure used in the first data release by the Auger South Observatory auger08 .

sources.summary: The paper treats 1ES1426+428 as a lesser-studied high-energy-peaked BL Lac with modest archival polarization, nearly constant optical flux, low measured polarization, and weak ray detection in the analyzed window.

quotes.quote: 1ES1426+428 1ES1426+428 is a lesser-studied BL Lac object at a redshift of z= 0.132 urry2000 . It is classified as an extreme high-energy-peaked BL Lac object (HSP) costamante2001 and is a HE ray source petry2000, horan2002 . report an optical degree of polarisation of 7 over the period of 1988-1990 MJD 47161 - 47892) and a non-constant EVPA which may show a slow rotation of 150 ^ . The optical flux maintains a fairly constant value of between 16-17 in the R band. We find the degree of polarisation stays below 6 , the optical flux is constant with a median R band magnitude of 15.6.

sources.summary: For H 1426+428, the paper fits its spectrum and finds the electromagnetic-cascade best fit nearly matches absorption-only, with cascade flux only several percent and limited line-of-sight room.

quotes.quote: Finally, fits for the source H 1426+428 are shown in Fig. fig19 . For this object, the formal best fit for the electromagnetic cascade model is practically coincident with the case of the absorption-only model. The contribution of the cascade component to the total flux in this case is vastly subdominant, only about several percent. In this respect, it is interesting that Furniss et al. estimated V_ LOS 0 for this source, i.e. that there is small room on the line of sight where electromagnetic cascade could develop. Nevertheless, we also present two fits for the case of dominant cascade contribution at low energy ( E < 1 TeV ) for completeness.

sources.summary: The paper finds 1ES 1426+428 has a flat X-ray spectrum extending toward 100 keV, requiring contamination checks, and interprets it as an extreme BL Lac with a very high synchrotron peak.

quotes.quote: 1ES 1426+428 The spectrum of this object is very well fitted by a single flat power--law model, with galactic absorption: there is no clear sign of bending in the shape of the residuals (see Fig. 10). Broken power--law models do not statistically improve the fit, and moreover give values of and both flatter than unity [ they are also only marginally different ( - 0.1) ] . The data in the PDS band are particularly important for this source, in order to assess or not the presence of a steeper component ( >1 ) that would reveal the position of the synchrotron peak.

For 1ES 1426+428, instead, the flat X--ray spectrum up to 100 keV constrains the synchrotron peak to lie near or even above that value, and makes this source the third object ever found with such high peak frequencies, after Mkn 501 and 1ES 2344+514.

Flux variations, however, should not be the only criteria to trigger the observations for the most extreme states: 1ES 1426+428 proves that the shift in the peak frequency can be de--coupled from high flux states in the soft X--ray band.

sources.summary: The review describes H 1426 + 428 as a BL Lac object whose HEGRA TeV spectrum shows absorption features useful for extragalactic background light studies.

quotes.quote: Fortunately, the BL Lac object H 1426 + 428 at the fourfold redshift z = 0.129 could recently be detected and its TeV spectrum measured by HEGRA is within the errors consistent with the characteristic absorption features expected . The source spectrum was assumed to be E^ 1.92 , consistent with X-ray synchrotron observations (Fig. eps10 ). The absorption feature consists in a strong hardening of the observed spectrum between about 2 and 5 TeV (Fig. eps9ab ). One can also fit a power law to the HEGRA data alone -- with no further justification than the simplicity of a 2-parameter straight line -- obtaining a somewhat lower overall statistical significance. However, this power law is quite flat and it deviates therefore strongly from a steep absorbed spectrum at energies below 1 TeV.

sources.summary: The paper treats H1426+428 as another TeV blazar constraining the anisotropic-space model, deriving lattice-scale bounds from its 5.37 TeV photons.

quotes.quote: The same problem also holds for Mrk421, 1ES1959+650 and H1426+428 . The observed highest energies of the photons from these blazars are listed in T1 .

Among them, a new bounds to a^ 1 can be derived from the 5.37 TeV photons from H1426+428, where we found 10^ 3.14 GeV = 1.4 TeV < a^ 1 10^ 9.5 GeV. Note here that the angle between Mrk501 and 1H1426+428 is 27 ^ , so the limits of two blazars cannot be combined into a single expression trivially. Taking into account the effects of the universe expansion, we have found bounds of a^ 1 : 2.5 TeV < a^ 1 10^ 10.5 GeV for a 20 TeV photon from Mrk501, and 1.4 TeV < a^ 1 10^ 9.5 GeV for a 5.37 TeV photon from H1426+428, under an assumption that the blazar gamma-ray travels to the discretized direction.

sources.summary: The paper models H1426+428 as a TeV blazar, constraining its SSC emission-region conditions and using its redshift-dependent TeV attenuation to test EBL models.

quotes.quote: We set constraints on the physical condition of H1426+428 with the synchrotron-self-Compton model applying several EBL models. We find that the emission region of H1426+428 is moving toward us with the bulk Lorentz factor of 20 and that its magnetic field strength is 0.1 G.

In this paper we make models of H1426+428 (at redshift z = 0.129) and estimate the intensity of EBL, solving the kinetic equations of electrons and photons to obtain the intrinsic SED of blazars, so that the observed SED is consistent with the electron injection and cooling processes.

Because the redshift of H1426+428 is 0.129, the effects of the attenuation of TeV rays should be very large and the object can be a good test against various EBL models. cos03 inferred the intrinsic spectrum of H1426+428 using the model spectrum of EBL given by pri01 , who employed the semi-analytical models of galaxy formation, as well as a EBL model spectrum designed to match the high fluxes below 2 m aha02 .

sources.summary: The paper describes VERITAS observations of H1426+428, a known BL Lac GeV/TeV source, finding only a marginal excess and deriving upper limits for source counts.

quotes.quote: This BL Lac object H1426+428 is a well established and well studied source of GeV/TeV rays . When the source was first detected, a GeV/TeV-flux of 20 of the flux from the Crab Nebula was reported . The analysis reveals a marginal excess (see Fig. 1). For this source and all other sources, Bayesian upper limits on the number of source counts are derived for a confidence interval of 99 following the recipe described in .

sources.summary: The paper reports that 1H 1426+428 is much brighter than Mrk 421, broadly shares its spectral trends, but has a June 2001 pointing with unusual peak-energy behavior.

quotes.quote: The source 1H 1426+428 has a similar behaviour to Mrk 421 in the E_p^*-L_p^* and E_p^*-b plots (see Fig. 2), even though it is an order of magnitude brighter than the latter.

The observation of on 16 June 2001 appears to confirm the statistical trend in the E_p^*-b plane, but in the E_p^*-L_p^* plane it lies in a different position relative to other pointings. As shown in Fig. 3, during this particular pointing, 1H 1426+428 shifted its SED peak energy without large variation of L_p^* , at variance with the following ones by . We find correlation coefficients r_ log =0.72 for the E_p^*-L_p^* relation, and r_ log =-0.47 for the E_p^*-b one; these confirm the similarity to Mrk 421 and to PKS 0548-322.

sources.summary: The paper reports simultaneous optical, X-ray, and VHE observations of blazar 1ES1426+428, finding intermediate X-ray activity, no significant MAGIC detection, lower VHE flux, and rising optical flux.

quotes.quote: We performed a simultaneous multiwavelength observation of the blazar 1ES1426+428 in May-June 2008 with the KVA telescope for the optical band, the Suzaku and Swift satellites for the X-rays and with the MAGIC telescope for the very high energy (VHE) band (E > 100 GeV). The analysis of the X-ray data showed that the source was in an intermediate state of activity, while MAGIC could not detect significant VHE gamma-ray emission from the source. This indicates that the VHE flux was more than one order of magnitude lower compared to historical detections. The optical flux increased by 20 during the MAGIC observations.

sources.summary: The paper calls J1428 + 4240 an extreme HBL with a high synchrotron peak, core-plus-halo radio structure, and mostly non-variable new optical monitoring.

quotes.quote: J1428 + 4240 (H1426 + 428, z=0.129 ) This weak TeV source has its synchrotron emission peak at the highest frequency known for any blazar; hence termed as ``extreme'' HBL (Aharonian et al. \ 2002; also, Costamante et al. \ 2001). Its radio structure consists of a core surrounded by a faint halo (Giroletti et al. \ 2004). The light curves presented here are its first intranight optical monitoring observations. The C_ eff values indicated it was non-variable on all 3 nights we monitored it; however, on application of the F test it was found to be variable on 1 of the 3 nights (Table 3).

sources.summary: The paper treats H 1426+428 as an HBL with strong TeV emission detected by Whipple, earlier non-detections, archival broadband SED data, and Fermi/LAT spectrum constraints.

quotes.quote: H 1426+428. A HBL object with the strongest TeV emission was detected in 2000 and 2001 by Whipple with an integral flux of (2.04 0.35) 10^ 11 cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 above 280 GeV (Horan et al. 2002). The object was monitored by Whipple from 1995 to 1998 during a general blazar survey, but no statistical signal was detected. No simultaneous broadband data are found and the data of broadband SED are from Wolter et al. (2008). The spectrum butterfly of the Fermi /LAT observations (Abdo et al. 2009) is also taken into account.

sources.summary: The paper identifies H 1426+428 as one of seven high-frequency-peaked blazars whose optically thick measurements contribute comparably to the excess, with a 3.5-sigma leave-one-out relevance.

quotes.quote: The seven sources with measurements extending to >2 contribute about equally to the overall excess. Removing each source spectrum and re-calculating the significance given in the following list provides an estimate on the relevance of each source: Mkn 421 ( S( Mkn 421 )=3.8 ), Mkn 501 ( S( Mkn 501 )=3.7 ), H 1426+428 ( S( H 1426+428 )=3.5 ), 1ES1101-232 ( S( 1ES1101-232 )=2.8 ), 1ES0347-121 ( S( 1ES0347-121 )=4.6 ), 1ES0414+009 ( S( 1ES0414+009 )=3.6 ), 3C279 ( S( 3C279 )=3.8 ). The result demonstrates that the different objects (6 high frequency peaked Blazars and 1 flat spectrum radio quasar) at widely different redshifts ( z=0.031 , 0.034 , 0.129 , 0.186 , 0.188 , 0.287 , and 0.536 ) contribute about equally to the overall significance. A systematic effect based upon the type of source or its distance is not evident.

sources.summary: The review treats 1ES 1426+428 as a first beyond-z=0.1 TeV source whose HEGRA spectrum helped reveal the expected EBL-induced flattening feature.

quotes.quote: The first meaningful limits in this band could be derived with the HEGRA data on 1ES 1426+428, which was the first object detected above 1 TeV beyond z=0.1. The HEGRA spectrum was significantly harder than measured below 1 TeV . To avoid <1.5 or hard upturns in the 1ES 1426+428 TeV spectrum, the EBL cannot be as high as 60 \ around 1.5 , or have a slope much flatter than ^ 1 between 2 and 10 \ , as in the EBL calculation by Ref. malkanstecker01 . Combining the data of all experiments available at the time (HEGRA, WHIPPLE, CAT), the overall 0.2-TeV spectrum of 1ES 1426+428 showed for the first time the typical flattening feature expected from a starlight-dominated EBL spectrum, instead of the further steepening expected from a flatter EBL spectrum (see Fig. flattening ).

sources.summary: The paper discusses H 1426+428 through its May 2001 outburst, 2002 state, and post-2004 behavior, with changing peak energy and comparable peak luminosity.

quotes.quote: H 1426+428 went through a large outburst in May 2001, which was excluded by one of our observation selection criteria (i.e., the logpar model was not required to fit the spectra as the synchrotron peak was much larger than the \ spectral coverage; see Section sec:spec_ana ); during the peak of this flare, its peak energy and peak luminosity were estimated to be larger than 25 keV and 10 ^ 45.6 erg \ s ^ 1 , respectively. In 2002, its peak energy ranged between 5 and 25 keV, and peak luminosity ranged between 10 ^ 44.6 and 10 ^ 45.3 erg \ s ^ 1 . After 2004, its peak energy ranged between 0.9 and 5 keV, which is lower than that in 2002, but its peak luminosity ranged between 10 ^ 44.7 and 10 ^ 45.3 erg \ s ^ 1 , which is quite similar to that in 2002. Our result is consistent with that of .

sources.summary: HEGRA observed H1426+428 as a TeV BL Lac object, reanalyzed its spectra, and found EBL absorption strongly affects the 700 GeV to 10 TeV observations.

quotes.quote: The detection and spectral measurements of H1426+428 by the CAT, and VERITAS groups 2002A&A...391L..25D,2002ApJ...580..104P indicate a steep spectrum between 250 GeV and 1 TeV (well described by a power law dN/dE E^ n with a photon index of n=3.66 0.41 for the CAT and n=3.54 0.27 for the VERITAS measurements) whereas at higher energies, a detection has been claimed only by the HEGRA group based upon observations carried out in 1999 and 2000 with n=2.6 0.6 in the following Paper I 2002A&A...384L..23A 2002A&A...384L..23A Paper I .

The wide energy range from 700 GeV up to 10 TeV covered by the HEGRA energy spectrum of H1426+428 offers the unique opportunity to probe the energy region where the source spectrum is heavily absorbed by pair-production processes on the NIR/MIR part of the EBL.

In summary, independent of the details of the model for the EBL, the HEGRA observations of H1426+428 between 700 GeV and 10 TeV are strongly affected by absorption of the EBL between 1-15 m. The possible strong absorption combined with the observable flux at TeV energies implies an intrinsic spectrum which dominates the broad-band SED of H1426+428.

sources.summary: The paper includes 1H 1430+423 despite marginal S2 evidence, arguing the S2 detection is confused but the source is clearly extragalactic.

quotes.quote: 1H 1430+423: This AGN is listed as an S1 and S2 detection in the WFC 2RE survey (Pye 1995) but only as an S1 detection in the original WFC RE survey (Pounds 1993). Our own inspection of the relevant WFC survey image confirms that any detection in the S2 band is at best very marginal (see Figure 2). The S2 detection in the 2RE processing appears to be a result of confusion with the high background signal in this field. Since this source is clearly of extragalactic origin, and notwithstanding selection criterion (2) above, we have included it in our sample.

sources.summary: The paper notes 1ES 1426+426 as a famous extreme high-energy peaked BL Lac with bright X-ray emission, hard gamma-ray spectrum, and a corrected synchrotron peak estimate.

quotes.quote: ID=3403 This is the famous EHBL 1ES 1426+426, which has been extensively studied in X-rays. Indeed, its SED reveals an extremely bright X-ray flux and an exceptionally hard ray spectrum, clearly identifying the source as an EHBL. The value _ peak =20.8 we found is clearly overestimated; the XMM-Newton and ASDC data allow us to position it at _ peak 17 -18.

sources.summary: The paper describes 1ES 1426+428 as a VHE BL Lac source in its monitoring sample and reports its optical/radio flux level, variability trends, and correlation behavior.

quotes.quote: 1ES 1426+428 : One of the faintest sources in both bands showing moderate variability in both bands. The surprisingly large modulation index in the radio band is possibly artefact of the few outliers. There is no significant trend in the radio data, we find no significant correlation between optical and radio light curves and polynomial subtraction does not improve the rms of the optical light curve (see Fig. Fig:lc22 ).

sources.summary: The paper treats 1ES 1426+428 as a known TeV emitter, finds a firm 2012 MAGIC detection, and uses Swift BAT data to constrain its extreme synchrotron peak.

quotes.quote: All the considered sources have not been detected by IACTs except for 1ES 1426+428, which was first discovered as a TeV emitter by Whipple 2002AA...384L..23A and recently detected with the VERITAS telescopes 2017ApJ...835..288A .
In addition, a firm detection of the known TeV emitter 1ES 1426+428 was found in the 2012 dataset.

In the case of 1ES 1426+428 the average 14-195 keV spectrum obtained with Swift BAT in 105 months of survey from 2004 to 2013 swiftbat was included in the archival (gray) SED and clearly constrain the peak position in the extreme region, above 10 ^ 17 Hz.

sources.summary: The paper studies H1426+428 as a higher-redshift TeV blazar whose low observed flux and inferred intrinsic spectrum are strongly affected by gamma-ray absorption.

quotes.quote: Inferring a spectrum for H1426+428 is difficult owing to poor statistics. Inspecting the range of the likely intrinsic spectrum, the low flux seems to be the consequence of heavy absorption owing to the comparatively high redshift of the source (four times larger than for the other blazars (Aharonian et al. 2002, Petry et al. 2002, Costamante et al 2003). The expected exponential cut-off energy at a redshift of z=0.129 obtains values of 100-200 GeV, i.e. at energies below the threshold energy of the detecting instruments (Whipple, HEGRA).

sources.summary: The paper treats H 1426+428 as an intermediate-distance TeV blazar prototype whose spectrum excludes many EBL shapes and gives mid-infrared constraints.

quotes.quote: The prototype for this category is the spectrum from H 1426+428 at a redshift of z = 0.129 , with energies ranging from 700 GeV up to 12 TeV.
Intermediate distance, wide energy range: H1426+428 Using the H 1426+428 energy spectrum 5 571 772 EBL shapes (corresponding to 69.09 of all shapes) are excluded.
H 1426+428, situated at an intermediate distance, provides some constraints on the EBL density in the MIR (between 1 and 15 m), connecting the upper limits derived from 1ES 1101-232 and Mkn 501.

gmw_00000529.name: Mrk 180
gmw_00000529.overview: Mrk 180 is a nearby BL Lacertae object [arXiv:2212.07331] associated with a giant elliptical galaxy [arXiv:0711.1158]. It features an asymmetric core-halo radio structure [arXiv:0711.1158] with an eastward jet [arXiv:1205.2399], and it exhibits long-term increasing trends and flares in both optical and radio bands [arXiv:1606.06431].

sources.summary: The paper presents the first radio-to-VHE campaign on Mrk 180, reporting prior VHE and Fermi status, modest optical variability, X-ray variability, and a factor-nine X-ray flare.

quotes.quote: Mrk 180 is a nearby (z = 0.046) HBL discovered at VHE by MAGIC in 2006 following an optical high state . No second VHE detection has been published up to now. EGRET did not report a detection of the source , but it is included in the Fermi LAT 1-year Catalog . Flux variations in the optical are modest due to the strong host galaxy, outshining the core . In X-rays, variability by a factor 3 at 1 keV have been reported .

An unprecedented X-ray flare with a flux increase of factor 9 was detected for Mrk 180, accompanied by a significant shift of the synchrotron peak to energies > 5 keV. The applied SSC models could not fit the corresponding simultaneous SED satisfactorily, indicating that more emission regions or different physical mechanisms (like EC or hadronic models) are needed to reproduce the behaviour of the source. Variability could be established at all wavelength regimes with a hint of inter-band correlations.

sources.summary: The paper models Mrk 180 as a BL Lac object, using long-term multiwavelength data to test leptonic and lepto-hadronic SED explanations and assess UHECR association.

quotes.quote: Markarian (Mrk) 180 is a BL Lacertae (BL Lac) object located at a redshift of 0.045 and a potential candidate for high-energy cosmic ray acceleration. We have analyzed the Fermi Large Area Telescope ( Fermi LAT) ray data of Mrk 180 collected over a period of 12.8 years and found no significant enhancement in the flux from the long-term ray light curve. We have also analyzed Swift X-ray, ultraviolet optical, and X-ray Multi-Mirror Mission (XMM-Newton) data to construct the multi-wavelength spectral energy distribution (SED).

The leptonic modeling is not sufficient to explain the multi-wavelength SED of Mrk 180. We have considered two lepto-hadronic models to improve the fit to the observed data points. The first model involves interactions of UHECRs injected by Mrk 180 with the radiation backgrounds, and in the second model, we have considered interactions of relativistic protons in the jet with the cold protons. The latter gives a slightly better fit to the data, however, more observational data is necessary to explain the radiation mechanisms in Mrk 180, as our results show large values of residuals in all the cases.

In this study we do not find any UHECR event from Mrk 180 contributing to the TA hotspot, hence, we conclude that for conservative values of EGMF, Mrk 180 is disfavoured as a source contributing to the TA hotspot, however, in future with more UHECR data it would be possible to investigate further on their association.

sources.summary: The paper studies Mrk 180 as a BL Lac object, modeling its multiwavelength SED and testing leptonic and hadronic scenarios for gamma rays and UHECRs.

quotes.quote: Mrk 180 is a BL Lac object, located at a redshift= 0.045, R.A.= 174.11008 deg, Decl.= 70.1575 deg, discovered by a Swiss-origin astronomer Fritz Zwicky. In March 2006, VHE ray emission was detected for the first time 2006outburst from this source, triggered by an optical burst. This source was monitored by several telescopes e.g. Fermi-LAT, Swift, Major Atmospheric Gamma Imaging Cherenkov Telescope (MAGIC), XMM-Newton, Monitoring of jets in Active Galactic Nuclei with VLBA Experiments (MOJAVE), KVA, ASM. An earlier study He identified Mrk 180 as a possible source of UHECRs in the context of explaining the origin of the TA hotspot at E>57 EeV.

The 12.8 years long, 30-day binned Fermi-LAT ray light curve Fig. fig:Mrk180_LC does not show any significant variation in the ray flux. There are a few data points with high ray flux with large error bars, so further temporal study is not feasible in this case. We have constructed the MWSED with analyzed and archival data (as mentioned in sec. sec:data ) which covers radio to ray wavebands. The MWSED has been modelled with three different models, discussed below.

sources.summary: The paper reports MAGIC detection of Mkn 180 after an optical brightening, finding a significant VHE signal, no night-to-night variability, and a measured power-law energy spectrum.

quotes.quote: The AGN Mkn 180 (1ES 1133+704) is a well-known HBL at a redshift of z=0.045 . Previous attempts to detect VHE rays only resulted in upper limits . The observation of Mkn 180 was triggered by a brightening of the source in the optical on March 23, 2006, detected by the KVA telescope.

The signal of 165 excess events was found with a significance of 5.5 . No evidence for flux variability between nights was found. The measured energy spectrum of Mkn 180 is shown in Fig. fig_spectrum . A fit by a power law gives a photon index =3.3 0.7 . The observed integral flux above 200 GeV is 11 of the Crab Nebula flux.

sources.summary: The paper links 1133+704 to Mrk 180, notes its candidate TeV BL Lac status and core-halo structure, and reports longitudinally polarized core and jet emission.

quotes.quote: 1133+704 1133+704 is associated with the giant elliptical galaxy Mrk 180. X-ray emission was detected by the Einstein and HEAO-1 observatories Hutter80 , and the object is a candidate TeV BL Lac Costamante02 . The kpc-scale radio structure has an asymmetric core-halo morphology AntonucciUlv85,LaurentMuehleisen93,Giroletti04a . The previous global VLBI observations of Kollgaard96 show a jet to the southeast.

A high degree of polarization was found in the inner jet, but with the corresponding vectors bearing no obvious relationship to the jet direction. Our new VLBI image (Fig.  fig:1133 ) reveals polarized emission in both the core and jet, with the predominant implied B field being longitudinal.

sources.summary: The paper identifies Mrk 180 as one of three optically triggered MAGIC discoveries of VHE blazars, notes its detection among optical flares, follow-up observations, and HBL classification.

quotes.quote: In 11 cases the source was detected by MAGIC. These 11 flares were detected in 9 sources: Mrk 421, 1ES 1959+650, PG 1553+113, 3C 66A, 3C 279, BL Lac, Mrk 180, 1ES 1011+496, S5 0716+714 (see Fig. 2).
For Mrk 180 the analysis of the follow-up observation from October-December 2008 is still ongoing.

The optically triggered Target of Opportunity observations have resulted in the discovery of three new VHE ray emitting blazars. Mrk 180 and 1ES 1011+496 are classified to be HBLs, while S5 0716+714 is classified as IBL or even LBL. However, the BL Lac population is continuous rather than bimodal or trimodal (see, e.g. ), and in all of the three discovered sources the synchrotron peak frequency is not too far from the optical band (see e.g. publicly available data at asdc.asi.it/blazars and Fig. 6. in ).

sources.summary: The paper presents Markarian 180 as one of the brightest TeV HBLs observed with the VLBA, with an eastward jet, four Gaussian components, and a polarized core.

quotes.quote: We observed the five TeV blazars 1ES 1101-232, Markarian 180, 1ES 1218+304, PG 1553+113, and H 2356-309 at five epochs each from 2006 to 2009 with the VLBA under observation code BP146, at frequencies from 5 to 22 GHz, for a total of 25 new VLBA images produced for this paper.
Polarization observations were made of Markarian 180 and PG 1553+113 (the two brightest sources) at the first epoch.

Markarian 180 Markarian 180 (1133+704, z =0.045) is among the two brightest of these five blazars in total radio flux density, and we observed it at a frequency of 22 GHz. At this frequency, a jet extends several mas to the east from a 40 mJy core. This jet is modeled by four Gaussian components at most epochs. VLBI polarization observations were done at the first epoch, and they showed that the VLBI core is 4 polarized, with the electric vector position angle aligned with the jet position angle.

sources.summary: The paper describes Mrk 180 as a nearby VHE-detected BL Lac with a bright host galaxy, little optical polarimetry, low polarization measurements, and sparse RINGO2 campaign coverage.

quotes.quote: Mrk 180 Mrk 180 (z=0.045 falco1999 ) is a nearby BL Lac object that was detected in VHE rays in 2006 (MJD 53795) albert2006 . The source has a bright host galaxy and is little studied in optical polarimetry. presented an optical degree of polarisation measurement of 2.4 which is consistent with the results we see from early 2012 (MJD 56000) when our observations begin. The RINGO2 data were taken as part of a multi-wavelength campaign which was started in 2008 (MJD 54500) rugamer2011 . We have few data points for the RINGO2 period of observation, polarimetry from the KVA is unavailable as are Fermi flux data.

sources.summary: The paper discusses Mkn 180 as an HBL with MAGIC VHE detection during optical outburst, soft spectrum, no variability, and a 2008 multiwavelength SED.

quotes.quote: Mkn 180. A HBL object was detected with VHE gamma-ray emission by MAGIC during an optical outburst in 2006. The integral flux above 200 GeV is (2.3 0.7) 10^ 11 cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 and corresponds to 11 Crab flux. The observed spectrum was rather soft with a photon index of 3.3 0.7 , and no variability was found (Albert et al. 2006a). A multi-wavelength campaign in 2008 was performed (Rugamer et al. 2011).

sources.summary: The paper presents Mrk 180 as a plausible TA-hotspot source, noting comparable posterior probability, TeV gamma-ray flux, distance suppression relative to M82, and enough hadronic-model energy for UHECRs.

quotes.quote: The starburst galaxy M82 has the largest relative probability 99.8 , and the star-forming galaxy UGC 05101 and the blazar Mrk 180 also have comparably large relative probabilities of being the source.

Compared to M82, the blazar Mrk 180 is much further from Earth, the observed UHECRs are suppression by a factor of 95 . The flux of TeV gamma-ray emission from Mrk 180 is as high as 0.11 crab  at 200  GeV. Assuming a hadronic model  and the accelerated cosmic ray spectrum index 2 , Mrk 180 can also be energetic enough to produce UHECRs at the hotspot.

sources.summary: The paper describes Mrk 180 as a VHE BL Lac source in its monitoring sample and reports its optical/radio flux level, variability trends, and correlation behavior.

quotes.quote: Mrk 180 : The source is close to the median flux density of the sample in both optical and radio bands. Very significant (highest significance within our sample) increasing trends extending through several years are found in both optical and radio light curves. Besides this, both light curves show flares. However, the DCF curve is really flat, with several subsequent points above 3 , suggesting that the significant correlation we find is mainly due to common trend seen in the optical and radio light curves.

sources.summary: The paper finds Mrk 180 in a 2008-2009 high-flux state with changing peak energy and luminosity, followed by a lower 2015 state consistent with earlier work.

quotes.quote: Mrk 180 was in a high-flux state between 2008 and 2009. During this period, its peak energy changed between 0.5 and 3 keV, while peak luminosity changed between 10 ^ 43.5 and 10 ^ 44.5 erg \ s ^ 1 . In 2015, it was in a relatively low-flux state with peak luminosity decreasing to 10 ^ 43 erg \ s ^ 1 . Our result is consistent with that of .

gmw_00000530.name: PKS 2005-489
gmw_00000530.overview: PKS 2005-489 is a bright, high-frequency-peaked BL Lacertae blazar [arXiv:0911.2709, arXiv:2303.15589] hosted by a giant elliptical galaxy [arXiv:2303.15589]. It exhibits extreme X-ray flux variations [arXiv:2303.15589] and persistent very-high-energy gamma-ray emission [arXiv:0911.2709], with its radio jet showing an elongated core and diffuse southwest emission [arXiv:2310.10206].

sources.summary: The paper monitors PKS 2005 489 as a bright high-frequency-peaked BL Lac object, detecting persistent VHE emission, X-ray variability, and evolving two-hump spectral energy distribution behavior.

quotes.quote: A strong VHE signal, 17 total, from PKS 2005 489 was detected during the four years of HESS observations (90.3 hrs live time). The integral flux above the average analysis threshold of 400 GeV is 3 of the flux observed from the Crab Nebula and varies weakly on time scales from days to years. The average VHE spectrum measured from 300 GeV to 5 TeV is characterized by a power law with a photon index, = 3.20 0.16_ stat 0.10_ syst .

The SED of PKS 2005 489, constructed for the first time with contemporaneous data on both humps, shows significant evolution. The large flux variations in the X-ray band, coupled with weak or no variations in the VHE band and a similar spectral behavior, suggest the emergence of a new, separate, harder emission component in September 2005.

PKS 2005 489 is one of the brightest BL Lac objects, at all wavelengths, in the Southern Hemisphere. It was initially discovered as a strong radio source in the Parkes 2.7 GHz survey discovery_paper and later identified as a BL Lac object BL_id . It belongs to the complete 1-Jy radio catalog stickel , and is one of the few extragalactic objects detected in the EUV band euv .

sources.summary: PKS 2005 489 is presented as a TeV-detected BL Lac with an unusual X-ray high state; the paper favors an additional jet component, possibly from magnetic reconnection.

quotes.quote: is a well-known, bright southern BL Lac object that has been detected up to TeV energies.
