We therefore conclude that we can neither make a decisive choice between the published values, nor improve their error bars, which are large enough to overlap.

sources.summary: The paper treats HE 1104 1805 as a lensed double quasar and revises its time delay to 0.85 years using non-interpolation analyses of poorly sampled lightcurves.

quotes.quote: We present a new determination of the time delay of the gravitational lens system HE 1104 1805 ('Double Hamburger') based on a previously unpublished dataset. We argue that the previously published value of t_ A-B =0.73 years was affected by a bias of the employed method. We determine a new value of t_ A-B =0.85 0.05 years (2 confidence level), using six different techniques based on non interpolation methods in the time domain. The result demonstrates that even in the case of poorly sampled lightcurves, useful information can be obtained with regard to the time delay.

The double quasar HE 1104 1805 at a redshift of z_Q = 2.319 was originally discovered by Wisotzki et al. (1993). The two images with (original) B magnitudes of 16.70 and 18.64 are separated by 3 195 (Kochanek et al. 1998). The spectral line ratios and profiles turned out to be almost identical between the two images, but image A has a distinctly harder continuum.

sources.summary: The paper describes HE1104 -1805 as a two-image gravitationally lensed quasar monitored by OGLE, reporting that the image light curves give a 157 21 day time delay.

quotes.quote: The gravitationally lensed quasar HE1104 -1805 with relatively large separation between two images A and B (about 3 ) was discovered by Wisotzki (1993). The object has been included to the list of regularly monitored objects by the Optical Gravitational Lensing Experiment (OGLE) at the beginning of the second phase of the project -- OGLE-II (Udalski, Kubiak and Szyma n ski 1997) in August 1997. The main goal of this sub-project of the OGLE survey was a determination of the time delay of HE1104 -1805. The quasar was regularly observed up to the end of the 1999/2000 observing season (August 2000) with frequency of one observation per 5--7 days (except for three month period, August--October, each year when the quasar was not visible from the Earth).

The long term monitoring of HE1104 -1805 gravitational lens during the OGLE survey has finally accomplished its original goal. We determined the time delay between the light curve of image A and B to be equal to 157 21 days where the error is the formal error of ^2 minimalization. In practice it can be somewhat smaller.

sources.summary: HE1104-1805 was monitored for a time delay, but uncorrelated microlensing variations and revised Wise/OGLE delays made it problematic for H_0 work.

quotes.quote: Seven years ago the present author prevailed upon Andrzej Udalski to monitor HE1104-1805 for a time delay with the OGLE telescope . The OGLE group was at that time focussing exclusively on LMC and galactic bulge microlensing, but HE1104-1805 is at a very different right ascension. Three years of superb OGLE data were obtained in which no believable correlated variations were observed. But there were appreciable uncorrelated variations, most of which were attributable to the brighter of the two images.

But by then had begun monitoring HE1104-1805 at the Wise Observatory. They used our data in conjunction with theirs to give a time delay that differed by a factor of two from the one used in figure fig:courbin . After Ofek and Maoz published their delay, reduced the remaining OGLE data, confirming their value and giving the lightcurves in figure fig:he1104 .

sources.summary: The paper describes HE1104-1805 as a galaxy-scale strong lens, giving its discovery context, lens or source properties, image configuration, and relevant environmental or model information for the survey.

quotes.quote: HE1104-1805 (herein HE1104) was serendipitously discovered by wisotzki1993 as a doubly imaged radio-quiet quasar at z_ s =2.319 with image separation 3 . The lens redshift z_l=0.729 0.001 was measured by lidman . Based on its spectrum and optical colors, the galaxy is likely an elliptical. HE1104 is unusual because the brighter image is closer to the lens galaxy, and lens models of the system require fairly large shear 0.125 to 0.142 . The time delay t = 161 7 days was measured by ofek2 , who also detected residual variation attributed to microlensing.

sources.summary: The simulations identify HE1104 as the hardest WFC3-IR lensed AGN host to model, but still recover host luminosity with residual uncertainty below 0.5 mag.

quotes.quote: HE1104 The results are summarized in Fig. fig:bias_HE1104 . Panels (a), (b), and () summarize the bias, i.e. the difference between inferred and input value of each parameter, allowing n to float in the range 1-4 (filled circles). The bias when n is kept fixed to the input value is represented as filled stars.

As we will discuss later, HE1104 is the hardest system to model among the systems with WFC3-IR imaging, owing to the fact that the lens configuration is a double - and thus we have considerably less information than in quads - and that the AGN images outshine the host galaxy by 2-4 magnitudes. In spite of all these challenges, the simulations here show us the residual uncertainty is within 0.5 magnitudes ( < 0.2 dex), whether fixing n or not, which is much smaller than the uncertainty on \ and thus acceptable for our purposes.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes HE1104-1805 as a lensed double quasar, giving its redshift, lens redshift, image separation, time delay, GALFIT photometry, and R-band difference images.

quotes.quote: HE1104-1805 The lensed double quasar HE1104-1805, discovered by Wisotzki_1993 in the Hamburg/ESO survey (HES), is located at redshift z_ Q = 2.32 , while the lensing galaxy has a redshift of z_ L = 0.729 Lidman_2000 . The two images are separated by 3.19 arcsec Lehar_2000 and display a time delay of t_ AB = 152.2 3.0 days , where image B leads Poindexter_2007 .

As an example, in Fig. fig:he1104GALFIT we show PSF photometry results of HE1104-1805 with GALFIT. The image separation is 3.2 arcsec (see Sect. sec:he1104 ) and the lensing galaxy is not visible in our data (but faintly in e.g. HST images).
In Fig. fig:diffimages we show the difference images and the reference image of HE1104-1805 in the R band.

sources.summary: The paper studies HE 1104 1805 as a lensed radio-quiet quasar, revealing the lensing galaxy continuum, constraining the lens redshift, time delay, and microlensing value.

quotes.quote: A new technique for the spatial deconvolution of spectra is applied to near-IR (0.95 - 2.50 ) NTT/SOFI spectra of the lensed, radio-quiet quasar HE 1104 1805. The continuum of the lensing galaxy is revealed between 1.5 and 2.5 . Although the spectrum does not show strong emission features, it is used in combination with previous optical and IR photometry to infer a plausible redshift in the range 0.8<z<1.2 .

From light curves measured over a period of 6 years, they derived a time delay for HE 1104 1805 of t = 0.73 years, with a second possible value of 0.3 years. Although we show that the complex lensing potential involved in HE 1104 1805 makes it difficult to determine H _0 from the time delay, we also show that HE 1104 1805 is probably much more of interest for microlensing studies, provided the lens redshift is better known.

sources.summary: The review reports microlensing constraints for HE 1104 1805, including optical and X-ray half-light radii, lens-star mass range, size-wavelength slope, and compact X-ray emission.

quotes.quote: In Blackburne et al. 2015 we analyze the lightcurves of z = 2.32 quasar HE 1104 1805 using dynamical microlensing magnification patterns. HE 1104 1805 has been observed at a variety of wavelengths ranging from the mid-infrared to X-ray for nearly 20 years. Dynamical microlensing analysis constrains the half-light radius of the accretion disk to ( _ 1/2 /cm) = 16.0 ^ +0.30 _ 0.56 , the half-light radius of the X-ray emission region to have an upper limit of ( _ 1/2 /cm) = 15.33 (95 confidence), a low inclination angle is preferred statistically, the mean mass of the stars in the lensing galaxy ( < M > ) ranges between 0.1 and 0.4 M _ and the slope of the size-wavelength relation r_ 1/2     ^ , is = 1.0 ^ +0.30 _ 0.56 . The majority of the observed continuum X-ray emission is found to originate within 30 r_ g , assuming a black hole estimate of M_ BH = 5.9 10 ^ 8 M _ based on the width of the H line (Assef et al. 2011).

sources.summary: The appendix describes HE 1104-1805 as a double-image lensed quasar with 3.19 arcsec image separation, source redshift 2.319, lens redshift 0.729, variable continuum fluxes, and an off-slit source.

quotes.quote: HE 1104-1805 is a double-image lensed quasar discovered by wisotzkietal93 . The image separation is = 3''.19 , the source redshift is z_s = 2.319 , and the lens redshift is z_l = 0.729 . wisotzkietal95 reported that the continuum flux in both images is highly variable but that the line fluxes do not change, as expected if microlensing is operating. On the acquisition image of HE 1104 there is a second source at 3.5" but it is completely off-slit.

sources.summary: The paper uses HE1104-1805 as a TeVeS lensing test case, finding it requires the largest dark matter component and cannot avoid dark matter within allowed K values.

quotes.quote: In particular, we examine the lensing system HE1104-1805, which within the TeVeS approach (with =0 ) requires the largest amount of dark matter. We find that the variation of the parameter k has considerably smaller effects than that of K .

Within the allowed parameter space 1 K 10^ 5 , 1 k 10^ 5 , implied by rotation curve data and solar system tests of gravity, the amount of dark matter required in the system HE1104-1805 is negligible only for values of K higher than 0.1, which however is excluded by gravitational measurements at solar-system scales.

sources.summary: The paper uses the EFOSC1 spectrum of HE1104--1805, identifies absorption near the line peak, and applies the same interpolation treatment used for HS0810+2554.

quotes.quote: HE1104--1805 --- We use the EFOSC1 ESO 3.6m telescope UV/optical spectrum of wisotzki95 . The \ profile shows a small amount of absorption near the peak of the line, similar to that of the HS0810+2554 \ profile. We therefore apply the same treatment to this line as to the HS0810+2554 profile, and find similarly consistent results with or without interpolation.

sources.summary: The paper reports lower-significance microlensing differences in HE 1104-1805, C IV wing changes, and prominent intrinsic variability; it places the system in the intrinsic-variability-dominated group.

quotes.quote: Only one system (HE 0435-1223) shows a small change (0.11 mag) with S/N > 2 in the selected integration window for the blue wing as defined in Figure em1 . Visually inspecting the data, we find changes in the extreme red wings of SBS 0909+532 and SDSS J1206+0118. These changes could have their origin in the UV Fe II blend 2861-2917 located redward of Mg II, showing up with different intensities for different quasar spectra (see Kova2015 ). At odds with the results obtained from the limited sample presented in Paper I, we find, on the basis of more available epochs, that the CIII ] line seems to be significantly more affected by microlensing than the Mg II line, indicating that it arises from a more compact region inside the BLR. We detect microlensing at the 2 level within the defined integration windows in four systems (SDSS J0246-0825, HE 0435-1223, SDSS J1155+6346, WFI 2033-4723), whereas evidence of intrinsic variability (at the 2 level) can only be found in one system (Q0957+561). Also, the systems SDSS 1004-4112, SDSS J1155+6346, and HE 1104-1805 show microlensing differences at a smaller S/N ( 1.5). Notice the impact on the statistics of the quadruple systems HE 0435-1223 and SDSS J1004+4112, which have been observed in four epochs.
In the case of C IV we have possibly detected prominent intrinsic variability for three systems (Q 0142-100, HE 1104-1805, and HE 2149-2745).

We derive robust estimates of both, intrinsic variability and amplitude of microlensing between the lensed images, and consistently separated a group of five objects dominated by microlensing (SBS 0909+532, FBQS J0951+2635, SDSS J1004+4112, SDSS J1206+4332, and SDSS J1339+1310) and another group of three systems in which intrinsic variability prevails (Q 0142-100, HE 1104-1805, and WFI 2033-4723). Two systems (HE 0435-1223 and HE 2149-2745) seem to be hybrid cases with both microlensing and intrinsic variability present. Finally, the case of QSO 0957+561 may be explained by intrinsic variability combined with the large time-delay between the images plus a possible contribution from microlensing.

gmw_00000543.name: HE2149-2745
gmw_00000543.overview: HE2149-2745 is a doubly lensed broad absorption line quasar [arXiv:1112.0005, arXiv:1503.02074] that exhibits intrinsic variability [arXiv:1101.1917, arXiv:2107.06227]. The system is gravitationally lensed by an elliptical galaxy [arXiv:1112.0005, arXiv:1503.02074] and is affected by chromatic microlensing [arXiv:1206.0731, arXiv:2107.06227].

sources.summary: The paper reanalyzes HE 2149-2745 V- and I-band light curves and finds competing 70-85 and 100-110 day delays too unstable for a reliable measurement.

quotes.quote: HE 2149-2745 We reanalysed the data set made available by 2002yCat..33910481B . These data consist of two light curves, one in the V band and one in the I band, as shown in Fig. fig:HE2149 .

This delay is based on the V band data, but, according to 2002AA...383...71B , agrees with the I band data. Our tests, based on the light curves as such, and using both methods, clearly reveal two possible delays: one around 70-85 days and one around 100-110 days. Unfortunately, the light curves of images A and B show little structure and hardly overlap, except for some points in the second season, when shifting them for a delay of more than 100 days, especially in the I band, which makes it very difficult to choose between the two possibilities.

Moreover, once we add random errors to the model light curve and perform Monte Carlo simulations, we only obtain a forest of small peaks, spread over the entire tested range of 50-140 days, instead of a gaussian distribution around one or two central peaks, demonstrating that these results are highly unstable. Leaving out two outlying data points in the B curve only slightly improves the situation: within the forest of peaks in Fig. HE2149_histo_somme , those in the range 75-85 seem to be slightly more important than those over 100 days. Nevertheless, we cannot derive a reliable time delay from these data sets for this system.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes HE2149-2745 as a two-image lensed quasar, reporting its redshifts, separation, adopted time delay, GALFIT treatment, and agreement with overlapping light curves.

quotes.quote: HE2149-2745 The quasar HE2149-2745, discovered by Wisotzki_1996 in HES as well, with z_ Q = 2.033 and z_ L = 0.603 Eigenbrod_2007 , also has two images that are separated by 1.70 arcsec . The time delay was determined to be t_ AB = 103 12 days by Burud_2002 , where image A leads. Eulaers_2011 reanalysed the same data, report an additional possible delay and conclude that the given delays are not reliable. Using data taken over 15 years by the COSMOGRAIL programme, Millon_2020 find t_ AB = 32.4 ^ +18.3 _ 9.3 days , which is the value we adopt.

This works analogously with the quasar HE2149-2745: Again, two images are visible, they have an angular separation of only 1.7 arcsec (see Sect. sec:he2149 ) and again the lens galaxy is orders of magnitudes fainter and is not accounted for in our GALFIT model to determine the quasar position and PSF.

We can compare the LCO light curve for HE2149-2745 with the R band light curve from Millon_2020 because the observing intervals overlap in the years from 2014 to 2016. They are in good agreement, although the fainter image B displays larger scatter in the LCO data.

sources.summary: HE 2149-2745 shows no microlensing in the authors' spectra, but prior BAL-quasar spectra had chromatic continuum changes now attributed mostly to chromatic microlensing.

quotes.quote: (q) HE 2149-2745 ( z_l = 0.603 , z_s = 2.033 ): Our spectra of this system do not show evidence of ML, however, presented spectra of the two lensed images of this BAL quasar, obtained on 19.11.2000, where they observed chromatic changes of the continuum. They mentioned subtle differences in the line profile of but we fail to detect clear ML signature of the lines using the MmD on these spectra. The MmD is however difficult to perform because of the significant emission blueward of and of the significant chromaticity of the spectral ratio which is sensible even on the small wavelength range covered by the line.

We display in Fig.  fig:MmD the decomposition of using ( ) instead of the average between the blue and red part of the line. The chromatic changes observed in these spectra have been discussed by as possibly caused by differential extinction or chromatic microlensing. Although we may not rule out that differential extinction is present, we are now able to say based on the new spectra that the slope in the spectra of was mostly caused by CML.

sources.summary: The paper treats HE2149 as a robust field/group lens galaxy whose lensing mass exceeds stellar mass enough to require an added cold dark component in MOND/TeVeS.

quotes.quote: However, the other four robust candidates (LBQS1009, HE1104, B1600, HE2149) are located in field/group regions, so that a cold component (CDM) would be required even within the MOND/TeVeS framework. However, the other four galaxies (LBQS1009, HE1104, B1600, HE2149) are located in average density regions (either in the field or in small groups). Hence, for these systems the excess of lensing mass can only be reconciled if an additional, cold, component is added, i.e. CDM.

It was found that, for a range of parameterisations of the MOND and TeVeS theories significant quantities of dark matter are present for at least five galaxies: LBQS1009, HE1104, B1600, HE2149 and Q0957, irrespective of the stellar initial mass function adopted. Although it is noted that Q0957 lives in a high density region -- so that the lensing analysis may be probing scales larger than a typical galaxy halo -- still an additional set of four robust candidates are found (LBQS1009, HE1104, B1600 and HE2149) that pose a serious challenge to these theories.

sources.summary: The paper discusses HE 2149 2745 as a bright lensed quasar and reports a direct lensing-galaxy redshift of 0.603, revising earlier estimates.

quotes.quote: HE 2149 2745 : Wisotzki et al. ( ) found this bright gravitationally lensed quasar at z =2.033 , with two images separated by 1.71 . They gave first estimates of the redshift of the lensing galaxy, refined later by Lopez et al. ( ), who infer a probable redshift range of 0.3-0.5 . Kochanek et al. ( ) give a fundamental plane redshift estimate of 0.37 0.50 . Burud et al. ( ) measure the time delay between the two images ( t=103 12 days) and report a tentative redshift of =0.495 0.010 by cross-correlating the lens spectrum with a template spectrum of an elliptical galaxy, but they notice that the signal-to-noise of the correlation function is poor. Our direct spectroscopic result disagrees with all previous estimates, with the new value of =0.603 0.001 .

sources.summary: The paper describes HE 2149-2745 as a doubly lensed BAL quasar with an elliptical main deflector and a galaxy group along the line of sight.

quotes.quote: HE 2149-2745 (k) . This doubly lensed BAL quasar was discovered in Wisotzki1996 by Wisotzki1996 in the context of the Hamburg/ESO Survey for bright quasars. The main deflector remained undetected until Lopez1998 and is an elliptical galaxy e.g. Cosmograil6 . According to Faure2004 a galaxy group lies along the line of sight to this system.

sources.summary: The paper describes HE2149-2745 as a galaxy-scale strong lens, giving its discovery context, lens or source properties, image configuration, and relevant environmental or model information for the survey.

quotes.quote: HE2149-2745 (herein HE2149) is a doubly imaged BAL quasar at z=2.033 discovered by wisotzki96 as part of the Hamburg/ESO survey of bright quasars. The elliptical galaxy lens redshift z_l=0.495 0.01 was measured by burud , who also determine the time delay between the images t = 103 12 days. Based on the large number of galaxies in the R band image of the HE2149 field, lopez suggest that the lens might be a member of a cluster. my and kurtis , however, only find several groups along the line of sight (at z=0.27, 0.45 , and 0.60 ), none of them associated with the lens.

sources.summary: HE 2149 2745 is described as a doubly imaged broad absorption line quasar with an elliptical lens galaxy, measured time delay, and possible cluster membership.

quotes.quote: HE 2149 2745 (hereafter HE2149) is a doubly imaged broad absorption line quasar at redshift z_s=2.033 , which was discovered by wisotskihe2149-96 in the Hamburg/ESO wide-angle survey for bright quasars. burudhe2149-02 reported a redshift of z_l=0.495 0.01 for the elliptical lens galaxy, consistent with the photometric redshift estimate of z_l=0.43_ 0.06 ^ +0.07 kochanekfundplane-00 . burudhe2149-02 also measured the time delay between the two images to be t = 103 12 days. Based on the large number of red non-stellar objects in R-band images of the field around the lens, lopezhe2149-98 suggested that the lens galaxy might be a member of a cluster.

sources.summary: The paper describes HE 2149 2745 as a double quasar with no reliable microlensing detection; observed long-term light-curve variations depend on interpolation and may be systematic.

quotes.quote: Quasars where no microlensing is confirmed The three quasars where no microlensing variability was reliably detected are the double quasar HE 2149 2745 observed at the Danish 1.5-m telescope, ESO-La Silla (October 1998 -- December 2000), the quadruple quasar RX J0911+0551 observed at the NOT (March 1997 -- April 2001), and the doubly imaged quasar B1600+434 observed at the NOT (April 1998 -- November 1999) (see Table table:1 ). Plots similar to Figs. 1 and 2 are shown in Figs. 3--5. For HE 2149 2745 (Fig. hev ) and RX J0911+0551 (Fig. ) we see some long term variations in the light curve difference but the analysis shows that the slopes differ depending on which of the two light curves are interpolated.

Thus, while we detect intriguing light curve differences and cannot rule out microlensing variability in these systems, this indicates that the detected magnitude changes may also be attributed to photometric or interpolation errors.

sources.summary: The paper finds HE 2149 2745 mostly wavelength-independent, except for a high K-band ratio likely due to intrinsic or microlensing variability.

quotes.quote: HE 2149 2745. The measured flux ratios are consistent with being independent of wavelength across 0.4--3.8 m ( F_B/F_A 0.24 ), except that our K band measurement ( F_B/F_A=0.280 0.006 ) formally disagrees with the others. However, the flux ratio is known to vary with recorded fluctuations at the level of 0.03 2002A&A...383...71B . Since K band corresponds to rest-frame 0.7 m, we conclude that the high K band flux ratio is likely due to intrinsic and/or microlensing variability.

sources.summary: The paper finds HE 2149-2745 is clearly affected by C IV microlensing, shows prominent intrinsic variability, and appears to be a hybrid system with both effects present.

quotes.quote: After consistently separating microlensing from intrinsic variability according to the criteria explained in Section sec2.2 (see also Tables table2 and table3 ), we find that nine objects are clearly affected by microlensing at the 2 level (HE 0435-1223, HS 0818-1227, FBQS J0951+2635, QSO 0957+561, SDSS J1004+4112, SDSS J1138+0314, SDSS J1206+4332, SDSS J1339+1310, HE 2149-2745) and two systems show evidence of intrinsic variability at the 2 level (HE 1104-1805, HE 2149-2745).
In the case of C IV we have possibly detected prominent intrinsic variability for three systems (Q 0142-100, HE 1104-1805, and HE 2149-2745).

We derive robust estimates of both, intrinsic variability and amplitude of microlensing between the lensed images, and consistently separated a group of five objects dominated by microlensing (SBS 0909+532, FBQS J0951+2635, SDSS J1004+4112, SDSS J1206+4332, and SDSS J1339+1310) and another group of three systems in which intrinsic variability prevails (Q 0142-100, HE 1104-1805, and WFI 2033-4723). Two systems (HE 0435-1223 and HE 2149-2745) seem to be hybrid cases with both microlensing and intrinsic variability present. Finally, the case of QSO 0957+561 may be explained by intrinsic variability combined with the large time-delay between the images plus a possible contribution from microlensing.
